name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads and presents dynamic featured exhibitions, events, and special offers, and provides navigation to key sections and a global newsletter subscription form.

core_features: Load homepage overview data via getHomepageOverview and expose featuredExhibitions, featuredEvents, and specialOffers to child components.
Display a list of featured exhibitions as interactive cards with navigation to the corresponding exhibition detail page.
Display a list of featured events with navigation both to a filtered events listing (by date) and to individual event detail pages.
Display a list of special offers, including special exhibition ticket offers and shop-related promotions, with navigation to the appropriate target pages using required URL parameters.
Provide clear navigation links from the homepage to Visit, Tickets, Exhibitions, Events & Workshops, Shop, Membership, and Support sections.
Provide a reusable global newsletter signup form in the footer that calls subscribeToNewsletter with topic, interest, and format preferences and reflects success/error state.
user_workflows:
View featured exhibitions and open exhibition detail:
1) User opens Home (index.html).
2) On page initialization, homepage-data-loader calls getHomepageOverview.
3) featured-exhibitions-list receives featuredExhibitions from page state and renders an interactive list.
4) User selects an exhibition card (mouse, touch, or keyboard).
5) onCardClick builds URL exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId={featuredExhibition.id} and navigates to it using the id from the clicked card.

View featured events and either see all events on that date or a specific event detail:
1) After getHomepageOverview resolves, featured-events-list renders featuredEvents.
2) User clicks anywhere on a specific event block except the title.
3) onEventBlockClick reads the event object from featuredEvents and constructs events.html?date={derivedOrProvidedDateValueForThatEvent}; it navigates to that URL, allowing the events page to show events for that date.
4) Alternatively, user clicks directly on the event title link.
5) onEventTitleClick constructs event_detail.html?eventId={featuredEvent.id} and navigates to that URL.

Use homepage special offers to access ticket booking or shop:
1) After getHomepageOverview resolves, special-offers-list renders specialOffers.
2) User activates the primary call-to-action for an offer identified (via its offerType or linkTarget) as a special exhibition ticket offer.
3) onOfferCtaClick builds tickets.html?exhibitionType=special_exhibition and navigates to it.
4) For an offer identified as a postcards/prints shop promotion, onOfferCtaClick builds shop.html?category=postcards_prints and navigates to it.
5) For any other offers, onOfferCtaClick uses the offer's linkTarget as the base path and appends any required query parameters as defined by the site-wide routing rules.

Navigate quickly to key site sections from the homepage:
1) primary-navigation-links is rendered in the header with static links to Visit, Tickets, Exhibitions, Events & Workshops, Shop, Membership, and Support pages.
2) User clicks a navigation item.
3) Browser navigates directly to the corresponding page (e.g., tickets.html) with or without query parameters depending on that destination page’s specification.

Subscribe to the newsletter from the global footer:
1) User scrolls to the footer and sees newsletter-footer-form.
2) User enters an email address, selects at least one topic (e.g., monthly_highlights, new_exhibitions_openings, special_offers), optionally selects interests (e.g., contemporary_art, family_activities, education_programs, community_events), and chooses a preferredFormat (html_email or plain_text).
3) User submits the form; onSubmit validates fields and, if valid, calls subscribeToNewsletter(email, topics, interests, preferredFormat).
4) On success, the component updates its local success state, optionally updates the URL with newsletterStatus=success and newsletterEmail={email}, and displays a confirmation message.
5) On error, the component sets an error state (optionally reflected via newsletterStatus=error and an error code in the URL) and shows an appropriate error message without leaving the page.
interactions: Page load / initialization: When index.html is loaded, homepage-data-loader triggers onInit, which calls getHomepageOverview once and populates page-level state with featuredExhibitions, featuredEvents, and specialOffers. Loading and error states are exposed to child components.

Click on a featured exhibition card: In featured-exhibitions-list, onCardClick reads the clicked exhibition’s id field and triggers navigation to exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId={id}. The underlying data for the card comes directly from getHomepageOverview.featuredExhibitions.

Keyboard activation of a featured exhibition card: Cards are focusable elements; onKeyActivate (Enter/Space) delegates to the same handler as onCardClick and navigates to exhibition_detail.html with the exhibitionId parameter.

Click on a featured event block (non-title area): In featured-events-list, onEventBlockClick reads the selected event’s date/time information (either as part of the object or derived from it) and constructs events.html?date={dateValue}; the user is taken to the events listing filtered to that date.
Click on a featured event title: In featured-events-list, the event title is a separate interactive element; onEventTitleClick reads the event id and navigates to event_detail.html?eventId={id}.

Click on a special ticket offer CTA: In special-offers-list, when an offer is determined to represent a special exhibition ticket promotion, onOfferCtaClick constructs tickets.html?exhibitionType=special_exhibition and navigates there.

Click on a postcards/prints shop offer CTA: In special-offers-list, when an offer represents a postcards/prints promotion, onOfferCtaClick constructs shop.html?category=postcards_prints and navigates there.

Click on other special offers: In special-offers-list, if an offer is neither a ticket nor shop postcards/prints promo, onOfferClick or onOfferCtaClick navigates to the URL indicated by the offer’s linkTarget, optionally adding query parameters if required by routing rules.

Click on header navigation links: In primary-navigation-links, clicking a link navigates to the corresponding section page (e.g., visit.html, tickets.html, exhibitions.html, events.html, shop.html, membership.html, support.html) using standard anchor navigation.

Newsletter form input: In newsletter-footer-form, onInputChange updates the component’s in-memory form state for email, topics (multi-select), interests (multi-select), and preferredFormat (single-select).

Newsletter form submission: In newsletter-footer-form, onSubmit validates that email is present and at least one topic is selected; if valid, it calls subscribeToNewsletter. During the API call, the component enters a submitting state and disables the submit button.

Newsletter subscription success: When subscribeToNewsletter returns success=true, onSuccess updates internal state with the returned NewsletterSubscription object, shows a confirmation message, and may update the URL to include newsletterStatus=success and newsletterEmail={subscription.email}.

Newsletter subscription error: When subscribeToNewsletter returns success=false or the call fails, onError records an error message (for display) and may update the URL to include newsletterStatus=error and an error code parameter.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Home page does not require any URL parameters to function but can respond to optional query parameters to control state and behavior. On every load or reload of index.html, the page performs these steps:
1) Parse URL query parameters at startup before or while calling getHomepageOverview.
2) Recognized optional parameters may include:
   - section: A string indicating which section of the homepage to focus (e.g., 'exhibitions', 'events', 'offers', 'newsletter'). After getHomepageOverview resolves and components render, the page controller scrolls or focuses the requested section.
   - highlightExhibitionId: If present, after featured-exhibitions-list has rendered, the component attempts to match this id against featuredExhibitions. If a match is found, that card is visually marked as highlighted and may be scrolled into view.
   - highlightEventId: If present, after featured-events-list has rendered, the component attempts to match this id against featuredEvents and highlight the matching event block similarly.
   - newsletterStatus: Used by newsletter-footer-form to display a persistent success or error message after redirection back to Home (e.g., newsletterStatus=success or newsletterStatus=error). This lets other flows (such as a separate email confirmation step) return the user to Home and still display status.
   - newsletterEmail: When combined with newsletterStatus, newsletter-footer-form can prefill or display the relevant email address, ensuring continuity after navigation.
3) None of these parameters change the data returned by getHomepageOverview; they only affect how already-loaded data is presented (for example, which item is highlighted or which section is focused).
4) All state that depends on dynamic content (featuredExhibitions, featuredEvents, specialOffers, and newsletter subscriptions) is derived from getHomepageOverview and subscribeToNewsletter responses; no permanent client-side storage such as localStorage is used. Any state that must survive navigation is encoded in the URL via the parameters above.
5) On internal navigation initiated from this page (to exhibition_detail.html, events.html, event_detail.html, tickets.html, or shop.html), this page constructs URLs solely using query parameters defined in the navigation specification and values obtained from the API responses (e.g., ids, dates) without persisting additional state outside the URL.

components.functionality: Initializes the Home page by calling getHomepageOverview once on page load, managing loading and error states, and storing the returned featuredExhibitions, featuredEvents, and specialOffers in a central page state that is passed down to child components.

Provides top-level navigation links from Home to key site sections, including Visit, Tickets, Exhibitions, Events & Workshops, Shop, Membership, and Support. Each link is a standard anchor tag pointing to the respective page. This component does not call any interfaces and does not depend on dynamic content.

Receives featuredExhibitions from homepage-data-loader and renders them as interactive cards. Each card uses the exhibition data (id, title, subtitle, dateRangeText, admissionLabel, exhibitionType, imageUrl, linkTarget) and allows users to open the corresponding exhibition detail page. It can also apply a highlight style based on the highlightExhibitionId URL parameter.

Displays the featuredEvents collection from homepage-data-loader. Each event is presented as a block containing summary info (title, eventType, dateTimeText, priceDisplayText, imageUrl, linkTarget). Clicking the event block opens the events listing filtered by that event’s date. Clicking specifically on the event title opens the event detail page. The component can highlight a specific event when highlightEventId is provided via the URL.

Renders specialOffers returned by getHomepageOverview. For each offer (id, title, description, offerType, linkTarget), it shows a summary and a primary call-to-action. If the offer represents a special exhibition ticket promotion, activating the CTA navigates to tickets.html with exhibitionType=special_exhibition. If the offer represents a postcards/prints shop promotion, it navigates to shop.html with category=postcards_prints. For other offers, linkTarget is used to determine the navigation destination and any associated query parameters according to routing rules.

Central controller that interprets URL parameters relevant to Home (such as section, highlightExhibitionId, highlightEventId, newsletterStatus, newsletterEmail) and coordinates behavior across components. It instructs specific components to scroll into view, highlight certain items, or display persisted newsletter status messages based on the parsed query parameters.

Footer component allowing users to subscribe to the gallery’s newsletter. It manages form fields for email, topics (multi-select of topic enums), interests (optional multi-select of interest enums), and preferredFormat (enum). On submission, it validates the input and calls subscribeToNewsletter. It then processes the response to show success or error states and can update the URL to reflect the subscription status.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strictly vertical, centered stack of sections: first the global navigation/branding bar (with logo and links inside the navigation-bar component), followed by a strong dividing rule, then the main content sections in order: Featured Exhibitions, Featured Events, Special Offers, and finally the Newsletter subscription. Each content section is constrained to a centered column (about 60–70% of viewport width) with its own section heading and a vertical stack of horizontally oriented items. Exhibition, event, and offer items all follow a consistent two-column row pattern where a visual or key metadata block appears on the left and the descriptive text appears on the right.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strictly vertical, centered stack of sections: first the global navigation/branding bar (with logo and links inside the navigation-bar component), followed by a strong dividing rule, then the main content sections in order: Featured Exhibitions, Featured Events, Special Offers, and finally the Newsletter subscription. Each content section is constrained to a centered column (about 60–70% of viewport width) with its own section heading and a vertical stack of horizontally oriented items. Exhibition, event, and offer items all follow a consistent two-column row pattern where a visual or key metadata block appears on the left and the descriptive text appears on the right.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 20–25% of the initial viewport is dedicated to the navigation-bar component, with generous padding above and below the logo and primary navigation links. A horizontal rule sits below this block, clearly separating it from the main content. The main content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered, with substantial white margins on the left and right. Within that column, each section (exhibitions, events, offers, newsletter) is separated by 32–48px of vertical whitespace and, where appropriate, a subtle horizontal rule. Each row inside the exhibition/event/offer lists uses approximately 35–40% of its width for the left visual/metadata column and 60–65% for the right text column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse and airy. Each section has a succinct heading and a limited number of featured items visible at once. Individual rows (exhibitions, events, offers) show a bold title, one or two supporting lines (institution, date, or short tag), and a short paragraph, with generous line spacing and whitespace between text blocks. Line lengths are moderate (around 60–70 characters) due to the central column width. This keeps the visual density low and lends an editorial, gallery-catalog feel rather than a packed listing.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye travels from top to bottom: first to the centered logo and navigation links, then to the dividing rule, which acts as a visual stop. Below this, the first section heading ('Featured Exhibitions') becomes the initial content anchor. Within each exhibition row, the eye naturally hits the artwork image on the left first, then moves right to the bold title and details. Subsequent sections (events, offers) reuse the same left-visual/right-text rhythm, maintaining a predictable scan pattern. The page concludes with a calm, centered newsletter form, visually treated as a final call-to-action but with a light, non-aggressive presence.

layout.overall_description: The homepage reads like a minimalist gallery catalog laid out on a centered column. At the very top, a full-width but visually centered navigation-bar holds the gallery’s logo and a compact horizontal row of navigation links, surrounded by generous white space. A thin, full-width horizontal rule beneath the nav cleanly separates this global chrome from the rest of the page. Below, in the central 60–70% of the viewport width, the content unfolds in stacked bands: first the Featured Exhibitions section, then Featured Events, then Special Offers, and finally a Newsletter signup in the footer area. Each content section begins with a simple, left-aligned or centered heading inside the same column, followed by a vertical stack of rows. Those rows share a consistent two-column composition: a rectangular image or key detail block on the left occupying around 35–40% of the row, and a right-hand column of text (title, supporting meta, and short description) filling the remaining 60–65%. Generous vertical spacing between sections and between rows gives each item room to breathe, framed by large left and right margins. The visual flow is calm and unhurried: you move down through exhibitions, then parallel event and offer formats, ending on a quiet, centered newsletter form that feels like a natural closing panel rather than a disruptive pop-up. The entire layout leans on black-and-white typography, rules, and whitespace, with color introduced only through artwork and small accents, echoing a museum-like atmosphere.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates behind the scenes and does not present a visible element on the page. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the document structure, just above the navigation-bar component, but it does not claim any visual space, padding, or margins. It initializes and feeds data to the featured-exhibitions-list, featured-events-list, and special-offers-list components before they render, ensuring that these sections load in place without shifting the layout. From a visual standpoint, the page appears as a continuous static layout; the data-loader simply allows the central content column to populate seamlessly within its predefined slots.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, does not render a box or occupy layout space; it runs at the root of the page lifecycle and affects only data state, not physical placement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Like the data-loader, this controller is non-visual. It conceptually wraps the main content region that includes the featured-exhibitions-list, featured-events-list, special-offers-list, and newsletter-footer-form. It does not introduce any visible edges, background colors, or spacing; instead, it manages loading, error, and empty states. When content is loading, any placeholders or skeleton elements would appear directly inside the corresponding lists’ slots, preserving the overall central column and spacing. This ensures the visual rhythm of sections and whitespace remains consistent regardless of underlying state changes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavior that may trigger state-based variations (e.g., inline loading placeholders inside lists), but itself is not positioned or sized in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-bar spans the full width of the viewport at the very top of the page but keeps its content centered within a fixed-width or max-width container aligned to the same central column as the rest of the page. The block occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the initial viewport height. At its center, the gallery logo is placed slightly above the navigation links, with 24–32px of vertical space around it, reinforcing its status as the key brand mark. Directly below the logo, the primary navigation links are laid out in a single horizontal row, centered in the same column, with equal spacing between each link. Top and bottom padding for the entire navigation block is generous (approximately 32–48px) to create a floating, gallery-like feeling. The background remains white, and all typography is in black or very dark gray. At the bottom edge of this component, a fine horizontal rule runs the full width of the viewport, visually anchoring the nav and signaling the transition into the content sections below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document (not sticky or fixed); as the user scrolls, it moves off-screen while preserving the initial strong top-of-page presence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section is the first main content block below the navigation-bar’s horizontal rule and occupies a central column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered with large white margins on each side. A section heading such as 'Featured Exhibitions' appears at the top of this block, either centered or left-aligned within the column, with approximately 32–40px of whitespace above it (from the nav rule) and 16–24px below it before the list of exhibitions begins. The list itself is a vertical stack of exhibition rows. Each row consumes nearly the full width of the central column and follows a two-column horizontal layout: on the left, a rectangular artwork image occupies about 35–40% of the row width; on the right, a text column occupies the remaining 60–65%. In the right column, the exhibition title appears first in bold, larger type, followed by the institution name and date range as smaller, lighter lines, then a short paragraph of descriptive copy. Vertical spacing between title, meta lines, and paragraph is consistent and airy (8–16px). Each exhibition row is separated from the next by generous vertical space—approximately 32–48px—to maintain a low-density, editorial feel. The entire section has a clear top and bottom breathing zone, with at least 40–48px of whitespace before the next section (Featured Events) begins, optionally marked by another subtle horizontal rule that aligns exactly with the nav rule above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing naturally with the document; exhibition rows wrap responsively so that on narrower viewports the layout can collapse into a vertical stack (image on top, text below) while still centered within the same global column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned directly below the featured-exhibitions-list, the featured-events-list begins after a comfortable vertical gap (roughly 40–48px) and an optional thin horizontal rule that spans the central column or the full viewport, subtly separating sections. This section shares the same central column width (60–70% of the viewport) and is perfectly aligned with the exhibitions section above, reinforcing structural harmony. At the top of the block, a heading such as 'Featured Events' is placed within the column, mirroring the styling of the exhibitions heading. Beneath the heading, events are displayed in a vertical stack of rows that echo the two-column exhibition layout for consistency. In each event row, the left column (about 35–40% of row width) can hold a smaller rectangular image, date badge, or time/location block, while the right column (about 60–65%) contains the event title in bold, followed by key details (date, time, venue) and a brief description. Each row has internal padding in the 16–24px range, and there is 24–32px of vertical space between event rows. This keeps events visually related to exhibitions but slightly lighter in emphasis. The bottom of the section leaves a 40–48px gap before the special-offers-list, maintaining the calm, gallery-like pacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the main scrollable content. Rows adapt to narrower screens by letting the left column stack above the right column while preserving generous vertical spacing and central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The special-offers-list sits below the featured-events section, separated by a generous vertical margin of about 40–48px and optionally a thin horizontal rule. It occupies the same centered column of 60–70% viewport width, aligning perfectly with the content above. A heading such as 'Special Offers' appears at the top of the section, styled consistently with previous section headings. Offer items are arranged in a vertical stack, each spanning the central column width. To remain visually consistent with the exhibition pattern but slightly more compact, each offer row can either use a scaled-down two-column layout or a single-column layout with a strong left-aligned accent. In the two-column variant, the left 35–40% holds a simple graphic, badge, or high-contrast block highlighting the discount or offer type, while the right 60–65% contains a bold offer title, a short line describing eligibility or validity, and a brief explanatory sentence or two. The internal padding around each offer row is in the 16–24px range, with 24–32px of vertical separation between offers. This section feels slightly denser than exhibitions but still airy, with ample whitespace above and below it. The bottom of this block leaves a 48–64px vertical gap before the newsletter-footer-form, indicating a transition from promotional content to global engagement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked with the main document flow. On smaller screens, the content naturally collapses to a single-column layout while preserving the same vertical rhythm and central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-footer-form anchors the bottom of the page as a calm, centered call-to-action. It begins after a pronounced vertical gap (48–64px) from the special-offers-list and optionally a thin horizontal rule, signaling that this is a global footer-level element. The form sits within the same central column as the other sections but can be slightly narrower (around 50–60% of viewport width) to emphasize focus and intimacy. At the top of the form area, a short heading and one-line description (e.g., 'Stay in the loop' plus a sentence about exhibition news) are center-aligned, with 16–24px of space between them. Below, the input controls (email field and submit button) are arranged horizontally where space permits: the email field takes up roughly 70–75% of the form width on the left, and the button occupies the remaining 25–30% on the right, both aligned on a single baseline. Surrounding the inputs is generous padding (24–32px) and ample white space above and below, reinforcing the minimalist, gallery-like tone. On very narrow screens, the field and button stack vertically while remaining centered. The entire newsletter section has extra bottom padding (48–64px) to create a soft, open ending to the page rather than a hard cutoff.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, acting as the final scrollable section at the bottom of the page; responsively centers and adjusts its internal layout (horizontal → vertical) without changing its role as a footer call-to-action.

name: Visit
title: Visit
description: Page for viewing practical visit information (hours, location, directions, admission, accessibility) and for browsing/filtering guided tours, including wheelchair-accessible weekday morning tours, with navigation into detailed tour booking pages and an optional newsletter subscription.

core_features: Load and display visit information (hours, location, directions, admission summary, accessibility summary) using getVisitInfo.
Allow navigation to specific sections of the page (Hours & Location, Guided Tours, Visitor FAQ) via the section URL parameter and in-page links.
Display guided tour filter controls (day type, time of day, accessibility, language) based on getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions.
Search and list guided tour schedules according to selected filters using searchGuidedTourSchedules, with special support for weekday morning wheelchair-accessible tours.
Enable users to open a specific guided tour detail page (tour_detail.html) by clicking a tour title or details action, passing tourId via URL parameters.
Provide an optional newsletter subscription form using subscribeToNewsletter for visitors who want updates about exhibitions and guided tours.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View basic visit information
1) User navigates to visit.html from the main navigation.
2) On page initialization, the controller component calls getVisitInfo.
3) The visit information section binds hoursSummary, locationAddress, directionsText, mapEmbedUrl, admissionSummary, and accessibilitySummary to appropriate sub-sections.
4) If the URL contains section=hours_location, the page scrolls/focuses the Hours & Location area after data load.

Workflow: Jump directly to Guided Tours from another page
1) User clicks a footer shortcut link “Guided Tours” on another page.
2) Browser navigates to visit.html?section=guided_tours.
3) On load, the controller parses section=guided_tours and sets the active section state.
4) Controller triggers loading of visit info (getVisitInfo) and guided tour filters (getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions).
5) Once the guided tours block is rendered, the controller scrolls to the guided tours section and highlights it as active.

Workflow: Discover weekday morning wheelchair-accessible tours
1) User lands on visit.html (optionally via a link that may include filter URL parameters such as dayType=weekday&timeOfDay=morning&isWheelchairAccessibleRoute=true).
2) The controller loads filter options via getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions.
3) The guided tours filter component initializes its filter state from URL parameters (if present) or from unselected defaults.
4) The filter component calls searchGuidedTourSchedules with parameters derived from current filters (dayType, timeOfDay, isWheelchairAccessibleRoute, language, and optional date range).
5) The guided tours list component displays matching tour schedules, including tourTitle, dateTimeDisplayText, accessibility indicator, and remainingSpots where provided.
6) If the user clicks a quick-action such as “Weekday morning accessible tours”, the filter component updates the URL parameters (dayType, timeOfDay, isWheelchairAccessibleRoute) and re-runs searchGuidedTourSchedules.
7) The user browses the updated list focused on weekday morning wheelchair-accessible tour options.

Workflow: Navigate from a tour in the list to its detail and booking page
1) User is viewing the guided tour search results on visit.html.
2) User clicks on a tour title or a “More details” button in a particular result item.
3) The guided tours list component reads the selected schedule entry, extracts its tourId, and triggers navigation to tour_detail.html?tourId=<selectedTour.tourId>.
4) The user continues detailed booking and selection on tour_detail.html (outside the scope of visit.html).

Workflow: Adjust guided tour filters (day type, time of day, accessibility, language)
1) User opens visit.html and scrolls to the guided tours section.
2) Filter controls are populated from getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions (dayTypes, timeOfDayOptions, accessibilityOptions, languageOptions).
3) User selects one or more filter criteria (e.g., a dayType, a timeOfDay, an accessibility option, and a language).
4) On any filter change, the filter component updates the URL query parameters to reflect the new filter state and calls searchGuidedTourSchedules with the chosen values.
5) The tours list automatically refreshes to show only schedules matching the filters.
6) If the user clears filters, the filter component removes corresponding URL parameters and triggers a new search with those parameters omitted.

Workflow: Subscribe to the newsletter from the Visit page
1) User scrolls to the newsletter subscription area on visit.html.
2) User enters an email address and optionally selects one or more topics and interests (e.g., new exhibitions, community events) and a preferredFormat.
3) User submits the form; the component validates required fields (e.g., email, at least one topic, preferredFormat).
4) On successful validation, the component calls subscribeToNewsletter with the captured values.
5) If subscribeToNewsletter returns success=true, the component displays a confirmation state using the returned message and subscription details; if success=false, it displays the error message and keeps the form editable.

Workflow: Use the footer shortcuts to reach Visitor FAQ
1) User is on any other page and clicks a footer shortcut link labeled “Visitor FAQ”.
2) The link navigates to visit.html?section=faq.
3) The controller parses section=faq and flags the FAQ subsection as the active fragment.
4) After initial data loading (including getVisitInfo and optional guided tours related calls), the controller scrolls the FAQ section into view and sets focus for keyboard users.
interactions:
Interaction: Header navigation to Visit page
- Element: Main navigation item labeled “Visit”.
- Action: Click.
- Result: Browser loads visit.html without additional parameters; controller initializes default state and calls getVisitInfo and getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions.

Interaction: Section anchor links within the Visit page
- Elements: In-page links or tabs labeled for example “Hours & Location”, “Guided Tours”, “Visitor FAQ”.
- Action: Click.
- Result: URL is updated (e.g., visit.html?section=guided_tours), controller processes section parameter, updates active section state, scrolls to target section, and keeps filter/search state intact.

Interaction: Footer shortcuts from other pages
- Elements: Footer links such as “Hours & Location” (visit.html?section=hours_location), “Guided Tours” (visit.html?section=guided_tours), “Visitor FAQ” (visit.html?section=faq).
- Action: Click on external page.
- Result: Browser navigates to visit.html with the section parameter; visit.html controller uses this parameter to focus the correct section after load.

Interaction: Guided tour filter changes (select inputs / checkboxes)
- Elements: Filter controls (e.g., dropdowns or radio buttons) for dayType, timeOfDay, accessibilityOptions, languageOptions.
- Action: Change selection.
- Result: Filter component updates internal filter state and synchronizes corresponding URL parameters; then triggers searchGuidedTourSchedules with the new filter values; tours list updates in place.

Interaction: Guided tours quick preset for weekday morning wheelchair-accessible tours
- Element: A quick-filter button or link labeled appropriately within the guided tours filter area.
- Action: Click.
- Result: Filter component sets dayType to the weekday value from getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions.dayTypes, timeOfDay to the morning value from timeOfDayOptions, and the accessibility option corresponding to wheelchair-accessible routes; it writes these to the URL parameters and runs searchGuidedTourSchedules with dayType='weekday', timeOfDay='morning', isWheelchairAccessibleRoute=true (and existing language or date range filters if any).

Interaction: Guided tours search results item click
- Elements: Each search result row’s clickable tour title and/or “More details” button.
- Action: Click.
- Result: Component reads the clicked item’s tourId and uses client-side navigation to open tour_detail.html?tourId=<tourId>, leaving further booking logic to the tour detail page.

Interaction: Newsletter subscription form submission
- Elements: Email input, topic checkboxes, interest checkboxes, preferredFormat selector, Submit button.
- Action: Click Submit or press Enter within the form.
- Result: Component validates fields; if valid, calls subscribeToNewsletter; disables submit while awaiting response; on success, shows confirmation state; on failure, shows error message while keeping form values for correction.

Interaction: Map/directions interactions
- Elements: Embedded map frame (src bound to mapEmbedUrl) and potentially links for external directions services based on locationAddress.
- Action: Click on map or directions link.
- Result: For map, user interacts within the embedded provider; for directions link, browser opens the external mapping/directions service with pre-filled address derived from locationAddress (composition handled outside of API data, using generic URL building).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Visit page relies on URL parameters to control section focus and guided tour filters, avoiding localStorage.

1) Section parameter (section)
- Accepted values: 'hours_location', 'guided_tours', 'faq'.
- Source: incoming navigation from index.html, about.html, contact.html, policies.html, or internal links within visit.html.
- Behavior on load:
  • Controller reads section from the query string.
  • If not present or invalid, the controller defaults to showing the page starting at the top (no special focus).
  • If section is valid, the controller sets an internal activeSection state and, once the corresponding DOM elements are rendered, scrolls that section into view and may set ARIA attributes to reflect focus.
- Behavior on in-page navigation:
  • When the user clicks an in-page section link, the controller updates the section parameter in the URL via history.pushState or history.replaceState so the state is shareable via URL.

2) Guided tour filters in URL
- Additional query parameters reserved for filter state:
  • dayType: maps to a value from getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions.dayTypes[].value (e.g., 'weekday' or 'weekend').
  • timeOfDay: maps to a value from getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions.timeOfDayOptions[].value (e.g., 'morning', 'afternoon').
  • isWheelchairAccessibleRoute: boolean-like string ('true' or 'false') corresponding to the selected accessibilityOptions entry for wheelchair accessibility.
  • language: maps to a value from getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions.languageOptions[].value.
  • startDate, endDate: optional ISO date strings or other supported format for constraining the date range of searchGuidedTourSchedules.
- Behavior on load:
  • The controller parses these parameters and initializes the filter component's internal filter state accordingly.
  • The filter component then calls searchGuidedTourSchedules with these values; any parameter missing or empty is passed as undefined/null so it is not applied as a filter.
- Behavior on change:
  • When the user changes any filter in the guided tours filter controls, the filter component updates the URL parameters to match the current filter state (adding, updating, or removing parameters as necessary) using the History API.
  • After updating the URL parameters, the component re-invokes searchGuidedTourSchedules with the updated filter values.
  • This ensures that the current search configuration is always encoded in the URL, allowing deep-linking and browser back/forward navigation to restore filter state.

3) Newsletter submission state
- Newsletter form state (success or error) is handled within the page and is not encoded in URL parameters by default, as it is more transient and personal.
- The URL remains unchanged on successful or failed subscriptions.

4) Interaction with browser history
- Each change to section or guided tour filter parameters uses history.pushState or history.replaceState so that navigating with the Back and Forward buttons restores both the highlighted section and the filter configuration.
- On popstate events, the controller re-reads the section and filter parameters from the URL, updates activeSection and filter component state, and re-runs searchGuidedTourSchedules if needed.

components.functionality: Central controller for visit.html. Initializes the page, parses URL parameters, orchestrates calls to getVisitInfo and getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions, passes filter-related URL parameters to the guided tours components, manages section focus based on the 'section' parameter, and listens for history popstate events to restore state.
event_handlers: onInit: On page load, parse URL parameters (section, dayType, timeOfDay, isWheelchairAccessibleRoute, language, startDate, endDate), call getVisitInfo and getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions, and propagate the parsed filter state and section to child components.
onUrlChange: On browser back/forward (popstate), re-parse URL parameters to update activeSection and the guided tours filter component state, and trigger a new tours search if filters changed.
onSectionChange: When a child component (e.g., section navigation) requests a section change, update the section parameter in the URL via History API and scroll the corresponding section into view.
onGuidedTourFilterStateSync: Receives updated filter state from the guided tours filter component and ensures it is reflected in the URL parameters.

components.functionality: Displays general visit information including hours, location, directions, admission summary, and accessibility summary. Provides a structured view for quick reference and anchors for the Hours & Location section.
event_handlers: onDataLoaded: When getVisitInfo returns, bind hoursSummary to a Hours area, locationAddress and mapEmbedUrl to a Location & Map area, directionsText to a Directions area, admissionSummary to an Admission area, and accessibilitySummary to an Accessibility area.
onSectionFocusRequest: When the page-controller indicates section=hours_location, scroll this component's root into view and optionally focus the first heading or relevant element for keyboard users.

components.functionality: Provides in-page navigation between the main visit sections (Hours & Location, Guided Tours, Visitor FAQ), and keeps the URL's section parameter synchronized with the user’s selection.
event_handlers: onInit: Read the initial activeSection from the page-controller (derived from the section URL parameter) and highlight the corresponding navigation item.
onClickHoursLocation: When the user selects the Hours & Location tab/link, notify the controller to set section=hours_location and scroll to visit-info-section.
onClickGuidedTours: When the user selects the Guided Tours tab/link, notify the controller to set section=guided_tours and scroll to guided-tours-section.
onClickFaq: When the user selects the FAQ tab/link, notify the controller to set section=faq and scroll to visit-faq-section.
onActiveSectionUpdate: When the controller updates the activeSection (e.g., via URL change), adjust the highlighted navigation item accordingly.

components.functionality: Container for the guided tours filter controls and search results list. Receives initial filter state from the controller (based on URL parameters) and coordinates searching and rendering guided tour schedules.
event_handlers: onInit: Receive filter options data from the controller (loaded via getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions) and initial filter state derived from URL parameters; render child components accordingly.

onFilterChange: When the filter controls change, call searchGuidedTourSchedules with parameters (tourId if relevant, startDate, endDate, dayType, timeOfDay, isWheelchairAccessibleRoute, language) and pass the results to the results list.
onSectionFocusRequest: When the controller sets section=guided_tours, scroll this section into view.

onQuickPresetApply: When quick presets (e.g., weekday morning accessible) are activated, normalize these into filter values and trigger the same searchGuidedTourSchedules flow as manual filter changes.

components.functionality: Displays and manages guided tours filter controls for day type, time of day, accessibility, language, and optional date range. Initializes from URL parameters and updates URL parameters and search queries whenever the user changes filters. Provides a quick preset for weekday morning wheelchair-accessible tours.
event_handlers: onInit: Populate filter control options using dayTypes, timeOfDayOptions, accessibilityOptions, and languageOptions from getVisitGuidedTourFilterOptions; initialize selected values from URL parameters provided by the controller.
onChangeDayType: Update local filter state for dayType, notify the controller to update the dayType URL parameter, and emit a filter change event to guided-tours-section.
onChangeTimeOfDay: Update local filter state for timeOfDay, notify the controller to update the timeOfDay URL parameter, and emit a filter change event.

onChangeAccessibility: When a user toggles the accessibility option matching wheelchair-accessible routes (key compared from accessibilityOptions), set or clear isWheelchairAccessibleRoute in local state and URL parameters; emit filter change.
onChangeLanguage: Update local filter state for language, synchronize language URL parameter, and emit filter change.
onChangeDateRange: When startDate or endDate is changed, update local state, update startDate and endDate URL parameters, and emit filter change.

onClickQuickPresetWeekdayMorningAccessible: Set dayType to the option whose value represents weekdays, timeOfDay to the option whose value represents morning, and isWheelchairAccessibleRoute=true; update these parameters in the URL through the controller and emit filter change.

onUrlFilterStateUpdate: When the controller updates filter state due to a URL change (e.g., back/forward navigation), align internal state and input control values with the new parameters without triggering duplicate searches.

components.functionality: Displays the list of guided tour schedules returned by searchGuidedTourSchedules. Shows key information, such as tour title, dateTimeDisplayText, languagesAvailable, accessibility indicator, and availability (remainingSpots), and allows navigation to tour_detail.html using tourId.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsReceived: When guided-tours-section passes new searchGuidedTourSchedules results, render them in list form, grouped or sorted as needed without hardcoded order rules beyond what the API returns.
onClickTourTitle: When a user clicks a tour title in a result item, extract the item's tourId and navigate to tour_detail.html?tourId=<tourId>.
onClickMoreDetails: For users who click a more explicit 'More details' action, perform the same navigation behavior using the associated tourId.

onEmptyResults: If searchGuidedTourSchedules returns an empty array, display a generic 'no matching tours' state and encourage users to adjust filters (text coming from a UI copy layer, not from hardcoded data values).

components.functionality: Hosts visitor FAQ content related to planning a visit (topics such as arrival, accessibility, tickets). Actual FAQ entries are provided by the content system or CMS outside of these interfaces. This component is only responsible for anchoring and section focus.
event_handlers: onSectionFocusRequest: When the controller sets section=faq, scroll this section into view and, if needed, focus the first FAQ heading for accessibility.
onContentUpdate: When external content updates FAQ entries (e.g., via CMS render), re-render the list while preserving anchor IDs so section navigation continues to work.

components.functionality: Allows visitors to subscribe to the gallery newsletter directly from the Visit page by specifying email, topics, interests, and preferredFormat, and sends this data to subscribeToNewsletter.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize an empty form model for email, topics (array), interests (array), and preferredFormat with no static defaults beyond what the UI requires (e.g., unset until user picks).
onChangeField: Update local form state whenever the user changes email, topics, interests, or preferredFormat.

onSubmit: Validate required fields (email, at least one topic, preferredFormat); if validation fails, show inline error messages; if validation passes, call subscribeToNewsletter(email, topics, interests, preferredFormat) and temporarily disable the submit button.

onSubscriptionResponse: When subscribeToNewsletter returns, if success=true, render a confirmation state using the returned message and subscription details; if success=false, display the error message and re-enable the submit button so the user can correct and resubmit.
onReset: Allow the user to reset the form after a successful subscription if they wish to subscribe another email address (creating a new call to subscribeToNewsletter).

components.functionality: Embeds an interactive map using mapEmbedUrl from getVisitInfo and exposes the location address for use in external directions links.
event_handlers: onDataLoaded: When getVisitInfo completes, set the <iframe> or equivalent map element's src to mapEmbedUrl and display locationAddress near the map.

onClickDirectionsLink: When the user clicks an external 'Get directions' link, construct a URL to the chosen maps provider using locationAddress and open it in a new tab or window (the mapping provider itself is external to this system).

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same top-down, linear structure as the exhibitions page: a full-width but visually centered outer shell controlled by the page controller (logo, primary nav, page title 'Visit'), followed by a slim horizontal separator, then a centered secondary navigation (section tabs). Below this, major content sections (Visit Info, Map & Directions, Guided Tours, FAQ, Newsletter) are stacked vertically within a constrained central column. Within complex sections (Guided Tours, Map & Directions), employ a two-column pattern similar to the exhibition rows: visual element (map or tour meta) on the left, structured text on the right.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same top-down, linear structure as the exhibitions page: a full-width but visually centered outer shell controlled by the page controller (logo, primary nav, page title 'Visit'), followed by a slim horizontal separator, then a centered secondary navigation (section tabs). Below this, major content sections (Visit Info, Map & Directions, Guided Tours, FAQ, Newsletter) are stacked vertically within a constrained central column. Within complex sections (Guided Tours, Map & Directions), employ a two-column pattern similar to the exhibition rows: visual element (map or tour meta) on the left, structured text on the right.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top ~20–25% of the initial viewport height for logo and primary navigation, with generous top and bottom padding. Allocate another ~10–15% of the initial viewport height for the 'Visit' page title and section navigation tabs, creating a distinct break before practical information. All content below sits inside a central column occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width (max-width ~960–1040px), horizontally centered with large left/right margins serving as white framing space. Major sections (Visit Info, Map, Tours, FAQ, Newsletter) are separated by 32–48px vertical margins and occasionally a light horizontal rule.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, editorial feel. Keep text lines at a moderate width (55–75 characters) within the central column. Each section focuses on a few key elements: clear headings, concise copy, and minimal controls. Two-column rows (e.g., map and tours list items) are spacious, with 16–24px internal padding and generous breathing room between stacked items (24–32px). Avoid dense control clusters; filters and forms should feel airy with clearly separated inputs.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in a strong vertical flow: logo and primary nav at the very top, then down to the centered 'Visit' heading, then to the horizontal section-navigation tabs which act as a pivot into the content. From there, attention moves to Visit Info (hours/admission/location), then to the visually prominent map on the left with directions on the right, then to the Guided Tours section where the left-side visual/meta column of each tour draws the eye before moving right to details and booking links. FAQ and Newsletter at the bottom provide a soft finish. Section tabs can remain subtly sticky at the top of the content area on scroll, acting as a secondary vertical anchor without overpowering the primary page structure.

layout.overall_description: The Visit page sits inside a calm, gallery-like frame: a wide, white canvas with a narrow, centered content column. At the top, the black-and-white logo and main navigation span horizontally, breathing in ample vertical whitespace. A thin rule separates this global chrome from the centered 'Visit' title, which sits alone with generous space above and below. Just beneath, a slim row of minimalist section tabs ('Info', 'Map & Directions', 'Guided Tours', 'FAQ') stretches across the central column, visually tying the page sections together; as you scroll, these tabs lightly dock to the top of the content area. Below, practical visit information appears first in a single-column text block, followed by a map-and-directions row that echoes the exhibition layout: a rectangular map panel on the left and neatly stacked directions text on the right. The Guided Tours area continues the two-column motif within each tour entry, using spacious cards where left-hand tour metadata balances right-hand descriptions and booking affordances. Further down, the FAQ section reverts to a single-column accordion for clean scannability, and the page closes with a compact, centered newsletter form that is narrower than the main content column, framed by generous whitespace. Throughout, the design relies on white space, restrained typography, and subtle rules to create a quiet, editorial rhythm aligned with the original exhibitions mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines a full-width, full-height canvas with a central content column. At the very top, it anchors the gallery's logo and primary navigation in a horizontal bar occupying roughly the top 20–25% of the initial viewport height, with generous 32–48px padding above and below the logo/nav row. Below this bar, a thin horizontal rule spans the full width of the content column, centered in the viewport. Under the rule, the page title 'Visit' appears as a single, bold line of text, centered horizontally within the main column and given 32–48px of whitespace above and below. Beneath the title sits the section-navigation-tabs component. All subsequent sections (visit-info-section, map-and-directions-block, guided-tours-section, visit-faq-section, newsletter-subscription-form) are stacked vertically within the same central column, which occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width, centered with large white margins on the left and right. Vertical gaps of 32–48px separate each major section, maintaining the airy, museum-like rhythm. On large screens the column’s width is capped around 960–1040px so lines never become too long, and on smaller screens the column gracefully expands to nearly full width with margins slightly reduced but still visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the scrolling page frame; all components scroll normally within it. The page controller itself is static but contains a top band (logo and primary nav) that stays at the top of the document flow and a content column that remains centered as the viewport width changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned directly beneath the section-navigation-tabs, the visit-info-section is the first content block in the main column. It begins with a left-aligned section heading like 'Plan Your Visit' inside the central column, with 16–24px of space above the heading relative to the tabs. Below the heading, the content is arranged in a light two-column layout on desktop: the left half (about 50% of the column width) stacks 'Hours' and 'Admission' in compact sub-blocks, while the right half (the remaining 50%) stacks 'Location' and 'Accessibility'. Each sub-block has its own small heading and a few lines of text, with 16–24px vertical spacing between them. The entire section has 24px of internal top and bottom padding relative to its heading baseline, but visually it feels open because of the 32px gap before the next section (map-and-directions-block). On narrow screens, this two-column layout collapses into a single column: Hours, Admission, Location, Accessibility appear one after another, maintaining 16–24px gaps. The section spans the full width of the main content column but remains visually light, with no heavy backgrounds—just text and perhaps very subtle dividers if needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally as part of the main content. On wider screens it behaves responsively, switching between two-column and single-column layouts based on viewport width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-navigation-tabs component sits directly beneath the centered 'Visit' page title and above the visit-info-section. It is a low-profile horizontal bar, approximately 48px tall, aligned within the central content column and spanning its full width (about 60–70% of viewport width). The tab labels (e.g., 'Info', 'Map & Directions', 'Guided Tours', 'FAQ') are arranged in a single row, evenly spaced or left-aligned with equal 24–32px gaps, using minimalist text styling. The tabs are vertically centered within the bar, with 8–16px of internal padding above and below the tab text. There is 16–24px of whitespace between the bottom of the 'Visit' heading and the top of the tabs, and another 24–32px between the bottom of the tabs and the top of the visit-info-section. A thin rule may appear directly under the tab row to echo the separators seen in the mockup, reinforcing the division between navigational chrome and content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially appears in normal flow under the page title, but once the user scrolls past the title, the tabs become sticky within the main content column, pinning themselves just below the global header area. They remain horizontally centered and maintain their width, providing quick access to sections without feeling heavy or fixed to the absolute top edge of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guided-tours-section appears below the map-and-directions-block, separated by approximately 48px of vertical space. Within the main content column, it starts with a left-aligned section heading such as 'Guided Tours' that sits flush with the column’s left edge. Beneath the heading, a short line of descriptive copy (one or two lines max) spans no more than 70% of the column width, reinforcing the low-density editorial feel. Below this intro, the guided-tours-filter-controls component forms a distinct horizontal band; the guided-tours-results-list is stacked immediately under the filters, with about 24–32px of separation. The entire tours section uses generous vertical spacing between tour cards (similar to the spacing between exhibition entries) so you can visually distinguish each available tour. From top to bottom, the section reads as: heading, brief description, filters, then a vertical sequence of two-column tour entries, all confined to the central column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls as a unified content section. Internally, it adapts responsively: filter layout and tour-card two-column layout condense into single-column stacks on smaller screens while preserving vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Nested at the top of the guided-tours-section, the guided-tours-filter-controls form a slim, airy control strip that spans the full width of the main content column. It sits 16–24px below the tours heading and intro text. On desktop, key filters (e.g., date, time of day, accessibility toggle) are arranged left to right in a single row, with each control occupying roughly equal width within the strip and separated by 16–24px horizontal gaps. The controls have 12–16px internal padding, and the entire filter area is framed by 16–24px of vertical padding, so the strip feels like a light horizontal band rather than a dense form. The rightmost side of the strip may remain mostly white space to preserve the minimalist aesthetic. On narrower screens, the filters stack in one or two rows, but retain the same generous spacing. There is approximately 24–32px of whitespace between the bottom of this filter strip and the top of the guided-tours-results-list, letting the filters read as a clear control area before the list begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the guided-tours-section. It does not fix or float independently but may be quickly reachable via the section tabs. Layout shifts from single-row to stacked depending on available width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guided-tours-results-list occupies the remainder of the guided-tours-section beneath the filter controls, spanning the full width of the content column. Each tour result is rendered as a horizontally oriented block that echoes the exhibition row pattern from the mockup: a left-side column and a right-side column within a single card. The left column occupies roughly 35–40% of the card’s width and may contain a thumbnail image, date/time badge, or key tour metadata, while the right column takes the remaining 60–65% for tour title, short description, accessibility indicators (e.g., 'Wheelchair accessible'), and a subtle booking link. Each card has 16–24px of internal padding and is separated from the next card by a vertical gap of about 24–32px, ensuring a calm, low-density feel. The list itself may extend beyond the initial viewport height, encouraging vertical scrolling, but because the cards are centered within the main column and consistently spaced, the eye naturally flows from one left-side visual block to the right-side text, then down to the next tour. On small screens, each card collapses to a single column with the visual/meta content stacking above the text content, while preserving generous vertical padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally as part of the page content. The list can grow in length; its cards maintain a consistent two-column pattern on larger screens and transition to stacked layouts responsively.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit-faq-section is placed beneath the guided-tours-section with a spacious 48–64px vertical gap to clearly separate it as a new informational area. It spans the full width of the central content column, adhering to the same max-width constraint. A left-aligned heading like 'Frequently Asked Questions' sits at the top of this section. Below the heading, FAQ items are arranged in a single vertical column, each occupying the full column width. Each FAQ item is presented as an accordion row: the question appears in a bold line with ample 12–16px padding above and below; when expanded, the answer text appears directly underneath with 16–24px of internal padding and a maximum line length similar to other body copy. 16–24px vertical spacing separates one FAQ item from the next, and a light divider line may optionally be used between items to mirror the subtle rules used elsewhere. Compared to the more structured, two-column sections above, this area feels text-forward and linear, aiding quick scanning and reducing cognitive load.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally as a standalone content section. FAQ items expand and collapse in place; no fixed or floating behavior is used here to keep the experience simple and calm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-subscription-form sits near the bottom of the page, directly beneath the visit-faq-section with a pronounced vertical gap of about 48–64px that frames it as an optional, low-pressure call-to-action. Unlike other full-width sections, this form is slightly narrower than the main content column: it is centered within the column and constrained to about 50–60% of the column width, creating a compact, jewel-box effect surrounded by white space on both sides. At the top of the form, a short heading like 'Stay Informed' is center-aligned, with a single supporting sentence below it. Beneath the text, the email input and submit button are arranged side by side in a single row on desktop, with the input taking approximately 70–75% of the form width and the button 25–30%. The row has 12–16px of vertical padding and generous horizontal padding inside the field and button. On smaller screens, the input and button stack vertically with 12–16px spacing. The entire form block has 24–32px padding and is visually anchored by the surrounding whitespace, providing a calm ending to the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, appearing last in the scrolling flow. The form reflows responsively between horizontal and vertical arrangements of its fields based on available width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The map-and-directions-block is placed immediately after the visit-info-section, with a 32–48px vertical gap separating the two. It echoes the exhibition-row visual pattern: a horizontally oriented block inside the main content column, with a visual element on the left and text on the right. The left side of the block is a rectangular interactive map panel that occupies roughly 40–45% of the block’s width and maintains a wide aspect ratio (e.g., 16:9) to feel like a framed artwork. The right side takes up the remaining 55–60% and stacks content vertically: a short heading like 'Map & Directions', followed by address, transit options, parking info, and any directional notes. The text column has clear segmentation—each subtopic separated by 8–16px vertical gaps—so content is easy to skim. The entire block includes 16–24px of internal padding and is visually bounded by whitespace above and below, but without a heavy border or background. On smaller screens, the map-and-directions-block collapses into a stacked layout: the full-width map appears on top, and the directions text spans nearly the full width underneath, with 16–24px of space between them. This preserves the emphasis on the visual map first, then the supporting textual guidance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as a single block. The map area itself may be interactive within its frame, but the block’s position remains fixed in the document flow, transitioning from side-by-side to stacked layout responsively.

name: Guided Tour Detail
title: Guided Tour Detail
description: Shows full details and available schedules for a guided tour, lets visitors choose a date/time, party size, and language, and then books the tour and sends it to checkout.

core_features: Load guided tour information using the tourId URL parameter via getGuidedTourDetail and display the tour title, description, duration, accessibility information, pricing type, and default language.
Display a list of upcoming tour schedules returned in upcomingSchedules, including dateTimeDisplayText, timeOfDay, languagesAvailable, and remainingSpots.
Preselect a schedule if tourScheduleId is provided in the URL; otherwise select a default available schedule (for example, first in upcomingSchedules).
Allow users to change the selected schedule, including selecting weekday morning slots when available, with validation against remainingSpots.
Allow users to specify the numberOfVisitors, constrained by the selected schedule's remainingSpots and any maxVisitors information, enabling bookings such as a 5‑visitor weekday morning tour.
Allow users to choose the tour language from the selected schedule's languagesAvailable, defaulting to the tour's defaultLanguage where compatible.

Provide a primary booking control that calls bookGuidedTourAndCreateOrder with the selected tourScheduleId, numberOfVisitors, and language, handles success/error responses, and redirects to checkout.html with checkoutOrderId on success.
Expose wheelchair accessibility status using isWheelchairAccessibleRoute and accessibilityDetails so users can confirm that the chosen tour route is wheelchair-accessible before booking.
Offer an optional inline newsletter subscription form that calls subscribeToNewsletter with user-selected topics, interests, and preferredFormat.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View a guided tour from Visit or Index page
1. User arrives on tour_detail.html with a tourId URL parameter (and optionally tourScheduleId).
2. On page load, the page reads tourId (required) and tourScheduleId (optional) from the URL.
3. The page calls getGuidedTourDetail(tourId).
4. While loading, a loading state is shown; if the call fails or returns no tour, an error state is displayed.
5. When data is returned, the tour summary section shows basic info and accessibilityDetails; the schedule selector lists upcomingSchedules.
6. If tourScheduleId is present and matches one of upcomingSchedules, that schedule is selected; otherwise, the first available schedule is selected according to page rules.
7. The visitor count and language selector are initialized based on defaults and the selected schedule (e.g., visitor count within remainingSpots, language compatible with languagesAvailable).

Workflow: Book a weekday morning wheelchair-accessible tour for 5 visitors and go to checkout
1. User lands on tour_detail.html for a specific guided tour with tourId in the URL. The tour returned by getGuidedTourDetail isWheelchairAccessibleRoute is true, confirming wheelchair accessibility.
2. The upcomingSchedules list includes one or more weekday schedules with timeOfDay marked as a morning value.
3. The user selects a desired weekday morning schedule from the schedule selector; the page sets the currently selected tourScheduleId in its state (and may update the URL to include this value).
4. The user sets numberOfVisitors to 5 in the visitor selector. The page validates that 5 is less than or equal to the selected schedule's remainingSpots and any maxVisitors constraints. If invalid, a validation error is shown instead of proceeding.
5. The user chooses a language from the schedule's languagesAvailable list (for example, the language matching the tour's defaultLanguage where available).
6. User clicks the primary booking button (e.g., Book Tour / Proceed to Checkout).
7. The page validates that a schedule is selected, numberOfVisitors is valid, and a language is chosen.
8. The page calls bookGuidedTourAndCreateOrder with parameters: selected tourScheduleId, numberOfVisitors = 5, and the selected language.
9. If the call returns success = true, the page retrieves checkoutOrder.id from the response and navigates to checkout.html?checkoutOrderId=<checkoutOrder.id>.
10. If success = false, the page displays message from the response and may prompt the user to adjust schedule or party size (for example, if availability changed).

Workflow: Adjust schedule, visitors, or language before booking
1. After initial load, the user reviews the current schedule selection in the schedule selector.
2. The user clicks on a different date/time entry in the upcomingSchedules list; the selected schedule state is updated and the visitor count maximum is recalculated based on remainingSpots.
3. If the previously selected numberOfVisitors exceeds the new schedule's remainingSpots, the visitor count is automatically reduced to the maximum allowed and a notice is displayed.
4. The language options update to match the new schedule's languagesAvailable; if the previously selected language is not available, the page prompts the user to choose another language.
5. The user adjusts the numberOfVisitors using the visitor selector controls and updates the language as desired.
6. Once satisfied, the user clicks the booking button to trigger bookGuidedTourAndCreateOrder and proceed to checkout on success.

Workflow: Subscribe to the gallery newsletter from the tour detail page
1. The user scrolls to the newsletter subscription component.
2. The user enters an email address and optionally selects topics, interests, and preferredFormat from the available options.
3. On form submission, the page validates that an email is present and at least one required topic is selected according to the interface requirements.
4. The page calls subscribeToNewsletter with the entered email, chosen topics, interests, and preferredFormat.
5. If success is true, the page shows a confirmation message and may clear or disable the form.
6. If success is false, the page displays the returned message and keeps the user input so they can correct it.
interactions: On initial load, read tourId and optional tourScheduleId from the URL. If tourId is missing, show an error state; otherwise, invoke getGuidedTourDetail(tourId).
Display a loading indicator while getGuidedTourDetail is in progress; hide it and show either content or an error message when the call completes.

In the schedule selector, each schedule item is clickable; clicking an item sets the selected tourScheduleId in the page state (and may update the URL query string) and updates dependent components (visitor max limits, language options).

In the visitor selector, users interact with a numeric input or increment/decrement control. Changes update numberOfVisitors in the page state and trigger validation against remainingSpots for the selected schedule.
In the language selector, users choose from a list derived from the selected schedule's languagesAvailable. Selection updates the language value in state.

The primary booking button triggers client-side validation of selected schedule, numberOfVisitors, and language. If validation passes, it calls bookGuidedTourAndCreateOrder; the button reflects a loading state until the response is received.

On successful bookGuidedTourAndCreateOrder response, the page performs a client-side redirect to checkout.html with checkoutOrderId in the query string. On failure, an error notification is shown using the message field from the response.
If getGuidedTourDetail returns no upcomingSchedules, the schedule selector shows a 'no available schedules' state and the booking button is disabled.
The newsletter subscription form validates required fields on submit and then calls subscribeToNewsletter; based on success or failure, a confirmation or error message is shown.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary URL parameter controlling this page is tourId, which is required. On every page load or URL change, the page reads tourId from the query string (tour_detail.html?tourId=<id>). If tourId is absent or empty, the page does not call getGuidedTourDetail and instead displays a missing-parameter error. If tourId is provided, the page calls getGuidedTourDetail(tourId) and uses the response to populate the tour overview and schedules.

An optional URL parameter tourScheduleId may also be present (tour_detail.html?tourId=<id>&tourScheduleId=<scheduleId>). After getGuidedTourDetail resolves, the page compares tourScheduleId to the tourScheduleId values in upcomingSchedules. If a match is found, that schedule is set as the selected schedule. If there is no match or no tourScheduleId, the page applies its own selection rule (such as choosing the first schedule in upcomingSchedules) and may update the URL to reflect the selected scheduleId for consistency.

The page may also maintain additional transient state (such as numberOfVisitors and language) and can optionally synchronize it with the URL using additional query parameters (for example, visitors and language) whenever the user updates the form. If used, these additional parameters are read on load to prepopulate the visitor and language selectors, and are updated when the user changes those values. However, the only required parameters to render functional content are tourId and, optionally, tourScheduleId.

Whenever tourId or tourScheduleId changes in the URL (for example, via user navigation or internal link updates), the page state is recalculated: when tourId changes, getGuidedTourDetail is called again; when tourScheduleId changes, the selected schedule is changed if it exists in upcomingSchedules. Booking actions always use the current in-memory state (selected schedule, numberOfVisitors, language) to call bookGuidedTourAndCreateOrder, and after a successful booking, the page navigates away to checkout.html with checkoutOrderId populated from the API response.

components.functionality: Reads tourId (and optional tourScheduleId) from the URL, calls getGuidedTourDetail with tourId, and manages loading, success, and error states for the page. On success, it passes the tour, accessibilityDetails, defaultLanguageLabel, and upcomingSchedules data to child components along with the preferred initial selected schedule.

Displays core information about the guided tour, including the title, description, defaultLanguageLabel, durationMinutes, admissionType, and wheelchair accessibility, using isWheelchairAccessibleRoute and accessibilityDetails. It does not perform API calls itself but reflects data loaded by tour-detail-loader.

Shows a list of upcomingSchedules for the tour, including dateTimeDisplayText, timeOfDay, languagesAvailable, and remainingSpots for each schedule. Allows users to select one schedule (including weekday morning options when present). The current selection is stored in page state and can be initialized using the tourScheduleId URL parameter or a default rule.

Provides controls to select numberOfVisitors and language for the currently selected schedule. The visitor count is constrained by the selected schedule's remainingSpots and any maxVisitors; the language options are taken from the selected schedule's languagesAvailable array. It preselects a language that matches the tour's defaultLanguage where available and ensures the current language is always valid for the selected schedule.

Hosts the primary booking button and related feedback (validation messages, availability warnings). When the user clicks the booking button, it validates that a schedule is selected, numberOfVisitors is within allowed limits, and a language is chosen. If validation succeeds, it calls bookGuidedTourAndCreateOrder with the selected tourScheduleId, numberOfVisitors, and language. On a successful response, it redirects to checkout.html?checkoutOrderId=<checkoutOrder.id>. On failure, it displays the provided message and can optionally prompt the user to refresh schedule selection or visitor count.

Highlights accessibility information specific to the tour using accessibilityDetails and isWheelchairAccessibleRoute from the loaded tour data. It is read-only and ensures users can confirm wheelchair accessibility before booking.

Collects an email address, topics, interests, and preferredFormat from the user and submits them via subscribeToNewsletter. It handles basic client-side validation (for example, required email and required topics) and renders success or error states based on the API response.

Centralizes display of non-blocking messages such as API errors, booking validation errors, and newsletter subscription feedback. It listens for events from other components (e.g., booking-action-bar, tour-detail-loader, newsletter-inline-form) and shows the latest messages to the user.

Synchronizes certain page state values with URL parameters. It reads tourId and tourScheduleId on initial load and URL changes, and may optionally write back the current tourScheduleId, numberOfVisitors, and language to the URL query string when they change.
