This allows deep-linking to a specific schedule and prefilled booking settings without using localStorage.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global logo/nav and the centered page title ('Guided Tour' / tour name), the main page content forms a centered two-column band, mirroring the exhibition rows from the mockup: the left column holds the visual/story context of the tour, while the right column holds all booking interactions. Supporting information (accessibility, notifications, newsletter) is arranged vertically around this main band, with the newsletter returning to a single centered column at the bottom, echoing the editorial structure of the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global logo/nav and the centered page title ('Guided Tour' / tour name), the main page content forms a centered two-column band, mirroring the exhibition rows from the mockup: the left column holds the visual/story context of the tour, while the right column holds all booking interactions. Supporting information (accessibility, notifications, newsletter) is arranged vertically around this main band, with the newsletter returning to a single centered column at the bottom, echoing the editorial structure of the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained to a central column spanning roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with large left/right margins acting as white framing space. Within this central area, the two-column band allocates about 35–40% of the width to the left (tour summary and accessibility info) and 60–65% to the right (schedule, form, booking bar). Vertical spacing between major sections (title to content, content to newsletter) follows a 32–48px rhythm, with internal component padding around 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Each block is visually light with short to moderate line lengths and clear separation between components. Interactive controls are grouped into concise, well-spaced clusters. No element feels crowded; each panel has breathing room on all sides, relying on whitespace, thin rules, and typography to structure the page, maintaining a quiet, museum-like density.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves straight down from the centered page title into the main two-column band. Colorful tour imagery in the left column anchors initial attention, then the eye naturally shifts right to the schedule selector, visitor and language form, and booking action bar. Feedback messages sit just above the main interactive stack, ensuring status is seen early. Below the main band, the flow returns to a centered single-column newsletter form, providing a calm closing. Loader and URL state sync act invisibly in the background; the loader temporarily sits above the content and becomes the primary focus while data is loading.

layout.overall_description: Within the familiar gallery-like shell of the site, the Guided Tour detail page presents its content in a restrained, editorial layout. After the global logo and navigation, a single centered page title introduces the tour, surrounded by generous whitespace above and below. A thin horizontal rule subtly marks the transition into the booking area. The main body is a wide yet clearly framed two-column strip centered in the viewport: on the left, the tour summary panel—anchored by a prominent artwork or tour image—runs down the page with supporting text and accessibility details stacked beneath, like an exhibition label beside an artwork. On the right, aligned to the same top baseline, is a taller column of booking controls: feedback messages at the top, followed by the schedule selector, then the visitor and language form, with a horizontally oriented booking action bar near the bottom that remains visually tied to this column. The overall width of this band is contained, with expansive white margins on both sides. A second horizontal rule or clear vertical gap separates this band from the closing newsletter sign-up, which returns to a single centered column that is slightly narrower, reinforcing a sense of completion. Throughout, black-and-white styling, restrained typography, and consistent vertical spacing provide a calm, gallery-like experience, while the interactive elements are clearly grouped and easy to scan. The loader, when active, overlays this entire central region with a light veil and centered spinner or message, temporarily becoming the focal point without breaking the underlying structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a semi-opaque white overlay that covers the entire central content column, starting just below the global navigation and page title and extending down to just above the footer newsletter area. It spans the same 60–70% width as the main content column and is horizontally centered, preserving the underlying frame of white margins on both sides. In the middle of this overlay, a small, minimalist loading indicator and short status text are vertically and horizontally centered, with at least 64px of whitespace around them. When active, the loader visually floats above all other components in the stacking order, slightly dimming or softening the content beneath while keeping the page's grid and margins intact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily fixed over the main content region (but not the global header), positioned relative to the viewport so it remains centered even when the user scrolls. It is full-height over the scrollable content area and blocks interaction with underlying components while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tour summary panel is positioned at the top of the main two-column band, aligned with the left column. The entire two-column band is centered horizontally on the page and spans roughly 65% of the viewport width, leaving wide white margins on both sides. Within that band, the tour summary panel occupies about 35–40% of the width on the left. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the schedule selector in the right column, creating a strong shared baseline. Inside the panel, a rectangular tour image sits at the top, taking the full width of this left column. Below the image, the tour title, gallery name, and a brief description are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing between each text block, and 16–24px padding on all sides of the panel area. The panel’s height is determined by its content, and there is a 24–32px vertical gap beneath it before the accessibility info panel continues down in the same left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the left column; it does not scroll independently or stick. On narrower screens, it shifts to become the first block in a single-column vertical stack, spanning close to 90–100% of the available width while maintaining generous side padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tour schedule selector forms the top block of the right column in the main two-column band. This right column occupies about 60–65% of the total band width and is aligned so that its left edge is separated from the tour summary panel by a 32–48px central gutter. The schedule selector is positioned immediately below any feedback messages but still near the top of this right column, ensuring it is visible within the initial viewport. It spans the full internal width of the right column with 16–24px padding on all sides. Inside, date and time options are arranged in a clean vertical stack: a bold section label at the top, date picker or date chips in one or two concise rows, then time slots beneath with comfortable 8–16px spacing. The bottom of this block leaves a 24–32px gap before the visitor-and-language form begins, creating clear separation between choosing a time and entering visitor details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, scrolling with the page. On narrow screens, it appears directly below the tour summary panel in a single-column stack, still centered with generous outer margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visitor-and-language form is positioned directly under the tour schedule selector within the right column, sharing the same width and left alignment. A vertical gap of approximately 24–32px separates it from the schedule selector, maintaining the airy, segmented feel. The form itself is a vertically stacked block spanning the full right column width, with uniform 16–24px internal padding and clear field groupings: a heading or label at the top, followed by fields for party size, visitor contact details, and language preferences, each separated by 16–24px vertical spacing. Any inline field groups (such as adults/children counts) are arranged horizontally but do not exceed about 70% of the column width, leaving breathing room. The bottom of the form leaves a 24–32px gap before reaching the booking-action-bar, giving the action bar a distinct base position within this column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical flow of the right column. On narrower screens, it follows the schedule selector in a single-column layout, maintaining centered alignment within the page’s main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking action bar sits at the bottom of the right column’s content stack, directly beneath the visitor-and-language form with a 24–32px vertical gap. It spans the full width of the right column while staying within the central 65% page band, preserving the large white margins on left and right. The bar is a low-profile horizontal strip with 16–24px padding, visually grounded by a thin top border or subtle tonal contrast. Inside, key information (such as total price and selected date/time summary) is aligned to the left half, while the primary 'Book Tour' button is aligned to the right edge, forming a clean, linear structure. The bar’s height remains relatively slim, keeping the minimalist, editorial character. On tall screens with ample space, the action bar appears just above the fold of the main content; as the user scrolls, it remains close to the bottom of the viewport, anchored visually to the right column rather than stretching full-bleed across the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky element within the right column: when the user scrolls past it, it pins to the bottom of the viewport but remains horizontally constrained to the right column’s width and central alignment. On small screens, it can either remain sticky at the bottom of the viewport or sit as a fixed-width block directly below the form, still centered and not touching the screen edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The accessibility info panel continues the left column beneath the tour summary panel. It is vertically aligned so that its left edge and width match the tour summary, forming a clean single column of contextual information. A 24–32px vertical gap separates it from the bottom of the tour summary. The panel itself uses a minimalist card treatment with 16–24px padding and a clear heading at the top (e.g., 'Accessibility & Practical Info'), followed by short paragraphs or bullet points with 8–16px vertical spacing. The overall height is modest, aiming for a compact, scannable block that terminates roughly mid-way down the right column’s content stack, allowing the right column’s interactive elements to remain visually dominant while the left column still feels balanced. A thin top rule or subtle title styling can distinguish this panel without adding heavy decoration.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the left column. On narrower screens, it appears beneath the tour summary and above the schedule selector in the single-column flow, maintaining a 24–32px gap above and below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter inline form sits below the entire two-column band, acting as a closing section for the page. A pronounced vertical gap of about 48px separates it from the bottom edges of both left and right column content, reinforcing a clear break from the booking content. It returns to the page’s single-column pattern: the form is horizontally centered and slightly narrower than the main content band, spanning roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop, with ample white space on both sides. A concise heading and one-line description are stacked at the top, with a 16px gap, followed by a horizontally arranged email field and submit button that collectively occupy most of this narrow column without touching the edges. The entire newsletter block has 24–32px internal padding, and its top may be separated from the main content by a subtle horizontal rule, in keeping with the mockup’s use of rules to mark section transitions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width section in the page flow but visually constrained to a centered narrow column. On smaller screens, the form expands to about 90% width with consistent side padding, with the email field and button stacking if necessary to preserve legibility.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Feedback messages appear in the right column, directly above the tour schedule selector and below any page-level heading or introductory text for the booking area. They span the full width of the right column, maintaining alignment with the schedule selector and form below. Each message is contained within a thin, minimalist banner with 12–16px padding and clear typographic emphasis (e.g., bold for success, subtle outline for warnings), but without heavy blocks of color, in order to fit the gallery aesthetic. Messages are stacked vertically with 8–12px gaps if multiple are present, and the entire feedback area leaves a 16–24px gap below itself before the schedule selector begins. This positioning ensures users see important feedback before adjusting their schedule or form entries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, expanding vertically as needed. On narrow screens, feedback messages appear above the schedule selector in the single-column stack, centered and matching the main content width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component operates entirely behind the scenes with no visible UI. Conceptually, it is aligned with the main content area, updating the URL to reflect selected schedule, party size, and language without altering the visual layout. It introduces no additional spacing, rules, or visual elements on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, non-spatial background logic. It does not affect layout flow or positioning of any visible components and simply listens to and updates state in response to user interactions.

name: Exhibitions
title: Exhibitions
description: Browse and filter current and upcoming exhibitions, sort results, add exhibitions to a visit plan, and create a free family-friendly visit plan of up to three exhibitions within the next seven days.

core_features: Initialize exhibitions filters and sort options using getExhibitionsFilterOptions and any incoming URL parameters (startDate, endDate, admissionTypes, isFamilyFriendly, sortBy).

Display a list of exhibitions returned by searchExhibitions, showing key information such as title, date range text, admission label, family-friendliness, and price indicator text, with links to exhibition_detail.html.

Allow users to adjust filters (date range within at most the next seven days, admission types including free admission, and family-friendly toggle) and re-run searchExhibitions with updated parameters.

Allow users to sort exhibitions based on sortOptions from getExhibitionsFilterOptions, including a sort that orders by earliest start date first, by calling searchExhibitions with the selected sortBy.
Provide per-exhibition actions to add an exhibition to the user’s visit plan via addExhibitionToVisitPlan and reflect visit-plan membership using the isInVisitPlan flag from searchExhibitions.
Provide per-exhibition removal controls for items added during the current page session by calling removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan with the stored visitPlanItemId.

Support building a free, family-friendly visit plan limited to three exhibitions within the next seven days by applying appropriate filters (free admission, family-friendly, constrained date range) and enforcing a client-side limit on how many matching exhibitions can be added.

Show an inline visit plan summary (total items in the plan as returned by addExhibitionToVisitPlan / removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan) and provide navigation to visit_plan.html once at least one exhibition is in the plan.

Integrate a newsletter subscription form that calls subscribeToNewsletter with the user’s email, selected topics, interests, and preferred format so visitors can receive updates about exhibitions and family activities.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Basic browse and filter
1. User lands on exhibitions.html via main navigation or a direct link.
2. Page controller reads URL parameters (startDate, endDate, admissionTypes, isFamilyFriendly, sortBy), if present.
3. Page controller calls getExhibitionsFilterOptions to retrieve default date range, admissionTypes, audienceSuitabilityOptions, and sortOptions.
4. Page controller determines the effective filter state:
   - If a URL parameter is present, it overrides the corresponding default from getExhibitionsFilterOptions.
   - If a URL parameter is missing, the default from getExhibitionsFilterOptions is used.
5. Page controller calls searchExhibitions with the effective startDate, endDate, admissionTypes, isFamilyFriendly, and sortBy.
6. Exhibitions list renders the returned array from searchExhibitions, showing each exhibition card with its title, subtitle (if any), dateRangeText, admissionLabel, priceIndicatorText, family-friendly indicator, and image if available.
7. User adjusts filters in the filter bar (e.g., changes dates, toggles admission types, toggles family-friendly):
   - Filter bar notifies page controller of new filter values.
   - Page controller updates the URL query string to reflect the new state.
   - Page controller calls searchExhibitions again with the updated parameters.
8. User adjusts sort order via the sort control:
   - Sort control notifies page controller of the selected sortBy value.
   - Page controller updates the sortBy parameter in the URL.
   - Page controller calls searchExhibitions with the same filters and new sortBy.
9. User clicks an exhibition title or image on a card; browser navigates to exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId={exhibitionId} using the exhibitionId from the selected result item.

Workflow 2: Create a 3-exhibition free family visit plan within the next 7 days
1. User opens exhibitions.html, either directly or from index.html/visit_plan.html with incoming parameters that may already pre-set a date range (e.g., today to seven days from today), admissionTypes (e.g., including free), and isFamilyFriendly.
2. Page controller initializes filters via getExhibitionsFilterOptions and incoming URL parameters, then runs searchExhibitions.
3. User activates a dedicated "Free family visit plan" preset control in the filter bar:
   - Filter bar sets isFamilyFriendly to true.
   - Filter bar ensures the admissionTypes selection includes the option whose value corresponds to free admission.
   - Filter bar constrains the date range so that the endDate is not later than seven days after the chosen startDate (using either incoming parameters or defaults from getExhibitionsFilterOptions).
   - Filter bar notifies page controller of the updated filters.
4. Page controller updates the URL query string with the new filter values (startDate, endDate, admissionTypes, isFamilyFriendly) and re-calls searchExhibitions.
5. Exhibitions list shows only exhibitions that match the free, family-friendly, within-next-seven-days criteria based on the searchExhibitions response.
6. Page controller calculates the current count of matching exhibitions that are already in the visit plan by counting result items where isFamilyFriendly is true, admissionType matches the free admission value, the exhibition’s startDate/endDate lie within the active date range, and isInVisitPlan is true.
7. User clicks "Add to Visit Plan" on an exhibition card:
   - If the current count of matching plan items is less than three, the card triggers addExhibitionToVisitPlan with that exhibition’s exhibitionId.
   - After a successful response, the page controller:
     • Updates local state: marks that exhibition as isInVisitPlan = true and stores visitPlanItemId for that exhibition.
     • Updates the visit-plan summary’s totalItems using the totalItems value from the response.
     • Recomputes the count of matching free family exhibitions in the plan.
   - If the count has reached three, the controller disables additional "Add to Visit Plan" actions for other exhibitions that match the criteria and shows a constraint message in the visit-plan summary.
8. If the user wants to change one of the chosen exhibitions within this page session:
   - The user clicks a "Remove from Visit Plan" action on a card that the user added in this session.
   - The card uses the stored visitPlanItemId for that exhibition to call removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan.
   - On success, the controller updates local state: sets isInVisitPlan = false for that exhibition, updates totalItems from the response, and decrements the computed count of free, family-friendly exhibitions.
   - If the count drops below three, "Add to Visit Plan" actions for other eligible exhibitions become enabled again.
9. Once the user is satisfied with up to three selected exhibitions, the user clicks a "My Visit Plan" button in the visit-plan summary.
10. Browser navigates to visit_plan.html (no additional parameters required), where the user can see the full visit plan and manage it further.

Workflow 3: Manage an existing visit plan from the listing
1. User arrives on exhibitions.html with some exhibitions already stored in a visit plan from previous sessions or other pages.
2. Page controller initializes filters and calls searchExhibitions.
3. Exhibitions list renders each exhibition card along with an "already in plan" indicator based on isInVisitPlan from searchExhibitions.
4. For exhibitions that the user adds to the visit plan during this page session, the page controller records visitPlanItemId values returned from addExhibitionToVisitPlan, allowing those specific items to be removable directly from this page via removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan.
5. For any exhibitions that were in the visit plan before this session (isInVisitPlan is true but there is no stored visitPlanItemId in current page state), cards show them as already saved but do not show inline removal; the user is guided to visit_plan.html to remove those items if desired.
6. The visit-plan summary always shows the current totalItems returned by the most recent addExhibitionToVisitPlan or removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan call, so the user knows how many exhibitions are in their overall plan.
7. User clicks the "My Visit Plan" button in the summary to navigate to visit_plan.html and manage the plan in detail.

Workflow 4: Subscribe to exhibition updates via newsletter
1. User scrolls to the newsletter signup component on exhibitions.html.
2. User enters an email address, selects at least one topic (e.g., a topic relating to new exhibitions), optionally selects interests (e.g., interests relating to family activities or education programs), and chooses a preferredFormat (HTML or plain text).
3. User submits the form.
4. Newsletter component calls subscribeToNewsletter with the collected email, topics, interests, and preferredFormat.
5. On success, the component displays a confirmation message based on the message field in the response and may clear the form or disable resubmission.
6. On failure, the component shows an error state and allows the user to adjust the form and resubmit.
interactions: On page load (onInit), the page controller reads URL query parameters (startDate, endDate, admissionTypes, isFamilyFriendly, sortBy), calls getExhibitionsFilterOptions, merges URL values with defaults, and calls searchExhibitions.

When the user changes date filters in the filter bar (onDateChange), the filter component emits new startDate and endDate values, the controller updates the URL, and calls searchExhibitions with updated dates while keeping the other filters unchanged.
When the user toggles admission type filters (onAdmissionTypesChange), the controller updates admissionTypes in URL and state, then re-calls searchExhibitions.
When the user toggles family-friendly-only (onFamilyFriendlyToggle), the controller updates isFamilyFriendly in URL and state, then re-calls searchExhibitions.
When the user chooses a new sort option (onSortChange), the controller updates sortBy in URL and calls searchExhibitions with the same filters and new sortBy.

When the user activates the "Free family visit plan" preset (onFamilyPlanPresetClick), the filter bar sets filters for free admission, family-friendly, and a constrained date range (within the next seven days), then triggers a filter change event that causes a searchExhibitions call.
When the user clicks an exhibition title or image (onCardPrimaryClick), the browser navigates to exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId={exhibitionId} using the selected item’s exhibitionId.

When the user clicks "Add to Visit Plan" on a card (onClickAddToVisitPlan), the card asks the controller to add the exhibition. The controller checks the current count of free, family-friendly exhibitions within the active date range (when in family plan mode). If under three, it calls addExhibitionToVisitPlan; on success it updates the relevant card’s isInVisitPlan state, stores visitPlanItemId, updates totalItems in the visit-plan summary, and potentially disables further additions once the limit is reached.

When the user clicks "Remove from Visit Plan" on a card added in this session (onClickRemoveFromVisitPlan), the card provides its stored visitPlanItemId to the controller, which calls removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan. On success, the controller updates card state and the visit-plan summary and, if in family plan mode, re-enables additional additions if the count falls below three.
When the user clicks the "My Visit Plan" button in the visit-plan summary (onClickGoToVisitPlan), the page navigates to visit_plan.html without additional parameters.

When the user submits the newsletter form (onSubmitNewsletter), the newsletter component validates that an email and at least one topic are selected, then calls subscribeToNewsletter. It displays success or error feedback based on the API response.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the primary source of truth for filter and sort state. On initial load, the controller parses startDate, endDate, admissionTypes, isFamilyFriendly, and sortBy from the URL. It then calls getExhibitionsFilterOptions to obtain default values and merges them: for each filter key, if the corresponding URL parameter exists, it is used; otherwise the default from getExhibitionsFilterOptions is applied. The resulting effective state is passed into searchExhibitions. Whenever the user changes any filter or sort option through the UI, the controller updates the in-memory state and rewrites the URL query string (e.g., using history.pushState or history.replaceState) so that the URL always reflects the current startDate, endDate, admissionTypes (as a comma-separated list), isFamilyFriendly (as a boolean string), and sortBy. A browser reload or a visit from another page with pre-populated parameters will therefore reproduce the same filter/sort state. The specialized "Free family visit plan" behavior is represented as a combination of filter values rather than a separate flag: activating it forces isFamilyFriendly to true, ensures admissionTypes includes the free admission value, and constrains the endDate to not exceed seven days after startDate. The controller recomputes the count of matching free family exhibitions in the visit plan based solely on the current searchExhibitions result set and its isInVisitPlan flags, combined with local knowledge of which newly added items have corresponding visitPlanItemId values from addExhibitionToVisitPlan. This derived count is transient and internal to the page; it is not stored in URL parameters. The visit-plan summary’s totalItems value is driven by the latest addExhibitionToVisitPlan or removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan response. Newsletter subscription state is not encoded in the URL; it is shared only within the newsletter component and reset on page reload.

components.functionality: Coordinates initialization, filter state, sorting, API calls, and overall page behavior. Reads URL parameters, calls getExhibitionsFilterOptions, merges defaults with URL values, calls searchExhibitions, manages in-memory visit-plan metadata (including per-exhibition visitPlanItemIds for items added during this session), and tracks the computed count of free, family-friendly exhibitions within the active date range for enforcing the three-exhibition limit.
event_handlers: onInit (reads URL parameters, calls getExhibitionsFilterOptions, computes initial filters, and calls searchExhibitions)
onFiltersChange (receives new startDate, endDate, admissionTypes, isFamilyFriendly from filter bar; updates URL and calls searchExhibitions)
onSortChange (receives new sortBy from sort control; updates URL and calls searchExhibitions)
onFamilyPlanPresetClick (applies free admission, family-friendly, and next-seven-days date constraints; triggers onFiltersChange)
onSearchResultsLoaded (stores the array returned by searchExhibitions and recomputes the count of matching free family exhibitions that are already in the plan)

onAddToVisitPlan (checks current free family count when applicable; if allowed, calls addExhibitionToVisitPlan and updates exhibition card state, visitPlanItemId mapping, totalItems, and free family count)
onRemoveFromVisitPlan (calls removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan for a known visitPlanItemId, updates exhibition card state, totalItems, and free family count)
onNavigateToDetail (navigates the browser to exhibition_detail.html with exhibitionId from the selected result item)
onUrlChange (optional handler if the URL query is changed externally, re-parses parameters and re-runs searchExhibitions)

components.functionality: Provides controls for refining which exhibitions are displayed. Includes a date range selector constrained to relevant periods, a multi-select or checkbox group for admissionTypes, a toggle for family-friendly-only, and a dedicated action to quickly apply the free, family-friendly, next-seven-days preset for building a family visit plan.
event_handlers: onMount (receives initial filter values and options from the page controller derived from getExhibitionsFilterOptions and URL parameters)
onDateChange (emits updated startDate and endDate to the page controller)
onAdmissionTypesChange (emits updated admissionTypes selection to the page controller)
onFamilyFriendlyToggle (emits updated isFamilyFriendly boolean to the page controller)
onFamilyPlanPresetClick (sets internal controls to free admission, family-friendly, and next-seven-days date range, then emits a consolidated filter change event to the controller)

components.functionality: Displays available sortOptions (as provided by getExhibitionsFilterOptions) and lets the user choose how exhibitions are ordered, such as by start date ascending. When the sort option changes, it updates the controller’s sortBy state and triggers a refreshed searchExhibitions call.
event_handlers: onMount (receives initial sortOptions and currently active sortBy from the page controller)
onSortChange (emits selected sortBy to the page controller for URL update and searchExhibitions invocation)

components.functionality: Renders the list or grid of exhibitions returned by searchExhibitions. For each item, it passes exhibition data to child exhibition-card components, including flags like isInVisitPlan and any locally stored visitPlanItemId for items added in this session. Manages loading and empty states using controller-provided status.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate (receives the latest array of exhibition results from the page controller after searchExhibitions completes)
onCardAddToVisitPlan (forwarded from a child card to the page controller with the selected exhibitionId)
onCardRemoveFromVisitPlan (forwarded from a child card to the page controller with the associated visitPlanItemId, when available)
onCardNavigateToDetail (forwarded from a child card to the page controller with exhibitionId)

components.functionality: Represents a single exhibition in the list. Shows title, optional subtitle, dateRangeText, admissionLabel, priceIndicatorText, family-friendly indicator, image, and an "Add to Visit Plan" or "In visit plan" state depending on isInVisitPlan and whether the card was added during this session. Provides primary navigation to the detail page and includes actions to add or remove the exhibition from the visit plan where possible.
event_handlers: onClickTitleOrImage (emits a navigate-to-detail event with this card’s exhibitionId)
onClickAddToVisitPlan (emits an add-to-plan event with exhibitionId; the parent list/controller will call addExhibitionToVisitPlan and update isInVisitPlan and visitPlanItemId for this card)

onClickRemoveFromVisitPlan (for cards added in this session that have an associated visitPlanItemId, emits a remove-from-plan event with visitPlanItemId; the parent/controller calls removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan and updates the card state)
onStateUpdate (receives updates from the controller when isInVisitPlan or visitPlanItemId for this exhibition changes, and re-renders the appropriate button/indicator state)

components.functionality: Displays a compact overview of the user’s visit plan while browsing exhibitions, including the total number of items and, when the free family plan preset is active, the count of free family exhibitions selected within the current date range. Enforces the three-exhibition limit by surfacing messages and reflecting when the limit has been reached.
event_handlers: onPlanStateChange (receives updated totalItems from the page controller whenever addExhibitionToVisitPlan or removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan completes)
onFamilyPlanCountChange (receives the current count of free, family-friendly exhibitions in the plan for the active date range to display progress toward the three-exhibition limit)
onClickGoToVisitPlan (navigates to visit_plan.html when clicked, via the page controller’s navigation handler)

components.functionality: Encapsulates logic for the free family visit plan constraints. Given the current filter state and searchExhibitions results, it computes how many exhibitions in the current result set both match the free, family-friendly, within-next-seven-days criteria and are already in the visit plan (using isInVisitPlan). It also determines whether additional exhibitions can be added under the three-exhibition limit, and exposes this as a simple boolean flag and count to the page controller and visit-plan summary.
event_handlers: onFiltersOrResultsChange (recalculates the count of matching plan items and whether the add limit is reached whenever filters or searchExhibitions results change)
onPlanMembershipChange (updates counts when addExhibitionToVisitPlan or removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan modifies which exhibitions are in the plan)

components.functionality: Collects an email address, newsletter topics, optional interests, and preferredFormat, then calls subscribeToNewsletter to create or update a newsletter subscription. Displays success or error messages based on the response.
event_handlers: onInit (optionally pre-fills topic or interest checkboxes relevant to exhibitions or family activities as configured by the page)
onFieldChange (updates local form state when the user changes email, topic selections, interest selections, or preferredFormat)
onSubmitNewsletter (validates the form, calls subscribeToNewsletter with email, topics, interests, and preferredFormat, and then shows feedback based on the success flag and message returned)

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom hierarchy mirroring the mockup: global branding (logo, nav, rules) at the very top, then this Exhibitions page begins with a centered page title and short explanatory copy, followed immediately by a horizontal control band (filters on the left, sort on the right, visit-plan status subtly attached to the right side). Below this band, the main column is a vertical stack of exhibition cards, each laid out as a horizontal row with image on the left and text on the right. A newsletter signup sits as a final centered block at the bottom, visually separated from the exhibition list.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom hierarchy mirroring the mockup: global branding (logo, nav, rules) at the very top, then this Exhibitions page begins with a centered page title and short explanatory copy, followed immediately by a horizontal control band (filters on the left, sort on the right, visit-plan status subtly attached to the right side). Below this band, the main column is a vertical stack of exhibition cards, each laid out as a horizontal row with image on the left and text on the right. A newsletter signup sits as a final centered block at the bottom, visually separated from the exhibition list.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The Exhibitions page content is contained within a centered max-width column occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, framed by generous left and right margins of white space. Within that column, the top 15–20% of the scroll area is used for the page title, short description, and control band (filters/sort/plan summary). The bulk of vertical space is given to the exhibitions list, with each exhibition card occupying a comfortable height and wide horizontal span. The visit-plan summary occupies a narrow vertical strip anchored to the right edge of the main content column near the top of the list, and is allowed to be sticky within that column on desktop. The newsletter form occupies a shallow band at the very bottom, centered and somewhat narrower (about 50–60% of the content width).
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps content density low and breathable: controls are not tightly packed, each exhibition card has substantial padding and generous vertical spacing between cards, and text lines are kept to moderate lengths. The filter and sort controls form a single clean band rather than a dense control panel. The visit-plan summary uses concise text and compact indicators, avoiding clutter. The newsletter form is limited to one brief line of copy and one short input/button row, with plenty of space around it.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow is strongly vertical and centered. The eye moves from the global header down to the centered 'Exhibitions' heading and description, then into the horizontal band of controls. From there, attention is drawn to the first exhibition card: first to the left-hand artwork image (color focal point), then across to the right-hand text block for title, venue, and dates. As the user scrolls, the right-anchored visit-plan summary forms a subtle secondary vertical attractor that remains in peripheral vision without overpowering the cards. Finally, the flow resolves at the bottom with the centered newsletter sign-up, acting as a quiet closing element.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global black-and-white header, the Exhibitions page begins with a centered page title and a single line of explanatory text, both surrounded by generous vertical whitespace and separated from the header by a thin horizontal rule. The main content sits within a wide but clearly framed central column, leaving broad white margins on both sides. Directly beneath the introductory text, a clean horizontal control band spans the width of this column: filters align to the left, the sort control aligns to the right, and a compact visit-plan summary sits attached to the right edge, forming a subtle vertical element that can stay visible as the user scrolls. Below this band, the page resolves into a calm, repeating rhythm of exhibition rows: each row is a horizontally oriented card where a rectangular thumbnail of the artwork on the left takes roughly one-third of the card’s width, and the right two-thirds are a vertical stack of title, institution, dates, and a short description. Cards are separated by generous vertical gaps, maintaining a gallery-like, editorial feel. At the very bottom, after the last exhibition card and a comfortable breathing space, a minimalist newsletter signup form appears as a centered, narrower block, providing a soft visual endpoint to the page without disrupting the monochrome, museum-like aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibitions-page-controller acts as the main content wrapper for this page, sitting directly below the global header (logo, nav, and separating rule). It establishes a centered content column with a max-width of approximately 1040–1200px, leaving equal white margins on the left and right that account for about 15–20% of the viewport width on large screens. Inside this wrapper, the page content is arranged vertically: first a centered page title and one short line of descriptive text, then a thin horizontal rule, then the filter/sort/visit-plan control band, followed by the vertical stack of exhibition cards, and finally the newsletter signup at the bottom. Vertical spacing between these internal sections follows the 32–48px rhythm, with slightly larger spacing above the first major block (title) and below the last block (newsletter) to preserve a quiet, gallery-like atmosphere. On smaller screens, the wrapper maintains centered alignment but allows inner components like the filters and exhibition cards to stack vertically while still respecting generous padding on all sides (16–24px).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard scrollable vertical container; it is not fixed or sticky. It defines the max-width, horizontal centering, and section spacing rules for all nested components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibitions-filter-bar is positioned at the top of the main content controls area, directly beneath the page title/description block and its separating rule, and aligned to the left side within the centered content column. It spans roughly 70–80% of the content column width, starting flush with the left inner edge and leaving a small clear area on the far right where the sort control and visit-plan summary visually anchor. The filter controls are arranged in a single horizontal row where possible (e.g., dropdowns for date, category, and tags), with each control spaced using 16–24px gaps to avoid visual density. If there are too many filters to fit comfortably, they wrap onto a second row while keeping left alignment. The bar itself has a subtle vertical padding of about 16px above and below the controls to create a clean band, and there is a 24–32px gap separating it from the exhibition list below. On smaller screens, the filter bar becomes a full-width block within the content area, with filter controls stacking vertically and left-aligned, each separated by 8–16px of vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; it scrolls naturally with the page. It acts as a flexible horizontal layout that can wrap controls to additional rows if horizontal space is limited.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibitions-sort-control is visually tied to the filter bar but aligned on the right side of the same horizontal band. On desktop, it occupies a compact area at the far right of the content column, horizontally aligned with the primary filter controls. The sort label and its dropdown or segmented control are arranged in a short horizontal row, taking up no more than 20–25% of the content column width. There is a clear separation (16–24px) between the rightmost filter element and the sort control to keep the band airy. Vertically, the sort control aligns with the centerline of the filter controls, giving the entire band a balanced, editorial feel with left-weighted filters and a right-weighted sort selector. On smaller screens, when the filter bar stacks, the sort control drops below the filters, still right-aligned within the same content width but with 16–24px vertical spacing separating it from the last filter element; if the screen is very narrow, it can center-align to maintain visual harmony.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static placement within the control band; it does not float independently but aligns within the same horizontal row as filters when space allows, and stacks neatly below them when space is constrained.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibitions-list occupies the entire width of the centered content column directly below the control band, separated from it by approximately 32px of vertical whitespace. Within this area, exhibition cards are stacked in a vertical sequence, each card spanning nearly the full width of the content column. Between each card there is a consistent vertical gap of about 32–48px, ensuring a calm scroll rhythm. The list’s left edge aligns exactly with the filter bar’s left edge, creating a strong vertical alignment line, while the right edge aligns with the sort control’s right edge, maintaining visually consistent margins. The list extends downward as far as necessary and serves as the main scrollable content; the peripheral visit-plan summary hovers visually near its right edge. On smaller screens, the list still behaves as a simple vertical stack, but each card adapts to a single-column layout, expanding to the full available width minus standard side padding (16–24px).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard vertical list, forming the core of the scrollable page. It is not sticky; it simply grows in height as more exhibition-card instances are rendered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each exhibition-card is a horizontally oriented row within the exhibitions-list, designed to mirror the mockup’s artwork/text pattern. The card spans roughly 90–100% of the content column width with internal padding of 16–24px on all sides to create a contained, airy block (this padding can be invisible or very subtly hinted at via spacing rather than borders to preserve minimalism). Inside the card, the layout is a two-column structure: on the left, the artwork image occupies approximately 35–40% of the card’s width and has a fixed aspect ratio (e.g., 4:3 or 3:2), aligning neatly with the top of the text column. On the right, occupying the remaining 60–65%, is a vertical stack of text: the exhibition title at the top in a bold, slightly larger type; directly beneath, the institution name in a secondary weight; then the date range; and finally a short description in normal body text. Line lengths are kept moderate, so the text column does not feel overly wide. Vertical spacing within the text column follows the 8–16px increments: tight between related labels (institution and dates), and slightly larger above the description. The entire card maintains a clear visual separation from its neighbors via a 32–48px gap above and below. On small screens, the exhibition-card stacks: the image moves to the top and spans 100% width of the card, with the text stack below it, still left-aligned and with the same internal spacing, while maintaining generous vertical spacing between cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the list as a block-level row. On larger viewports, it behaves as a flexible two-column layout; on smaller viewports, it switches to a single-column stacked layout while preserving internal spacing and hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit-plan-inline-summary is placed within the same horizontal band as the filters and sort control but visually attached to the far right side of the content column, slightly offset as a slim vertical panel. On desktop, it appears just to the right or directly beneath the sort control, forming a narrow vertical strip that is about 20–25% of the content column width and aligned to the right edge. The summary may display a concise title (e.g., 'Your Visit Plan'), a count of selected exhibitions (e.g., '2 of 3 selected'), and the valid date window (e.g., 'Within next 7 days'), stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing and ample padding (16–20px) around the content. It has a subtle visual distinction (e.g., a fine keyline or a slightly shaded background) to separate it from the white page while still fitting the minimalist aesthetic. As the user scrolls down through the exhibitions-list, this summary remains visible: it is positioned so that its top edge aligns with the top of the first exhibition card, and it can become sticky within the viewport, staying pinned near the upper-right of the content column as the list scrolls beneath it. On smaller screens, the summary drops below the filters and sort controls as a full-width block with centered or left-aligned text, losing the sticky behavior if necessary to avoid obscuring content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, the component is sticky within the main content column: it remains fixed relative to the viewport once scrolled into place, hugging the top-right area of the column while the exhibition cards scroll underneath. On smaller screens, it behaves as a normal block element in the vertical flow without stickiness.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The family-plan-constraint-controller has no prominent, dedicated visual block; instead, its presence is expressed through subtle inline messaging and states associated with the visit plan and exhibition cards. When the user approaches or exceeds constraints (more than three exhibitions or dates beyond seven days), a single line of concise helper or error text appears immediately below the visit-plan-inline-summary within the same right-aligned panel, left-aligned to that panel’s content. This text uses a slightly smaller font and a muted but clearly noticeable color (e.g., a soft red or dark gray) and occupies the full width of the summary panel, with 8–12px vertical spacing separating it from the plan summary content. Additionally, constraint-related states on exhibition cards (e.g., disabled 'Add to Plan' buttons) are reflected inline on the right side of the card’s text column, aligned with the dates or action row, without disrupting the left/right image-text structure. All such messages are brief and respect the minimalist, editorial spacing: no more than one line where possible, and always surrounded by at least 8px of whitespace so they do not clutter the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual as a primary block; it manifests as inline state changes and short text messages anchored directly under the visit-plan summary or adjacent to relevant interactive elements on cards. It scrolls with its parent components and does not introduce additional fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-signup-form is positioned at the very bottom of the Exhibitions page, directly after the final exhibition-card in the exhibitions-list, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to clearly signal a new, closing section. It is centered within the same main content column but slightly narrower than the exhibition cards, occupying approximately 50–60% of the column width to create a more intimate feel. The layout is vertically stacked: a short, single-line heading or prompt centered at the top, followed by a brief one-line description if needed, and then a horizontal row containing the email input and a submit button. The input and button row spans roughly 70–80% of the form’s width and is centered, with 8–16px spacing between input and button. Internal vertical spacing between heading, description, and input row follows the 8–16px increments, leaving substantial whitespace above and below the entire block to avoid competing with the main exhibition content. On smaller screens, the form still sits last in the vertical flow, but the input and button can stack vertically while remaining center-aligned, with consistent spacing between elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and centered at the bottom of the page’s main content. It does not stick or float; it appears only after the user has scrolled through the exhibitions, acting as a natural endpoint to the page.

name: Exhibition Detail
title: Exhibition Detail
description: Page for viewing detailed information about a single exhibition, managing its inclusion in the user’s Visit Plan, and accessing ticket booking and newsletter subscription.

core_features: Load and display detailed exhibition information using the getExhibitionDetail interface based on the exhibitionId URL parameter.
Show admission and scheduling information, including admissionLabel, dateRangeText, and basic suitability indicators such as family-friendly status.
Allow users to add the exhibition to their Visit Plan using addExhibitionToVisitPlan and remove it using removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan when applicable.
Provide navigation back to the exhibitions listing and forward to the tickets page for exhibitions that support ticket booking.
Offer an inline newsletter subscription form using subscribeToNewsletter, with configurable topics, interests, and email format.
user_workflows: View exhibition details: User lands on exhibition_detail.html with an exhibitionId URL parameter; the page calls getExhibitionDetail; once data is returned, the exhibition’s information, schedule, admission, and suitability are displayed.

Add exhibition to Visit Plan: User clicks the control to add the exhibition to their Visit Plan; the page calls addExhibitionToVisitPlan with the current exhibitionId; on success, the interface updates state to reflect that the exhibition is in the Visit Plan and may reveal a link to visit_plan.html.

Remove exhibition from Visit Plan: If the exhibition is currently in the Visit Plan and a corresponding VisitPlanItem can be identified, the user clicks the control to remove it; the page calls removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan with the relevant visitPlanItemId; on success, the Visit Plan state and UI toggle back to the non-saved state.

Navigate to ticket booking: For exhibitions that use ticket booking (for example, those with a compatible admissionType and relatedTicketedSlotsCount greater than zero), the user clicks the Book Tickets action; the page navigates to tickets.html with exhibitionId and exhibitionType passed via URL parameters.
Return to exhibitions listing: User clicks the Back to Exhibitions action; the page navigates to exhibitions.html without additional parameters.
Open Visit Plan: After successfully adding an exhibition to the Visit Plan, the user can click View My Visit Plan; the page navigates to visit_plan.html for broader Visit Plan management.

Subscribe to newsletter: User fills in email, selects topics and optionally interests and preferredFormat, then submits the form; the page calls subscribeToNewsletter; on success, the user receives confirmation and the form state updates accordingly.
interactions: On page load, parse the exhibitionId from the URL query string and trigger getExhibitionDetail; handle loading and error states based on the response.
On successful getExhibitionDetail response, bind exhibition, admissionLabel, dateRangeText, isInVisitPlan, heroImageUrl, and relatedTicketedSlotsCount to the relevant components.

On click of the Add to Visit Plan control, trigger addExhibitionToVisitPlan with the current exhibitionId; handle success by updating the Visit Plan toggle state, storing visitPlanItemId from the response when available, and optionally exposing the View My Visit Plan navigation link.

On click of the Remove from Visit Plan control (when a visitPlanItemId is available), trigger removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan; handle success by updating the Visit Plan toggle state and clearing any stored visitPlanItemId related to this exhibition.
On click of the Book Tickets action (when enabled by exhibition/admission context), navigate to tickets.html with exhibitionId and exhibitionType as URL parameters.
On click of Back to Exhibitions, navigate to exhibitions.html.
On click of View My Visit Plan, navigate to visit_plan.html.

On submit of the newsletter subscription form, call subscribeToNewsletter with the user-entered email, selected topics, selected interests, and preferredFormat; show success or error feedback based on the response.
page_functionality.state_logic: The main page state is driven by the exhibitionId URL parameter and the results of the assigned interfaces. On initial load, the page reads exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId=... from the query string. If exhibitionId is missing or empty, the page enters an error state and does not call getExhibitionDetail. If exhibitionId is present, the page calls getExhibitionDetail(exhibitionId) and shows a loading state until the response arrives or fails. When getExhibitionDetail succeeds, the exhibition object, admissionLabel, dateRangeText, isInVisitPlan, heroImageUrl, and relatedTicketedSlotsCount are stored in in-memory page state only (no localStorage) and bound to components. The isInVisitPlan value determines the initial state of the Visit Plan toggle. When a user adds the exhibition to their Visit Plan, the page calls addExhibitionToVisitPlan(exhibitionId); on success, the response (including visitPlanItemId and totalItems) updates the internal Visit Plan state for this exhibition and sets isInVisitPlan to true. When removing, the page uses a stored visitPlanItemId (gathered from a prior addExhibitionToVisitPlan call or from broader application context) to call removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan; on success, it sets isInVisitPlan to false and clears the visitPlanItemId from page state. Navigation actions modify only the browser URL: Back to Exhibitions navigates to exhibitions.html; Book Tickets navigates to tickets.html?exhibitionId=<exhibition.id>&exhibitionType=<exhibition.exhibitionType>; View My Visit Plan navigates to visit_plan.html. Newsletter subscription does not alter URL parameters; it only updates transient component state with success or error details from subscribeToNewsletter. All state transitions occur in memory and are reinitialized on each fresh page load based solely on the current URL and interface responses.

components.functionality: Initializes the Exhibition Detail page by reading the exhibitionId from the URL, triggering the getExhibitionDetail interface call, and managing loading and error states.
Displays primary exhibition information including title, subtitle, description, dateRangeText, admissionLabel, isActive status, isFamilyFriendly suitability, and location when available.
Shows the main exhibition image using heroImageUrl or exhibition.imageUrl and updates dynamically when the loaded exhibition data changes.

Summarizes admission information and schedule for the exhibition, rendering admissionLabel and dateRangeText, and optionally highlighting whether the exhibition is currently active based on startDate and endDate.

Displays suitability indicators derived from exhibition.isFamilyFriendly and exhibition.exhibitionType, such as whether the exhibition is appropriate for families or linked to family-focused programming.

Allows the user to add or remove the exhibition from their Visit Plan. It shows an Add state when isInVisitPlan is false and a Remove state when isInVisitPlan is true, using addExhibitionToVisitPlan and removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan to synchronize with the backend.
Provides navigation to the Visit Plan overview page (visit_plan.html), typically shown or emphasized after a successful addExhibitionToVisitPlan operation.

Determines whether ticket booking is applicable based on exhibition.admissionType, exhibition.isActive, and relatedTicketedSlotsCount, and when applicable renders a Book Tickets action that navigates to tickets.html with the exhibitionId and exhibitionType URL parameters.
Enables the user to navigate back to the exhibitions listing page (exhibitions.html) from the Exhibition Detail page.

Collects an email address, selected topics, optional interests, and preferredFormat from the user and calls subscribeToNewsletter to register for gallery newsletters; displays feedback based on success or failure.

Shows generic status messages for the page such as loading, error while fetching exhibition details, and feedback from Visit Plan and newsletter actions. Messages are derived only from interface responses and not hardcoded data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global chrome (logo/nav/title, assumed outside this spec) at the top, then a clearly separated main content area. Within the main content, the exhibition detail is arranged as a two-column block: the artwork image on the left and an information/controls column on the right. Beneath this main row, supporting content such as the newsletter form and navigation links (back, visit plan summary) continue in a centered single-column stack. Status messages sit near the top of the content flow so they are seen immediately after the page loads or an action completes.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global chrome (logo/nav/title, assumed outside this spec) at the top, then a clearly separated main content area. Within the main content, the exhibition detail is arranged as a two-column block: the artwork image on the left and an information/controls column on the right. Beneath this main row, supporting content such as the newsletter form and navigation links (back, visit plan summary) continue in a centered single-column stack. Status messages sit near the top of the content flow so they are seen immediately after the page loads or an action completes.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained to a centered max-width column (about 60–70% of viewport width), with wide left/right margins echoing the gallery-like framing. The top 20–25% of the viewport remains visually dedicated to branding/nav (assumed global). The exhibition’s primary two-column detail row occupies the next major band of the page, with the image taking roughly 35–40% of the row width and the right-hand info/controls taking 60–65%. Below, full-width (within the central column) sections like the newsletter block maintain generous vertical spacing (32–48px) between them. Internal padding within panels stays in the 16–24px range.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains deliberately sparse and readable: a single featured exhibition per page, a limited number of key actions (ticketing, visit plan), and short paragraphs of copy. Text lines are kept to a moderate width within the right column to avoid long line lengths. Generous whitespace separates all components, and the number of visible elements per screen is kept low to preserve a calm, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from top to bottom: first through the global logo/navigation, then down to the exhibition title area introduced by the status messages (if present). From there it moves into the main two-column band, starting with the artwork image on the left as the visual anchor, then across to the exhibition title, institution, dates, and suitability badge on the right. Below this, attention naturally drops to the admission/schedule summary and the main actions (ticketing and visit-plan toggle). Secondary links and the visit plan summary sit slightly below or to the side of the primary actions. Finally, the eye is guided down to the newsletter subscription form near the bottom, with the back-to-exhibitions link providing a simple exit path within the central column.

layout.overall_description: Within a wide but centered page frame, the exhibition detail page presents a single exhibition as if it were a featured work in a gallery catalogue. After the global logo and navigation, a quiet band of whitespace introduces the main content column, where any status messages appear as a slim horizontal strip at the very top of the content area. Directly beneath, the main exhibition detail occupies a broad two-column row: the artwork image, large and prominent, anchors the left 35–40% of this row, while a stacked composition of title, institution, dates, suitability indicator, admission/schedule summary, and key actions occupies the right 60–65%. The right column is structured vertically, with the bold title near the top, key facts and badges just beneath, and then a visually strong ticketing call-to-action alongside the visit plan toggle and related links. Below this primary band, the layout relaxes back into a single centered column: a generous whitespace break leads into a minimalist newsletter signup block, followed by the back-to-exhibitions link placed cleanly at the bottom of the content. Throughout, thick margins, consistent vertical spacing, and restrained typography preserve the clean, modern, editorial quality of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component defines the central content frame for the exhibition detail. It establishes a max-width container centered horizontally on the page, occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width with symmetrical white margins on the left and right. Vertically, it begins just below the global header/nav area, leaving a generous gap of approximately 48px before any exhibition-specific content appears. All subsequent components (status messages, image, info panel, actions, newsletter, navigation links) are positioned inside this central column, aligned to its left edge but visually centered within the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static, full-height wrapper for all exhibition detail content, controlling max-width and horizontal centering but scrolling normally with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibition info panel occupies the upper portion of the right-hand column in the main two-column band. Horizontally, this right column spans about 60–65% of the central content width and sits directly to the right of the exhibition image, with a consistent 24–32px gap between image and text to maintain breathability. Within this column, the panel is aligned to the top of the image so that the exhibition title starts roughly level with the top edge of the artwork. The panel’s content (title, institution name, date range, and descriptive copy) is stacked vertically with generous 8–16px spacing between lines and 16–24px internal padding on all sides, but without heavy borders, maintaining a clean, typographic block. The description text runs to a moderate line length, stopping well before the full width of the right column to keep reading comfortable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right column of the main content row, flowing naturally with the image viewer to its left and allowing its height to expand based on content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibition image display is the dominant visual element in the main content area and is placed on the left side of the primary two-column row. It starts near the top of the exhibition detail content, aligned vertically with the top of the info panel on the right. Horizontally, it occupies about 35–40% of the central content width and sits flush to the left edge of the main container, leaving wide white margins between it and the viewport edges. The image maintains a consistent aspect ratio (a horizontal rectangle) and is large enough to feel immersive but not full-bleed, with 16–24px of whitespace above and below separating it from adjacent sections. There is a clear 24–32px gutter between the right edge of the image and the start of the right-hand info column, keeping the two columns distinct yet visually related.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, responsive block within the main content flow; it scales proportionally within its allocated left column but scrolls normally with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The admission and schedule summary is placed within the right-hand column directly below the primary exhibition info (title, institution, dates, description). It reads as a compact information strip or sub-panel that spans most of the right column width, with 16–24px of vertical spacing above and below to separate it from body copy and action controls. Inside this block, key details (ticket price, opening hours, specific dates) are arranged in a short vertical list or a minimal two-column mini-grid, but overall kept visually light—no heavy borders, just subtle typographic emphasis. Its left edge aligns with the left edge of the exhibition info text, maintaining a clean column. The height of this block is modest, occupying roughly 10–15% of the right column height in the initial viewport, ensuring it’s visible at a glance without overwhelming the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block nested in the right column, expanding vertically as needed while preserving the vertical flow from description to actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The suitability indicator appears as a compact badge or label in the upper section of the right-hand column, closely associated with the core exhibition info. It is positioned just below the primary meta information (e.g., institution name and date range) and just above or beside the start of the description text, with about 8–12px of spacing around it. The badge aligns with the left edge of the main text column, occupying only a small fraction of the width (roughly 15–20%), and is visually subtle—outlined or lightly filled, consistent with the minimalist aesthetic. Its size is small enough not to compete with the title but prominent enough to be noticed early when scanning the right column from top to bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline/block element within the exhibition info panel, moving naturally with the text and not fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit plan toggle is positioned in the right-hand column as a key interactive control, located just below the admission and schedule block. It is aligned to the left edge of the text column and sits within a small vertical band dedicated to actions, with approximately 16px of space above it (separating it from the schedule) and 8–16px below (separating it from the visit plan summary link). The control itself (e.g., button or switch with label) spans about 40–50% of the right column width, making it clearly tappable/clickable but not as visually dominant as the main ticketing CTA. Its alignment creates a vertical rhythm of information then actions within the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control in the main flow; it does not float or stick but remains in place relative to the surrounding info and actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit plan summary link appears immediately below the visit plan toggle within the right-hand column’s action area. It is a text-based link, smaller and lighter than primary buttons, aligned either directly under the toggle’s left edge or slightly indented to indicate its supporting role. It occupies a narrow horizontal strip, perhaps 20–30% of the right column width, and is separated from the toggle by about 8px of vertical space. Its position near the toggle ensures a logical grouping: users first add or remove the exhibition from their visit plan, then have the option to jump to the summarized plan. There is additional whitespace (16–24px) below this link before any subsequent elements, preserving clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline/block link within the right column’s vertical stack of actions, scrolling with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ticketing call-to-action is the strongest action in the right-hand column and is positioned so it is immediately visible without scrolling. It sits in the upper portion of the actions area, typically just below the admission and schedule block and slightly above or side-by-side with the visit plan toggle. In layout terms, it spans a larger portion of the right column—around 50–70% of the column width—and is aligned to the left edge of the text. Vertical spacing around it is generous (16–24px above and below) so it stands out as a distinct, tappable block. Its visual weight (solid fill, clear label) contrasts with the lighter text links, echoing the mockup’s restrained but clear hierarchy of emphasis.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level button in the right column; it does not stick or float but remains anchored within the action area of the main detail section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-exhibitions link is positioned within the central content column but away from the primary focus of the main two-column band, so it acts as a quiet navigation outlet. It appears in the lower part of the page, directly beneath the main exhibition detail area and typically just below the newsletter section. It is left-aligned with the central column and occupies a single text line, spanning perhaps 20–30% of the content width. A generous vertical gap of about 32px separates it from the section above, giving it breathing room and allowing it to function almost like a subtle footer link without competing with primary actions higher on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static link at the bottom of the main content flow, scrolling naturally and not fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter inline form is presented as a full-width (within the central container) single-column block situated below the main exhibition detail section. There is a clear horizontal rule or a generous whitespace gap (32–48px) above it to mark the transition from exhibition-specific content to broader engagement. The form’s content—typically a heading, short description, and email input with submit button—is centered horizontally within this block but aligned to the left within the column, preserving the editorial feel. Internal padding of 16–24px around the form elements creates a light, card-like presence without heavy borders. The form occupies a modest vertical footprint (approximately 15–20% of the viewport height on desktop), ensuring it’s noticeable but not overwhelming, and leaves another 24–32px of space below before the back-to-exhibitions link.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the central column, spanning its full width and scrolling with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Status messages for the exhibition detail page appear as a slim horizontal band at the very top of the exhibition content area, just inside the central column defined by the page-init component. They span the full width of this central column and are centered horizontally, with 8–16px of vertical padding inside to keep the message legible yet unobtrusive. Vertically, this band sits directly below the global header/page title and about 24–32px above the main two-column exhibition row, acting as an informational preface. When present (e.g., confirming ticket booking or visit plan changes), the band uses subtle background contrast but maintains the minimalist aesthetic. When no status is active, this area collapses, and the main content shifts up to occupy the space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky block at the top of the main content flow; it appears or disappears depending on status but does not remain fixed during scrolling.

name: Tickets & Admission
title: Tickets & Admission
description: Search, filter, and book dated tickets for gallery exhibitions and general admission, then send the selected booking to checkout.

core_features: Load dynamic ticket filter options (date range, exhibition types, time-of-day, adult price range, sort options) via getTicketFilterOptions and initialize the filter controls.
Read incoming URL parameters (date, exhibitionId, exhibitionType, timeOfDay, maxAdultPrice, sortBy) to pre-populate filters and constrain initial search results.

Provide a date picker and filter controls for exhibition type, time-of-day, maximum adult ticket price, and availability, plus a sort control (e.g., price-based), all backed by getTicketFilterOptions and URL state.
Search for available exhibition ticket slots using searchExhibitionTicketSlots based on the current filters and sort order, updating results dynamically.

Client-side filter of ticket slots by exhibitionId when that URL parameter is present, so the list can be pre-filtered to a specific exhibition without changing the searchExhibitionTicketSlots interface.
Allow users to select a single ticket slot from the results list and set ticket quantities (Adult, Teen, Child) for that slot.

Support the scenario of finding the cheapest special exhibition ticket for a given date, time-of-day, and maximum adult price by combining filters (exhibition type, time-of-day, maxAdultPrice) with sortBy set to a price-ascending option and then selecting a slot from the resulting list.
Create a TicketBooking and corresponding CheckoutOrder for the selected slot and quantities via createTicketBookingAndOrder, handling success and error states.
Navigate to checkout.html with the checkoutOrderId returned by createTicketBookingAndOrder when the user clicks "Continue to Checkout" and the booking is successfully created.
Offer an optional inline newsletter subscription form on the page that uses subscribeToNewsletter to create a NewsletterSubscription based on user-selected topics, interests, and format.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Basic ticket search and booking
1. User lands on tickets.html via the main navigation without URL parameters.
2. Page-init logic calls getTicketFilterOptions to obtain dateRangeDefaults, exhibitionTypes, timeOfDayOptions, adultPriceRange, and sortOptions.
3. The date picker initializes to a date derived from the filter defaults (for example, the range start) because no date URL parameter is present.
4. Default filter values are applied and reflected into the URL query parameters (date, exhibitionType if any, timeOfDay, maxAdultPrice, sortBy).
5. The page calls searchExhibitionTicketSlots with the current filter values and onlyAvailable set according to the "Only show available" toggle.
6. The ticket slot results list renders the returned slots.
