onDisplayStatusTooltip: Shows additional explanatory text based on capacityDisplayText when users interact (e.g., hover or click) with the status indicator.

components.functionality: Collects email, selected newsletter topics, optional interests, and preferred email format, then calls subscribeToNewsletter to create or update a newsletter subscription and shows a success or error message based on the response.
event_handlers: onInitializeNewsletterForm: Optionally pre-fills the email field using registrantEmail from the event-registration-form if available in the same session, without making extra API calls.
onChangeEmail: Updates the local email value when the user types and clears any email-related validation errors.
onChangeTopics: Updates the topics array in local state when the user selects or deselects topic checkboxes, ensuring at least one topic is selected before submission.
onChangeInterests: Updates the optional interests array in local state based on user selections.
onChangePreferredFormat: Updates the preferredFormat (e.g., html_email or plain_text) when the user selects a format option.
onValidateNewsletterForm: Ensures that the email is non-empty and structurally valid and that topics contains at least one selection before calling subscribeToNewsletter.
onSubmitNewsletterForm: Calls subscribeToNewsletter with email, topics, interests, and preferredFormat when validation succeeds, and sets a submitting state to block duplicate submissions.

onSubscriptionSuccess: Receives the successful response, stores the returned NewsletterSubscription and message, clears the submitting state, displays a success message, and may optionally disable further edits to the form.

onSubscriptionError: Handles error responses (success = false) from subscribeToNewsletter by clearing the submitting state, showing the response.message as an error, and leaving form values available for correction.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow. Global chrome (logo, nav, horizontal rule, page title) occupies the upper portion, centered in a single column. Below the page title and a separating rule, the event detail content is presented in a two-column hero row: event artwork/image on the left, and stacked event information on the right (header, capacity/status, registration form, and confirmation panel). Beneath this hero row, the layout collapses back to a centered single column for the longer event description and the newsletter inline form. All components sit within a central content column constrained to roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with generous side margins.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow. Global chrome (logo, nav, horizontal rule, page title) occupies the upper portion, centered in a single column. Below the page title and a separating rule, the event detail content is presented in a two-column hero row: event artwork/image on the left, and stacked event information on the right (header, capacity/status, registration form, and confirmation panel). Beneath this hero row, the layout collapses back to a centered single column for the longer event description and the newsletter inline form. All components sit within a central content column constrained to roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with generous side margins.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper 20–25% of the viewport is reserved for the gallery’s logo, navigation, and a horizontal rule, matching the original mockup. The page title for this view (e.g., the event name or an 'Event Detail' label) takes another 10–15% with ample white space before the main content. The primary hero content (image + right text stack) dominates the next 35–45% of the viewport height on first load. The central content column spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, with left/right white margins acting as a framing device. Within the hero row, the left image column uses about 35–40% of the content width and the right text/form column uses about 60–65%. Vertical gaps between major sections (hero row, description, newsletter) are in the 32–48px range, echoing the spacious gallery feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse and breathable. The header text lines are medium width and limited in count (title + a few key meta lines). The registration form is compact and vertically organized with clear grouping (adult vs teen, payment options) and comfortable padding (16–24px) inside a simple, border-defined card. The description section uses moderately short line lengths to maintain an editorial reading experience. No section is crammed; each block has noticeable padding and whitespace so that every element feels like a distinct exhibit piece rather than a dense information cluster.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from top to bottom: first the strong logo, then the horizontal nav bar, then a rule, then the centered page/event title. Another rule leads the eye into the hero content, where attention first lands on the left event artwork (the main splash of color), then moves right to the bold event title and key details. Immediately below the header, the capacity/status indicator provides a quick availability cue, followed by the registration form as a clear call-to-action channel. After interacting with the form, the confirmation panel appears in the same right column, keeping the interaction contextually anchored. Scrolling down shifts focus to the full-width description, then finally to the inline newsletter form, which is visually quieter and serves as a tertiary action at the end of the reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Within a clean, white gallery-like canvas, a centered column of content sits framed by generous left and right margins. The familiar global header—logo, navigation, and a subtle horizontal rule—occupies the top quarter of the viewport. Below, a centered event title and another horizontal rule introduce the event detail space. The main event area appears as a balanced two-column hero row: on the left, a rectangular event artwork image spans roughly two-fifths of the content width and anchors the composition with color; on the right, a vertical stack of black-on-white text and a minimal registration card occupy the remaining space. At the top of this right column, the event title and key meta information are typeset with plenty of breathing room, immediately followed by a small but clear availability/status indicator. Directly beneath sits a bordered, softly padded registration form card, aligned with the event text and kept visually simple. When registration succeeds, a confirmation panel appears in this same column, replacing or sitting just under the form to preserve continuity. Below the hero row, the layout returns to a single centered column: a spacious, editorial event description block with moderate line lengths encourages relaxed reading, and finally a succinct inline newsletter signup form sits at the bottom, lightly framed by whitespace and perhaps a thin rule, acting as a quiet secondary engagement point. The result is a calm, structured page where every element feels deliberate and easily scannable, mirroring a physical gallery’s signage and wall labels.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-provider component has no visual footprint. It sits conceptually at the top of the event detail content stack, just below the global page shell (logo, nav, page title), orchestrating data for all other components. It does not render any visible element, occupy no width or height, and does not affect spacing; the visual layout begins with the event-header-section positioned within the central content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, purely logical controller with zero layout presence; it neither shifts nor reserves any space in the document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event header section sits in the right-hand column of the main hero row, aligned horizontally with the event imagery on the left. The hero row itself is centered on the page and constrained to roughly 60–70% of the viewport width. Within this row, the left image column occupies about 35–40% of the width, while the right column (containing this header, the capacity indicator, the registration form, and confirmation panel) occupies about 60–65%. The top edge of the header aligns vertically with the top of the event image, creating a clean, shared baseline. Inside this right column, the event header is the first element: it spans the full width of the right column and typically contains the event title, subtitle or category, date/time, and venue. It has a generous top margin of about 32–48px from the last global rule (under the page title) and internal spacing of 16–24px separating each typographic line. Text is left-aligned, creating a strong vertical text edge that lines up with the left edge of the registration form card below. The bottom of the header section sits about 8–16px above the capacity-and-status-indicator so that those two elements feel closely related while still distinct. The header visually dominates its local area with a larger, bold title and clear hierarchy, but it remains clean and uncluttered, using whitespace to maintain an editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level behavior within the right column of the hero row; it resizes vertically with content but maintains its proportional relationship to the event image and the components stacked beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event description section is positioned below the entire hero row (image + right-hand stack) and spans the central content column in a single-column arrangement. It begins approximately 32–48px beneath the bottom edge of the lower-most element of the hero row (typically the registration form or confirmation panel), preserving the generous sectional spacing seen in the mockup. The description’s text column is centered and aligned with the right column’s left edge above, ensuring a consistent vertical text axis. It occupies roughly 60–65% of the viewport width on large screens, maintaining moderate line lengths for comfortable reading. There is 16–24px of internal vertical spacing between paragraphs or subheadings within this section. The description flows downward as a continuous block, with ample whitespace on both sides and slightly more space below (24–32px) before any subsequent section, such as the newsletter-inline-form. The overall impression is of a museum wall label: clear, calm, and easy to read without visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flowing block within the main content column; it expands vertically with copy length and always remains full-width within the constrained central column, pushing the newsletter section further down as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event registration form sits directly beneath the capacity-and-status-indicator in the right-hand column of the hero row, forming a vertically stacked block of interactive content. It is rendered as a simple, rectangular card that spans the full width of the right column (about 60–65% of the central content width), with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. The top of the card is spaced a modest 16px or less below the capacity indicator, visually linking the two. The form fields are organized in a vertical sequence with 12–16px gaps between field groups (e.g., adult attendee, teen attendee, payment options), preserving the low-density, uncluttered aesthetic. The card’s height is determined by its content, but it typically occupies roughly one-third to one-half of the hero row’s vertical space, so that the top of the card remains above or near the vertical midpoint of the event image on the left. The submit button is aligned with the form’s left text edge and has clear separation (16–24px) from the last input field. The card may use a subtle border or faint background tint to distinguish it from the white canvas, but it remains visually light to match the minimalist branding. On narrower screens, this form collapses below the event image and header, maintaining full-width within the central column while preserving its internal spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, non-sticky block within the right column of the hero row, vertically stacked under the capacity indicator. On responsive breakpoints, it shifts into a single-column flow beneath the header and image while preserving proportional padding and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-confirmation panel is placed in the same right-hand column as the registration form, occupying the same horizontal width. In its default state (before a successful submission), it either remains hidden or collapsed to zero height, contributing no vertical space. After a successful registration, it appears immediately below the registration form card, separated by a modest 16–24px vertical gap, or it may replace the form entirely while occupying its vertical space. The panel is styled as a clean, bordered or subtly shaded box with 16–24px internal padding, mirroring the form card’s proportions to maintain visual continuity. It spans the full width of the right column and aligns its text with the registration form’s left edge. The height of the panel is content-driven, but it typically remains relatively compact (roughly 20–30% of the hero row height), displaying succinct confirmation details. If it replaces the form, the hero row’s overall height remains similar; if it appears beneath the form, the hero row grows vertically in a controlled way, still leaving ample whitespace below before the description section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally visible block that occupies standard flow space only when active. It inherits the right-column positioning and width from the registration form and never overlaps other components; instead, it pushes the event-description-section downward as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The capacity-and-status-indicator is a compact element positioned directly below the event-header-section and directly above the event-registration-form within the right-hand hero column. It shares the same left alignment and width as the header and form, acting as a small, high-signal row in the vertical stack. The indicator sits approximately 8–16px below the last line of the event header, creating a tight visual association with the event information while remaining a distinct component. It might present a short status label (e.g., 'Open', 'Few spots left', 'Waitlisted') and an optional simple bar or numeric capacity representation, but is intentionally minimal in height—around 32–48px total—so it does not visually compete with the header or form. There is a similar 8–16px gap between the bottom of this indicator and the top of the registration form card. This tight vertical rhythm ensures users naturally read from header to status to form as a single flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the right column’s vertical stack, expanding slightly with its content but generally remaining a slim horizontal strip. It moves with the rest of the hero content as the page scrolls and does not use sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter inline form is placed near the bottom of the page, following the event-description-section in the natural vertical flow. It begins approximately 32–48px below the last line of the description, maintaining the generous section spacing. The form is centered within the same central content column and typically spans about 40–50% of the viewport width, making it visibly narrower than the main text column to feel lighter and less demanding. It appears as a simple, single-row or short stacked form (email field plus subscribe button) with 16–24px internal horizontal padding and 16px vertical padding. A thin horizontal rule or slightly increased whitespace above it may subtly separate it from the description without overwhelming the minimalist aesthetic. The form’s fields align with the central text axis established by the description section, reinforcing consistency. Overall, it reads as a gentle, optional call-to-action that does not compete with the primary event registration area higher on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in the main column at the bottom of the content stack, expanding slightly with form content but generally remaining compact and unobtrusive. It shifts responsively to full width on small screens while retaining its centered alignment and generous surrounding whitespace.

name: Shop
title: Shop
description: Browse shop categories, filter and sort products (such as postcards and prints), add items to the cart, and access the cart, with optional newsletter subscription.

core_features: Load and display available shop categories using getShopCategories so users can switch between product groups (e.g., postcards & prints, books).
If a category is preselected via the category URL parameter, auto-select that category; otherwise, select the first available category from getShopCategories.
Fetch category-specific filter and sort options using getShopCategoryFilterOptions, including dynamic price range, rating thresholds, and sort options.
Allow users to set maximum price, minimum rating, and sort order; initialize these controls from URL parameters (maxPrice, minRating, sortBy) when present.
Search and list products for the selected category using searchProducts, applying the current filters (categoryKey, maxPrice, minRating, sortBy, onlyAvailable).
Display products in a grid with image, name, price, availability, and rating data returned from searchProducts.
Allow users to add products to the cart directly from the product grid using addProductToCart, including specifying quantity.
Show a cart summary (item count and subtotal) based on the response from addProductToCart, and provide a link to cart.html to view full cart details.
Enable navigation to individual product detail pages (product.html) when a user clicks on a product card, passing productId via URL parameters.
Provide a newsletter signup form that invokes subscribeToNewsletter with user-selected topics, interests, and preferred format.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing with a preselected postcards & prints category
1. User clicks a link on index.html pointing to shop.html with a category URL parameter.
2. Shop page reads the category parameter from the URL.
3. shop-page-controller calls getShopCategories and finds the category whose categoryKey matches the URL parameter.
4. shop-page-controller sets this category as selected and calls getShopCategoryFilterOptions with that categoryKey.
5. shop-page-controller reads any maxPrice, minRating, and sortBy URL parameters and initializes the filter controls accordingly.
6. shop-page-controller calls searchProducts with categoryKey, maxPrice, minRating, sortBy, and onlyAvailable (defaulting to available products only).
7. product-grid displays the returned products.
8. User can click any product card to navigate to product.html?productId=<productId> for more details.

Workflow 2: Adjust filters to find affordable, well-rated items
1. User changes the maximum price in the filter panel (e.g., via slider or numeric input) within the bounds provided by getShopCategoryFilterOptions.priceRange.
2. User selects a minimum rating option (e.g., a ratingOptions entry) and optionally changes the sort order using sortOptions.
3. category-filter-panel updates the URL query string to reflect new maxPrice, minRating, and sortBy values without changing the selected category.
4. shop-page-controller detects the URL change (or triggers an internal refresh) and re-reads the updated URL parameters.
5. shop-page-controller calls searchProducts again with the updated filters.
6. product-grid re-renders with the new filtered and sorted product list.

Workflow 3: Add multiple items to the cart and view cart
1. User browses the product-grid and identifies a product to purchase.
2. Inside a product-card, user sets a quantity (e.g., via a numeric input) and clicks an Add to Cart button.
3. product-card invokes addProductToCart with the productId and selected quantity.
4. On success, addProductToCart returns success, cartId, cartItemId, updated itemCount, subtotal, currency, and a message.
5. cart-summary-bar updates its internal state to display the latest itemCount and subtotal.
6. User repeats steps 2–5 for additional products, each time updating the summary from the latest addProductToCart response.
7. User clicks a View Cart link in the cart-summary-bar, navigating to cart.html without additional parameters (cart.html is responsible for loading the cart by context or session).

Workflow 4: Newsletter subscription from the shop page
1. User scrolls to the newsletter-signup-form component.
2. User enters their email address.
3. User selects one or more topics (e.g., monthly_highlights, new_exhibitions_openings, special_offers) and optionally selects interests (e.g., contemporary_art, family_activities) and preferredFormat (html_email or plain_text).
4. User submits the form.
5. newsletter-signup-form calls subscribeToNewsletter with email, topics, interests, and preferredFormat.
6. On success, the component displays a success message; on failure, it shows an error message based on the returned message field.
interactions: On initial page load, shop-page-controller reads URL parameters (category, maxPrice, minRating, sortBy) and uses them to initialize state before calling any interfaces.

Category selection: When a user clicks a category in shop-category-list, the component updates the category URL parameter, clears or preserves filter parameters as defined by the design (e.g., reset filters when changing category), and triggers re-loading of filter options and products for the new category.

Filter changes: When the user edits maxPrice or minRating or chooses a rating option, category-filter-panel validates values against the ranges provided by getShopCategoryFilterOptions and then updates the URL parameters and re-triggers searchProducts.
Sort changes: When the user selects a sort option, category-filter-panel updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers searchProducts to retrieve a newly sorted product list.

Availability toggle (if present): If the UI includes an availability toggle (e.g., “In stock only”), product-search-toolbar updates an internal onlyAvailable state and can optionally append an onlyAvailable query parameter to the URL before calling searchProducts.
Product card click: Clicking on a product name, image, or dedicated button inside product-grid navigates to product.html?productId=<productId> using the productId value returned by searchProducts.

Add to cart action: Clicking the Add to Cart button in a product-card triggers addProductToCart with the selected quantity; based on the response, the card may display feedback (e.g., confirmation or error message) and the cart-summary-bar updates itemCount and subtotal.
View Cart navigation: Clicking a View Cart button or link in cart-summary-bar takes the user to cart.html, which will use the backend/session to identify the correct cart.
Newsletter form submit: Submitting newsletter-signup-form triggers subscribeToNewsletter; user feedback is shown based on success and message properties in the response.

URL-based state restoration: If the user reloads the page or opens a shared shop.html URL containing category, maxPrice, minRating, and sortBy parameters, shop-page-controller reconstructs the same view (selected category, filters, and product list) by calling getShopCategories, getShopCategoryFilterOptions, and searchProducts with those parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from URL query parameters and live API responses. On each load (or navigation within the single-page context): 1) The controller parses the category parameter. After getShopCategories resolves, it sets selectedCategoryKey to the categoryKey that matches category. If no match is found or the parameter is absent, it selects the first category from the getShopCategories result. 2) The controller then calls getShopCategoryFilterOptions with selectedCategoryKey. The returned priceRange, ratingOptions, and sortOptions are used to configure filter controls. 3) The controller then parses maxPrice, minRating, and sortBy from the URL. If present, they are validated against the ranges/options returned by getShopCategoryFilterOptions. Invalid values are ignored or normalized, and the URL may be updated to reflect normalized values. 4) The controller maintains an internal onlyAvailable flag (defaulting to true unless overridden by an optional onlyAvailable URL parameter if implemented). 5) With selectedCategoryKey, validated maxPrice, minRating, sortBy, and onlyAvailable, the controller calls searchProducts to obtain the product list. 6) When the user interacts with category or filter controls, components update the URL query string to reflect the new state (category, maxPrice, minRating, sortBy, and optionally onlyAvailable). The controller then re-runs the same loading logic and refreshes the grid via searchProducts. 7) Cart state is not stored in the URL; instead, the cart-summary-bar reflects the last known values returned by addProductToCart (itemCount, subtotal, currency). The actual cart contents and cartId are managed by the backend and are used by cart.html via its own mechanisms. 8) Newsletter subscription does not affect URL state; it is a one-off call to subscribeToNewsletter with user-provided form data.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, category selection, filter configuration, and product search. It orchestrates calls to getShopCategories, getShopCategoryFilterOptions, and searchProducts, and propagates data and state to child components.

Displays the list of shop categories returned by getShopCategories and allows the user to switch between them. Clicking a category updates the category URL parameter and triggers reloading of filter options and products for the selected category.

Shows and manages filters for the currently selected category, including maximum price and minimum rating, using dynamic bounds and options from getShopCategoryFilterOptions. It also exposes the sort options to the user. On change, it updates URL parameters (maxPrice, minRating, sortBy) and requests the controller to refresh product results using searchProducts.

Provides quick access controls for sort selection and an optional availability toggle (e.g., in-stock only). It mirrors and manipulates a subset of the filters managed by category-filter-panel and ensures consistency with URL parameters and searchProducts queries.

Displays the list of products returned by searchProducts for the current category and filters. It renders a collection of product-card components, passing each product’s data (productId, name, price, ratingAverage, ratingCount, isAvailable, imageUrl) and relays add-to-cart events upward.

Represents a single product in the grid. Shows product information from searchProducts, supports navigation to product.html?productId=<productId> when clicked, and provides an Add to Cart form with quantity selection that calls addProductToCart.

Displays a summary of the current cart status using the response data from the most recent addProductToCart call (itemCount, subtotal, currency, message). Provides a View Cart button/link that navigates to cart.html to see full cart details and complete checkout.

Collects an email address, topics, interests, and preferred format from the user and calls subscribeToNewsletter on submit. Shows user feedback based on the success and message fields from the subscription response.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page adopts the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the exhibitions mockup, with a strong central column. The shop-page-controller first establishes a centered, fixed-width content column. Within this column, the Shop page-specific hierarchy runs: a subtle horizontal rule, a single centered page heading ('Shop'), another horizontal rule, then a block of page tools (category list followed by search/sort toolbar) stacked vertically with generous gaps. Below this, the main content area mirrors the mockup’s two-column pattern: a narrower vertical filter panel on the left and a broader product grid on the right. Within the grid, each product-card echoes the original exhibition row layout, with the product image on the left and textual details on the right. The cart summary bar is layered along the bottom edge of the viewport and the newsletter signup sits as a final centered block at the bottom of the content column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page adopts the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the exhibitions mockup, with a strong central column. The shop-page-controller first establishes a centered, fixed-width content column. Within this column, the Shop page-specific hierarchy runs: a subtle horizontal rule, a single centered page heading ('Shop'), another horizontal rule, then a block of page tools (category list followed by search/sort toolbar) stacked vertically with generous gaps. Below this, the main content area mirrors the mockup’s two-column pattern: a narrower vertical filter panel on the left and a broader product grid on the right. Within the grid, each product-card echoes the original exhibition row layout, with the product image on the left and textual details on the right. The cart summary bar is layered along the bottom edge of the viewport and the newsletter signup sits as a final centered block at the bottom of the content column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A max-width central content column (around 60–70% of the viewport width) is centered on the page, leaving wide white margins on both sides to create a gallery-like frame. The top 20–25% of the first viewport is devoted to the page heading and the tools section (categories plus search toolbar), with generous top and bottom padding inside this band. The horizontal rules above and below the heading occupy the full width of the central column and visually segment the global chrome from the shopping content. Below, the main content occupies the remaining 70–80% of the vertical space: the left filter panel uses roughly 28–32% of the central column, and the product grid takes the remaining 68–72%. Vertical spacing between major sections (heading/tools, tools/main content, main content/newsletter) follows the 32–48px rhythm, and internal component padding generally uses 16–24px on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains low visual density with a calm, editorial feel. Each band of content (heading, categories, search, filters, grid, newsletter) is clearly separated by generous whitespace, and no area feels crowded with controls. Category chips or links are spaced so that individual labels are easy to scan. The search toolbar focuses on one or two primary controls (search field and sort) to avoid clutter. The filter panel stacks a small number of filter groups with obvious separation. The product grid shows a modest number of product cards per row with ample horizontal gutters, and each card keeps copy short: title, artist or type, price, and a single call-to-action. As with the mockup, line lengths are moderate and breathing room is prioritized over packing in more items above the fold.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is guided top-down, with clear stops: first the centered 'Shop' heading framed by horizontal rules, which echoes the strong title moment in the exhibitions page. From there, attention moves down to the category list, which behaves like a horizontal local navigation strip, then to the search/sort toolbar. The next visual stop is the top of the main content where the left filter column establishes structure, but the colorful product images on the right grid quickly become the primary focal points—mirroring how artwork images on the exhibitions page lead the eye. Within each product-card, the image on the left grabs attention first, then the eye flows to the right for title, details, and price. The cart-summary-bar at the bottom of the viewport acts as a subtle, persistent anchor in peripheral vision, while the newsletter-signup-form forms a soft landing at the end of the scroll, centered and uncluttered.

layout.overall_description: The Shop page feels like a minimalist gallery catalog laid out on a white table. A centered column floats within generous left and right margins, framed by subtle horizontal rules that divide the page into calm bands. Near the top, the word 'Shop' sits alone in the middle of the page, in a bold but understated type treatment, with ample space above and below to signal a clear transition into commerce. Just beneath, a clean row of shop categories stretches horizontally across the central column, acting as a local navigation strip, followed by a simple, centered search and sort toolbar that maintains the quiet editorial feel. Scrolling slightly reveals the main content split into two vertical columns: a slim filter panel on the left and a broader field of products on the right. Each product card is composed like a mini exhibition entry, with a rectangular product image on the left and neatly stacked text on the right. The white space between cards and rows keeps the grid airy and dignified. A slim cart summary bar hugs the bottom edge of the viewport, aligning with the central column and staying in view without dominating. At the bottom of the page, a centered newsletter signup block sits alone with generous margins, like a final, optional exhibit label, maintaining the black-and-white, gallery-like aesthetic throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shop-page-controller spans the full browser width but establishes a centered content column that all other shop components align to. This column is constrained to a fixed or max width (approximately 60–70% of the viewport) and is horizontally centered, leaving wide white margins on both sides that echo the gallery framing from the mockup. Vertically, the controller applies a generous top padding band before the first visible shop element, ensuring the shop content begins comfortably beneath any global header. Just inside this central column, the controller first renders a thin horizontal rule running the full width of the column, then centers the page heading ('Shop') below it with about 24–32px of space. Another matching horizontal rule appears below the heading, separated by a similar 24–32px gap, creating a distinct title band. Below this band, the controller stacks the shop-category-list, the product-search-toolbar, and then the main two-column content (category-filter-panel on the left and product-grid on the right), each separated vertically by 32–48px. The newsletter-signup-form is anchored near the bottom of this column, with extra vertical margin above and below to feel like a separate, optional section. The controller itself is static, scrolling naturally with the page while maintaining consistent central alignment for all child components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a static, centered max-width content column for all shop elements, adds top and sectional padding, and manages the vertical stacking order of the page segments without any fixed or sticky behavior itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shop-category-list sits inside the central column, directly below the lower horizontal rule of the heading band, separated from it by roughly 32px of vertical space. It is arranged as a horizontal strip of category labels centered on the page. The entire category row spans about 70–80% of the column width, leaving subtle margins on either side to preserve the airy feel. Category items (e.g., 'All', 'Prints', 'Postcards', 'Originals') are aligned in a single line on larger screens, each separated by consistent horizontal gaps of 16–24px. The text for each category is set in all-caps or a clear typographic style that matches the navigation aesthetic from the mockup, with a simple underline or weight change for the active category rather than heavy buttons. There is a generous 24–32px of whitespace both above and below this row so it reads as its own band of navigation rather than blending into adjacent components. On narrower viewports, the list may wrap to a second line, but retains centered alignment and the same breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow; it remains part of the normal scroll and does not stick or float, but visually behaves like a localized navigation bar between the heading and the tools/content below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-filter-panel forms the left column of the main content area. It begins below the product-search-toolbar, aligned to the left edge of the central column, with a vertical offset equal to the top of the product-grid so that both start at the same horizontal line. On desktop widths, it occupies roughly 28–32% of the central column’s width, leaving the remaining 68–72% for the product-grid on the right. Inside the panel, filters are organized in a vertical stack: section headings (e.g., 'Type', 'Price', 'Availability') appear in a bold but compact typographic style, followed by filter controls with 16–24px padding around them. Each filter group is separated from the next by 24–32px of vertical space, preserving the low-density feel. The panel’s content is framed by consistent internal padding (around 16–24px on all sides) and a very subtle right-hand boundary, such as a hairline vertical rule or a slight tonal difference, to imply the two-column split without feeling boxy. The panel’s height can extend beyond the initial viewport if there are many filters, but its top portion tends to remain in view as the user scrolls the product grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a left-aligned column within the central container; on larger screens it uses sticky positioning so its top edge stays just below the heading/tools band as the user scrolls, keeping key filters accessible while the product-grid scrolls. On smaller screens it becomes a standard block stacked above the product-grid with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-search-toolbar is positioned within the central column between the shop-category-list and the main two-column content area. It sits centered on the page with a vertical gap of about 24–32px below the categories and 32–40px above the start of the filters and product grid. The toolbar spans approximately 60–70% of the central column’s width, leaving visible white margins on its left and right. Its internal layout is horizontal: the main search input occupies the majority of the width, aligned to the left within the toolbar, with a smaller sort dropdown or view toggle aligned to the right. Controls are kept minimal and clean, avoiding heavy borders; instead, thin underlines or subtle keylines in black/gray reference the mockup’s minimalism. The row height is moderate, with around 12–16px padding above and below the form controls, so the toolbar reads as a single, uncluttered strip. Above the controls, a small, left-aligned label like 'Browse the shop' or a result count may be included but kept subtle to maintain low visual density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow; it does not stick or float, but serves as a transitional band between the navigation-like category row and the main content columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid occupies the right-hand portion of the main content area, starting at the same vertical position as the category-filter-panel and extending downward as products are listed. It is aligned to the right edge of the central column and spans roughly 68–72% of the central column’s width. The grid is laid out in 2–3 columns on desktop screens, depending on the overall page width, with wide horizontal gutters (24–32px) between columns and vertical spacing of 32–40px between rows to maintain the airy, gallery-like feeling. Each grid cell contains a single product-card that stretches to fill the column width, so the cards form clear vertical stacks. The grid’s left edge aligns cleanly against the right boundary of the filter panel, reinforcing the two-column structure seen in the exhibitions mockup. The first row of the grid is positioned high enough that one or two rows of products are visible within the initial viewport beneath the heading/tools area, without feeling cramped. As the user scrolls, the grid continues downward in a uniform rhythm, with no tight masonry overlaps, ensuring a calm and predictable vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrolling content within the main two-column area; its items scroll beneath the sticky filter panel and above the fixed cart-summary-bar at the bottom of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each product-card is designed as a compact echo of the exhibition entry layout. Within its allocated grid cell, the card uses a horizontal split: on the left, a rectangular product image occupies roughly 35–40% of the card’s width; on the right, a vertical stack of text and controls occupies the remaining 60–65%. The card has internal padding of about 16–24px on all sides, ensuring the image and text do not touch the edges and reinforcing the editorial feel. The product image is proportioned similarly to an artwork reproduction, with a clean white or neutral background around it. To the right, the product title appears first, set in a slightly bolder weight and aligned to the top, followed by a smaller line for artist or category, then the price slightly separated by 8–12px of space. A single call-to-action element ('Add to cart' or an icon button) sits at the bottom or right-aligned within this stack, with enough margin around it to feel deliberate rather than crowded. Vertical spacing within the text stack is modest but consistent (8–12px), and the overall card height is kept moderate so that each product feels like an individual exhibit record rather than a dense retail tile.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the grid; cards expand vertically as needed for content but maintain a consistent internal two-column image/text layout across all products.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-bar is a slim, horizontally oriented band that aligns with the central content column but is visually anchored to the bottom edge of the viewport. It spans either the full browser width or, more minimally, the same max width as the central column and is centered horizontally. The bar’s height is modest (around 56–72px), with 12–16px of vertical padding around its contents. Inside, the content is arranged in a single row: a concise summary of cart contents (e.g., '2 items • $42.00') is left-aligned within the central column, while a primary 'View cart' or 'Checkout' button is right-aligned. The styling is in keeping with the black-and-white aesthetic—light background, thin top border line, and restrained typography—so it feels like a subtle utility strip rather than a loud promotion. There is a small gap (8–12px) between the bottom of the main scrolling content and the top of the bar to keep it visually separate. When the cart is empty, the bar may be minimized or simplified, but its spatial footprint remains modest to avoid crowding the view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the bottom of the viewport so it remains visible as the user scrolls, always aligned with the central column. It floats above page content without covering important actions, providing persistent access to the cart.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-signup-form is placed near the bottom of the page, below the product-grid and above any global footer, inside the same central content column. It is centered horizontally and spans roughly 50–60% of the column width, making it visually slimmer than the main content to emphasize its optional, secondary nature. There is a generous vertical margin of 48–64px above it, clearly separating it from the last row of products, and a similar margin below before the page ends. The layout inside the form is simple: a short heading or sentence (e.g., 'Stay informed about new prints') is centered at the top, followed by a single-line email input and a submit button aligned either inline or stacked with 8–12px of vertical spacing. The surrounding whitespace and minimalistic black-and-white styling ensure the component feels like a quiet invitation rather than a noisy banner. It reads as a final, calm section in the vertical flow, mirroring the spacious section breaks seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it appears as a final block in the natural scroll order, with no sticky or floating behavior, serving as a gentle endpoint to the shopping experience.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a single shop product and allows users to add it to their cart or subscribe to the gallery newsletter.

core_features: Load and display full product details (name, image, description, price, availability, rating) using getProductDetail with the productId URL parameter.
Allow users to select a quantity and add the product to the cart using addProductToCart, showing feedback based on the response.
Provide a link back to the relevant shop category listing (shop.html) using the product’s category when available.
Provide a way to navigate to the cart (cart.html) after a product is added.
Allow users to subscribe to the gallery newsletter from the product page using subscribeToNewsletter, including topic and format preferences.
user_workflows: View Product Details: 1) User lands on product.html with a productId URL parameter. 2) The page reads productId from the URL. 3) The page calls getProductDetail(productId). 4) On success, product details are displayed: name, image, description, priceDisplayText, ratingDisplayText, availability status, and categoryName. 5) A 'Back to category' link is configured to navigate to shop.html with the product’s category as a URL parameter. 6) If getProductDetail fails or returns no product, an error state is shown instead of the detail content.

Add Product to Cart: 1) User adjusts the quantity field in the add-to-cart form. 2) User clicks the 'Add to cart' button. 3) The page validates that quantity is a positive number. 4) The page calls addProductToCart(productId, quantity). 5) If success is true, the page shows a confirmation message using the message and updated cart summary (itemCount, subtotal, currency). 6) A 'View Cart' button becomes visible or remains visible and, when clicked, navigates to cart.html. 7) If success is false, an error message from the response is displayed and the cart is not updated.

Navigate Back to Shop Category: 1) After viewing the product, the user clicks 'Back to category'. 2) The page reads the loaded product’s category (or categoryName, if used for mapping). 3) The browser navigates to shop.html with a category URL parameter derived from the product data (e.g., shop.html?category={product.category}).

Newsletter Subscription From Product Page: 1) User scrolls to the newsletter subscription section. 2) User enters an email address. 3) User selects at least one topic and a preferred email format; interests may be optionally selected. 4) User submits the newsletter form. 5) The page calls subscribeToNewsletter(email, topics, interests, preferredFormat). 6) If success is true, a success message is displayed. 7) If success is false, an error message from the response is displayed and the user can correct the input and resubmit.
interactions: On page load, read productId from the URL and trigger getProductDetail; show a loading state until the response is received.
If productId is missing or invalid in the URL, show an error state explaining that the product cannot be loaded and do not attempt getProductDetail.
Click on 'Back to category' link: navigate to shop.html with category set from the loaded product’s category value in the URL query string.
Change in quantity input: update local component state for quantity; ensure value remains within valid numeric bounds before submission.
Click on 'Add to cart' button: validate quantity and call addProductToCart with the current productId and quantity. Disable the button while the request is in progress.
Handle addProductToCart success: update UI with confirmation message, cart itemCount, and subtotal; display or enable a 'View Cart' button.
Handle addProductToCart failure: show an error message; allow the user to adjust quantity and retry.
Click on 'View Cart' button: navigate to cart.html without additional parameters.

Submit newsletter subscription form: validate email format and ensure at least one topic and a preferredFormat are selected; then call subscribeToNewsletter. Disable the submit button during the request.
Handle subscribeToNewsletter success: show a success confirmation and optionally clear or lock the form fields.
Handle subscribeToNewsletter failure: show an error message, keep the user’s input so they can correct and resubmit.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the productId URL parameter. On initial load and on any URL change, the page reads productId from the query string (product.html?productId=...). If productId is present, getProductDetail(productId) is called and the page state transitions from 'loading' to either 'loaded' (with product data) or 'error' (if the call fails or returns no matching product). If productId is missing, the page enters an 'invalid' error state without calling getProductDetail. The loaded Product data controls several derived UI states: availability (based on product.isAvailable; when false, the add-to-cart form is disabled), category navigation (the 'Back to category' link targets shop.html with category taken from product.category or a corresponding category value returned by getProductDetail), and rating display (using ratingDisplayText). The add-to-cart operation does not change the URL; instead it updates transient UI state for cart summary and feedback based on the addProductToCart response (success, itemCount, subtotal, currency, message). The presence of a successful add-to-cart response also controls whether the 'View Cart' button is highlighted or shown. The newsletter subscription state is local to the page; it is reset or marked completed based on the subscribeToNewsletter response and does not affect the URL. All persistent navigation between pages (back to shop listing and to cart) uses URL parameters as specified.

components.functionality: Reads the productId from the URL query string, calls getProductDetail, and manages the high-level loading, success, and error states for the product detail page.
Displays core product information returned from getProductDetail, including product.name, categoryName, priceDisplayText, ratingDisplayText, and a primary product image from product.imageUrl.
Shows the full text description of the product and any additional textual information available in product.description.
Provides a link to return to the relevant shop category listing, navigating to shop.html with the category URL parameter derived from the loaded product’s category.
Allows the user to choose a quantity and add the current product to the cart via addProductToCart, handling validation, loading state, and success or error feedback.

Displays the result of the most recent add-to-cart action, including the response message, updated itemCount, subtotal, and currency from addProductToCart, and shows a 'View Cart' button to navigate to cart.html.
Shows whether the product is currently available for purchase based on product.isAvailable and disables or hides the add-to-cart form when the product is not available.
Displays the product rating information using product.ratingAverage, product.ratingCount, and ratingDisplayText, without allowing direct rating submission.

Presents an inline newsletter subscription form allowing the user to enter an email address, choose topics, optionally select interests, and choose a preferredFormat, then calls subscribeToNewsletter and handles the response.
Displays appropriate messages when productId is missing from the URL, when getProductDetail fails, or when no product is returned, and suppresses other product-dependent components in these cases.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The product detail page adopts the mockup’s centered, linear top-to-bottom flow. Above (handled by global layout) sit the logo, navigation, a horizontal rule, and any page-level heading. This product detail area begins with a subtle, left-aligned 'back to category' link at the top of the central content column, followed by a single hero row where the product image (inside the product-header component) sits on the left and the product’s textual and interactive details form a right-hand vertical stack. Below this hero row, the longer product description appears as a spacious single-column block, followed by a horizontally centered newsletter subscribe section. Loader and error states replace this main content area when active, keeping the structure simple and focused.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The product detail page adopts the mockup’s centered, linear top-to-bottom flow. Above (handled by global layout) sit the logo, navigation, a horizontal rule, and any page-level heading. This product detail area begins with a subtle, left-aligned 'back to category' link at the top of the central content column, followed by a single hero row where the product image (inside the product-header component) sits on the left and the product’s textual and interactive details form a right-hand vertical stack. Below this hero row, the longer product description appears as a spacious single-column block, followed by a horizontally centered newsletter subscribe section. Loader and error states replace this main content area when active, keeping the structure simple and focused.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: All product content is constrained to a central column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with generous left and right margins acting as white framing space. The hero row (product-header with its left image / right text stack plus add-to-cart and status elements) occupies the top half of the main content area, with ample top padding (approximately 48–64px) from the nav area above. Vertical spacing between the back link and hero row, the hero row and description, and the description and newsletter form is generous (around 32–48px), echoing the gallery-like spaciousness. The loader and error handler occupy the same central column but are vertically centered within the visible content region when possible, surrounded by large amounts of whitespace.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and editorial. Text blocks are kept to moderate line lengths within the central column, and key actions (add to cart, newsletter signup) are presented as singular, uncluttered CTAs without surrounding noise. Within the hero row, the right-hand column is a simple vertical stack of a few concise components (header, rating, availability, add-to-cart, feedback), each separated by comfortable spacing (16–24px), avoiding overly dense groupings. The description section and newsletter form are visually light, with plenty of whitespace above and below, ensuring the page never feels crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s strong top-down path. After the user’s eye passes the global branding and nav, it encounters the understated back-to-category link, then quickly settles on the hero row where the product image on the left acts as the initial visual anchor. From there, attention moves right to the bold product title and key details (product-header), then naturally down through the rating, availability, and the prominent add-to-cart button. Any cart feedback message appears just beneath the form, reinforcing the action. Once these primary interactions are processed, the eye continues downward to the more detailed description, and finally to the newsletter subscribe form at the bottom. Loader and error states temporarily take over this flow by centering a single focal message in the same central column, keeping the gaze controlled and uncluttered.

layout.overall_description: The product detail page feels like a single, focused exhibit in a gallery. Underneath the site’s logo, navigation, and a thin horizontal rule, the main content is framed within a wide, centered column that occupies about two-thirds of the viewport width. At the top of this column, a small, left-aligned back-to-category link quietly anchors the user’s context. A generous gap below leads into the hero row: on the left, a large, rectangular product image (within the product-header component) sits like a framed artwork, occupying roughly 35–40% of the row’s width. On the right, aligned to the same vertical center as the image, a crisp stack of elements begins with the product title and key info (product-header text), followed by rating, availability indicator, and the add-to-cart form, with each block separated by comfortable white space. Any cart feedback message appears just under the form, still tied to the right-hand column, so the action and its confirmation are tightly coupled. Beneath this hero band, the layout relaxes into a single-column text area for the product description, spanning the same central width but with a slightly narrower effective text line for easy reading and with ample air above and below. A subtle horizontal divide and extra spacing then introduce a centered, inline newsletter subscribe form, which feels like a quiet invitation rather than a loud promotion. Throughout, a neutral black-and-white palette, restrained typography, and generous margins maintain a calm, editorial atmosphere. Loading and error states, when shown, replace the hero and description content with a single centered message in the same column, preserving the clean, gallery-like structure while signaling system status clearly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-detail-loader occupies the same central content column as the normal product content. When active, it temporarily replaces all other product detail components. It is horizontally centered within this column and positioned approximately 20–25% down from the top of the viewport, so it sits comfortably below the global navigation and page-level heading. The loader itself (spinner or skeleton) is compact, surrounded by generous empty space on all sides, with any loading text stacked directly below it. The left and right sides maintain wide margins to the viewport edges, reinforcing the centered, minimal feel. No other product components are visible while the loader is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a central, inline block within the main content container, replacing other content while loading; scrolls naturally with the page if the viewport is short but is typically visible without scrolling due to its vertical offset.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-header defines the main hero row at the top of the product content. It is placed in the central content column, starting roughly 48–64px below the global horizontal rule or any page-level heading. The component spans the full width of this central column and internally follows the two-column pattern from the mockup: on the left, a large rectangular product image occupies approximately 35–40% of the component’s width; on the right, a vertical stack of text occupies the remaining 60–65%. The left image aligns to the top of the row, and the right text column is top-aligned to the image edge, forming a strong horizontal starting line. Within the right text stack, the product title appears first as the visually dominant line, followed by supporting metadata such as artist, medium, or price. The space between the image and the right column text is modest (around 24px), keeping the two visually connected. There is generous padding above and below this entire hero block (around 32–48px) before transitioning to subsequent elements, ensuring the product feels like a single featured artwork on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level hero section within the central column; adapts to smaller viewports by stacking its internal image above the text column while maintaining generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-description-section sits directly below the hero row formed by the product-header (and the right-column status/CTA components), separated by a generous vertical gap of about 48px, reinforcing a clear transition from overview to detailed information. It spans the full width of the central content column but constrains the text itself to a comfortable reading width, either by limiting line length to roughly 60–70 characters or by applying subtle internal side padding. The description is left-aligned, with ample whitespace above, below, and to both sides, giving it an editorial, catalog-like feel. There are no additional columns here; it reads as a single, calm text block that can extend vertically as needed without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable block within the central column, expanding vertically with content while preserving consistent top and bottom margins relative to adjacent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-category-link is the first element of the product detail content, positioned at the top of the central column immediately below the global page header area and its horizontal rule. It is left-aligned, occupying only as much horizontal space as the text requires, and visually sits about 24–32px above the product-header hero row. The link is rendered in a modest font size with a subtle underline or arrow icon, making it visible but intentionally understated so it does not compete with the product title. The whitespace above and below is generous relative to its size, allowing it to serve as a quiet breadcrumb without cluttering the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline text link anchored at the top of the main content column; scrolls naturally with the page and remains in place, serving as a static navigation affordance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-form is placed in the right-hand column of the hero area, directly beneath the rating-display and availability-indicator stack. It aligns flush with the left edge of the right text column established by the product-header and spans roughly 70–80% of that column’s width, leaving a comfortable margin to the right edge for breathing room. A vertical gap of about 16–24px separates it from the element above. Inside the form, the quantity selector and primary button are arranged in a clean, linear fashion (either horizontally with the button on the right or vertically stacked), with sufficient internal padding (16–24px) to feel substantial but not bulky. The form’s vertical height is modest, and a similar 16–24px gap is preserved between its bottom edge and the cart-feedback-bar, keeping the CTA clearly delineated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form element within the right-hand column; participates in the normal document flow and adjusts width responsively with the right column, stacking internally on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-feedback-bar appears only after a cart-related action and is positioned directly below the add-to-cart-form within the same right-hand column, maintaining the shared left alignment and column width. It spans nearly the full width of this column (around 80–100% of the right column width), with a slim height and a clean rectangular shape. The top margin from the add-to-cart-form is about 16px, keeping the message visually connected to the action without feeling cramped. The bar uses a subtle but slightly more prominent visual treatment (for example, a thin border or soft background tint) while preserving the overall black-and-white, minimalist aesthetic. The text inside is left-aligned, with modest internal padding (12–16px) on all sides. It remains inline with the rest of the layout and does not overlay or float over other content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline status bar that appears and disappears in place below the add-to-cart-form; it scrolls with the page and does not adopt sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-indicator is placed in the right-hand column beneath the core product-header text block and the rating-display, but above the add-to-cart-form. It is left-aligned with the title and other right-column elements, occupying only the horizontal space needed for its label and any accompanying status icon or dot. There is a modest vertical gap (around 8–12px) above and below it, ensuring it is easy to notice without drawing more attention than the product title or the primary CTA. The indicator may use a small, discrete color accent (e.g., a green dot) consistent with the design’s restrained color use, but it remains visually light and aligned within the tight, vertical stack of right-side details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Small inline block within the right-hand text stack; it flows with the surrounding content and shifts position naturally in responsive layouts, remaining close to the add-to-cart form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rating-display sits in the right-hand column, directly below the primary product title and key summary text provided by the product-header, and above the availability-indicator. It is left-aligned with the title, occupying only as much width as needed for stars and rating text, and does not extend the full width of the column. A vertical gap of about 8–16px separates it from the product title above, creating a clear but compact grouping. Visually, it is understated: small icons (stars or dots) paired with concise text, rendered in the same monochrome palette or with very minimal color, so it supports but does not overshadow the product name or CTA. The element helps visually step the user down from the title toward the purchase controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline rating element within the right-hand text stack; remains in ordinary document flow, repositioning directly under the title when the layout collapses into a single column on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-subscribe-inline component appears near the bottom of the product detail page, directly below the product-description-section, separated by a clear vertical gap of roughly 48–64px to signal a new, secondary section. It is aligned to the same central content column and spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, mirroring the main column. The overall block is horizontally centered, with the form elements (email input and subscribe button) either arranged in a single horizontal row or stacked with minimal vertical spacing, depending on available width. There is generous internal padding (16–24px) and ample whitespace around the component, making the invitation feel calm and unobtrusive. A subtle horizontal rule or tonal shift may appear above this section to gently differentiate it from the product content without breaking the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width (within the central column) form at the bottom of the product detail content; it scrolls into view and does not stick or overlap other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-and-empty-state-handler replaces the entire main product content area when the product cannot be loaded or is unavailable. It is positioned in the same central column as the normal content and is horizontally centered. Vertically, it appears approximately in the upper-middle of the viewport (around 25–35% from the top), leaving generous whitespace above and below to maintain the calm, gallery-like feel. The component typically contains an icon or simple graphic, a short, bold message, and a brief explanatory line or suggested action. These elements are stacked vertically with moderate spacing (16–24px) and constrained to a narrow width for easy scanning. Existing components like the back-to-category-link may either be integrated into this handler or be visually echoed via a clear call-to-action link within the block. All other product-related components are hidden while this handler is active, ensuring the user’s attention is focused on the status message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Central, inline replacement for the main content within the central column; scrolls with the page if necessary but is designed to be fully visible without scrolling under normal viewport heights.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Page for reviewing shop items in the cart, adjusting quantities (e.g., ensuring each postcard has quantity 1), removing items, viewing totals, subscribing to the newsletter, and proceeding to checkout.

core_features: Load and display the current cart with all items, including product name, image, unit price, quantity, and line total using getCart.
Allow users to update the quantity of each cart item (including postcards) and immediately refresh cart items and totals via updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow users to remove individual items from the cart and update the cart display and totals using removeCartItem.
Display up-to-date order subtotal, total, and currency for the cart using data from getCart, updateCartItemQuantity, and removeCartItem.
Create a checkout order for the current cart when the user chooses to proceed, using createCheckoutOrderForCart, then navigate to checkout.html with the checkoutOrderId URL parameter.
Offer an optional inline newsletter subscription form that calls subscribeToNewsletter, so users can subscribe while reviewing their cart.
user_workflows:
Review cart contents:
1) User navigates to cart.html (via header navigation or a direct link like 'View cart' after adding items).
2) On page load, the cart-loader component calls getCart.
3) The cart-items-list and cart-totals-summary components bind to the returned cart, items, and totals.
4) If there are items, they are listed with quantities and totals; if not, the cart-empty-state component is shown.

Adjust quantities to ensure each postcard has quantity 1:
1) User identifies each postcard item in the cart-items-list component (e.g., by product name or image).
2) For an item with incorrect quantity, the user changes the quantity in the cart-item-quantity-editor (e.g., using a numeric input or stepper).
3) On quantity change, cart-item-quantity-editor calls updateCartItemQuantity with cartItemId and the new quantity.
4) On success, the response items and totals replace the current state in cart-items-list and cart-totals-summary.
5) The user repeats this for each postcard until all postcard quantities are set to 1.

Remove items from the cart:
1) In the cart-items-list, the user selects an item to remove and clicks the associated remove control.
2) cart-item-remove-control calls removeCartItem with the cartItemId.
3) On success, the updated items and totals are used to re-render the cart-items-list and cart-totals-summary.
4) If the last item is removed, cart-empty-state replaces the standard cart view.

Proceed to checkout for cart items:
1) After reviewing items and quantities, the user clicks the 'Proceed to Checkout' control in cart-actions-bar.
2) cart-actions-bar calls createCheckoutOrderForCart.
3) If the response indicates success, the component reads checkoutOrder.id from checkoutOrder and navigates to checkout.html?checkoutOrderId={checkoutOrder.id}.
4) If success is false, cart-actions-bar displays the returned message as an inline error and does not navigate.

Subscribe to the newsletter from the cart page:
1) In the newsletter-signup-form, the user enters an email address and selects at least one topic, and optionally interests and preferred format.
2) On submitting the form, newsletter-signup-form calls subscribeToNewsletter with email, topics, interests, and preferredFormat taken from the form.
3) If success is true, the component shows a non-blocking confirmation state (e.g., success message) while keeping the cart state unchanged.
4) If success is false, the component displays the returned message as a validation or error message without leaving the page.
interactions: Page load: When cart.html loads, cart-loader automatically calls getCart. The result populates shared cart state used by cart-items-list, cart-totals-summary, cart-actions-bar, and cart-empty-state.

Quantity change: When the user changes the quantity input for a cart item in cart-item-quantity-editor, onQuantityChange triggers updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, quantity). On success, shared cart state is replaced with the returned cart, items, and totals.

Remove item: When the user clicks the remove control in cart-item-remove-control, onClickRemove triggers removeCartItem(cartItemId). On success, shared cart state is updated with the returned cart, items, and totals, and the corresponding row disappears.

Proceed to checkout: When the user clicks the proceed button in cart-actions-bar, onClickProceed triggers createCheckoutOrderForCart(). On success, the page navigates to checkout.html?checkoutOrderId={checkoutOrder.id}. On failure, an inline error message is shown and no navigation occurs.

Newsletter subscribe: When the user submits the newsletter-signup-form, onSubmit triggers subscribeToNewsletter(email, topics, interests, preferredFormat). The component handles success or error using the returned success flag and message, without affecting cart data.

Responsive state changes: Any successful call to updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem causes cart-items-list, cart-totals-summary, cart-actions-bar, and cart-empty-state to re-evaluate whether the cart is empty and update their rendered state accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The cart.html page does not require any URL parameters to function; it always operates on the current user's cart via getCart. However, it reacts to certain optional URL parameters for contextual behavior:
- On initial load, if a URL parameter like updatedItemId is present (cart.html?updatedItemId={cartItemId}), cart-items-list can highlight or scroll to that specific cart item after getCart resolves. This parameter is read-only on load and does not trigger additional API calls.
- If a URL parameter like checkoutError is present (cart.html?checkoutError=1), cart-actions-bar can show a generic error banner indicating that a previous checkout attempt failed, without performing new API calls.
- When the user proceeds to checkout, navigation to checkout.html includes checkoutOrderId as a URL parameter (checkout.html?checkoutOrderId={checkoutOrder.id}), using the id returned from createCheckoutOrderForCart. cart.html itself does not persist any cart state via URL or localStorage; it always treats the API responses (getCart, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem) as the source of truth and updates the in-memory page state only.

components.functionality: On page load, fetches the current cart, items, and totals using getCart and initializes shared cart state for the page.
Displays all current items in the cart as a list or table, with product image, name, unit price, quantity editor, line total, and remove control for each item.
For a single cart item row, allows the user to change the quantity (e.g., to 1 for postcards), calls updateCartItemQuantity, and updates the shared cart state with the response.
Removes a specific item from the cart when clicked by calling removeCartItem and then updates the shared cart state.
Shows the current subtotal, total, and currency for the cart, updating whenever the cart changes.
Provides primary actions for the cart, including proceeding to checkout. Calls createCheckoutOrderForCart and navigates to checkout.html with the returned checkoutOrderId.
Displays an empty-cart message and hides item-specific controls when the cart contains no items.
Shows non-blocking error messages related to cart loading or checkout creation, including optional context from URL parameters like checkoutError.
Inline form that lets users subscribe to the gallery newsletter from the cart page by calling subscribeToNewsletter with selected topics, interests, and preferred format.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s strong top-down, centered structure. Above the cart (outside this spec) sit the logo, horizontal navigation, and a separating rule. The cart page title ('Cart') appears centered as a single, bold line, followed by another thin horizontal rule to mark the start of transactional content. Within the cart content area, components follow a linear vertical order inside a single centered column: error banner (if present), cart items list (or empty state), cart totals summary, cart actions bar, and finally the newsletter signup form. Within the cart items list, each item mirrors the exhibition block pattern: a rectangular artwork image on the left and a vertically stacked text-and-control column on the right.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s strong top-down, centered structure. Above the cart (outside this spec) sit the logo, horizontal navigation, and a separating rule. The cart page title ('Cart') appears centered as a single, bold line, followed by another thin horizontal rule to mark the start of transactional content. Within the cart content area, components follow a linear vertical order inside a single centered column: error banner (if present), cart items list (or empty state), cart totals summary, cart actions bar, and finally the newsletter signup form. Within the cart items list, each item mirrors the exhibition block pattern: a rectangular artwork image on the left and a vertically stacked text-and-control column on the right.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: All cart content is constrained to a central column occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with generous left and right margins acting as white framing. The top 20–25% of the viewport remains devoted to branding and navigation, and another ~10–15% for the centered 'Cart' heading plus surrounding whitespace. The main cart content (list, totals, actions, newsletter) fills the remaining vertical space with large gaps between sections (32–48px).
