- Step 1: User interacts with controls in the toolbar (e.g., selects a sort option, toggles family-friendly-only filter, or switches between compact and detailed views).
- Step 2: The page updates the URL parameters accordingly (e.g., ?sort=priority&familyFriendlyOnly=true&view=compact) using client-side navigation without a full page reload if desired.
- Step 3: The client re-applies sorting and filtering logic to the current getVisitPlan result and re-renders the list.
- Step 4: If the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, the page reads the updated URL parameters and reapplies the corresponding state.

Print or save the Visit Plan for offline use:
- Step 1: User clicks the 'Print / Save' button in the toolbar.
- Step 2: The page prepares a print-friendly view (e.g., ensures all items currently visible with applied filters are included in the DOM) and triggers the browser’s print dialog (e.g., via window.print in implementation).
- Step 3: User uses the browser-native options to print the plan or save it as a PDF for offline use.
- Step 4: After closing the dialog, the user remains on the My Visit Plan page with the same URL parameters and state.

Subscribe to the newsletter from the Visit Plan page:
- Step 1: User scrolls to the newsletter subscription form section.
- Step 2: User enters an email address and selects topics, interests, and preferred format according to the allowed NewsletterSubscription fields.
- Step 3: User submits the form; the page calls subscribeToNewsletter with the entered values.
- Step 4: On success, a confirmation message is displayed (via notification component), and optionally the URL is updated (e.g., newsletterStatus=success).
- Step 5: On error, an error message is displayed with guidance to correct inputs and resubmit; the URL can optionally reflect this state (e.g., newsletterStatus=error).

Browse more exhibitions to add to the Visit Plan:
- Step 1: User clicks the 'Browse more exhibitions' link either from the toolbar, the empty-state component, or the bottom of the list.
- Step 2: The page navigates to exhibitions.html (no required parameters).
- Step 3: From exhibitions.html, the user can add more items to their visit plan (via functionality defined on that page) and later return to visit_plan.html to see updates.
interactions: On initial page load, the visit-plan-data-loader component calls getVisitPlan and handles loading and error states before rendering the main content.
Clicking on an exhibition row (excluding its remove button) triggers navigation to exhibition_detail.html with exhibitionId set from the corresponding plan item.
Clicking the 'Remove' button on a visit plan item triggers removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan with that item's visitPlanItemId and, on success, removes it from the list and updates the summary.
Changing the sort selection in the toolbar (e.g., to sort by priority, title, or date range text) updates the sort URL parameter and reorders the list client-side.
Toggling a 'Family-friendly only' filter checkbox updates the familyFriendlyOnly URL parameter and filters the rendered list to only items where isFamilyFriendly is true.
Switching between compact and detailed view modes updates the view URL parameter and changes whether fields like subtitle, notes, and admissionLabel are shown in each list item.
Clicking the 'Print / Save' button triggers the browser print dialog so the user can print or save a PDF of the current (filtered/sorted) list.
Clicking the 'Browse more exhibitions' link navigates the user to exhibitions.html.
Submitting the newsletter subscription form triggers subscribeToNewsletter with email, topics, interests, and preferredFormat based on user input.
On successful newsletter subscription, a success notification is displayed and may be reflected in the URL; on failure, an error notification is displayed with no change to getVisitPlan data.
If getVisitPlan returns an empty items array, the page hides the list and toolbar components and shows the empty-visit-plan-state component with a link to exhibitions.html.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The My Visit Plan page uses URL parameters to control view configuration, filter options, and some transient feedback states, without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms.

URL parameters and their effects:
- sort: Controls client-side sorting of visit plan items after getVisitPlan has loaded. Supported values can include 'priority', 'title', or other options defined in the implementation. If absent, a default sort order (e.g., by addedAt or title) is used in the client.
- familyFriendlyOnly: Boolean-like parameter (e.g., 'true' or 'false') that determines whether the list only displays items where isFamilyFriendly is true. When the parameter is missing or set to a false-equivalent value, all items are shown.
- view: Controls view density of the list (for example, 'compact' may show only title and dateRangeText, while 'detailed' also shows subtitle, admissionLabel, priority, and notes). If absent, a default view mode is applied.
- planMessage (optional): Used to reflect actions taken on the visit plan (e.g., 'removed' after a successful removal) so that on a reload, the notification-banner can display a corresponding message. The value is read on load and then interpreted by the client.
- newsletterStatus (optional): Used to reflect the result of a newsletter subscription attempt ('success' or 'error') so that the notification-banner can present consistent feedback, even across a page reload.

Lifecycle behavior:
- On page load, the page reads the current URL's query parameters and derives its initial UI state: sorting, filtering, view mode, and any feedback messages.
- After determining UI state, the page invokes getVisitPlan (which does not accept any parameters), and stores the resulting visitPlan and items in memory.
- Once items are available, the client applies the sort, filter, and view rules derived from URL parameters and renders the list. All further changes to sort, filter, or view are applied to this in-memory representation without additional calls to getVisitPlan.
- When the user adjusts toolbar controls (sort, filter, view), the page updates the appropriate URL parameters (using query string updates) and immediately re-applies the new state to the loaded items. The updated URL ensures that using the browser back/forward buttons restores the previous view state.
- When the user removes an item using removeExhibitionFromVisitPlan, the operation does not depend on URL parameters. After a successful response, the client updates the in-memory list, optionally updates a feedback-related URL parameter (e.g., planMessage), and re-renders the list. The total item count may also be reflected in the URL if desired (e.g., planCount), but the canonical source of truth remains the API.
- Submitting the newsletter subscription form via subscribeToNewsletter does not change the visit plan data; however, on success or failure, the client may update newsletterStatus (and potentially a short message) as URL parameters to represent the outcome, then display these via the notification-banner component.
- Navigation to exhibition_detail.html always uses an exhibitionId URL parameter, and navigation to exhibitions.html does not require specific parameters from this page.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading URL parameters, calling getVisitPlan, and distributing the loaded visitPlan and items to child components along with derived state (sorting, filtering, view mode, and message flags). Handles loading and error states.
Displays visit plan-level information such as the visit plan name (if provided by getVisitPlan) and the total count of items currently loaded. Updates automatically when items are removed.

Provides controls to sort visit plan items, toggle a family-friendly-only filter, switch between compact and detailed views, invoke print/save, and navigate to browse more exhibitions. Updates URL parameters when controls change.

Renders the list of visit plan items using the current sorting, filtering, and view mode. Delegates item-level actions (open details, remove) to visit-plan-item-row. If, after applying filters, no items remain, signals the empty-visit-plan-state to display instead.

Represents a single visit plan item row, showing dynamic fields like title, subtitle, dateRangeText, admissionLabel, family-friendly indicator, priority, notes, and imageUrl depending on current view mode. Handles navigation to exhibition details when the main row is clicked and removal of the item from the plan when the remove action is clicked.

Displays when getVisitPlan returns no items or when all items have been removed. Provides explanatory text and a prominent link to exhibitions.html so the user can start adding exhibitions to their visit plan.

Shows transient success or error messages related to operations on this page, such as successful removal of an exhibition from the visit plan or newsletter subscription results. Reads optional state from URL parameters so notifications can persist across reloads if desired.

Collects user input for email, topics, interests, and preferredFormat, and submits these values to subscribeToNewsletter. On success, triggers a success notification and updates newsletterStatus in the URL. On error, shows validation or server error feedback and can set newsletterStatus accordingly.

Encapsulates the logic to prepare the current list view (respecting sort, filter, and view mode) for printing and then invoke the browser’s print dialog, enabling users to print or save their visit plan.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page continues the mockup's linear, top-to-bottom flow. Global elements (logo, navigation, page title rule) remain as in the existing site. Below that, the 'My Visit Plan' area is treated as a centered single-column content region. Inside this region, we follow a clear sequence: a summary header introducing the visit plan, an optional notification banner just below the header, then a horizontal toolbar for managing the list, followed by the visit plan list itself. Each saved exhibition in the list uses the same two-column pattern as the mockup: artwork image on the left, text and controls on the right. If there are no items, a centered empty state replaces the list. At the bottom of the content column, a newsletter signup form appears in a single centered column. Utility components like the data loader and print/save handler sit on top of or behind this flow, not disrupting the core layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page continues the mockup's linear, top-to-bottom flow. Global elements (logo, navigation, page title rule) remain as in the existing site. Below that, the 'My Visit Plan' area is treated as a centered single-column content region. Inside this region, we follow a clear sequence: a summary header introducing the visit plan, an optional notification banner just below the header, then a horizontal toolbar for managing the list, followed by the visit plan list itself. Each saved exhibition in the list uses the same two-column pattern as the mockup: artwork image on the left, text and controls on the right. If there are no items, a centered empty state replaces the list. At the bottom of the content column, a newsletter signup form appears in a single centered column. Utility components like the data loader and print/save handler sit on top of or behind this flow, not disrupting the core layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column for the 'My Visit Plan' page spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered horizontally with generous left and right margins acting as white framing space. The upper 20–25% of the viewport remains dedicated to the site’s global branding and navigation. Immediately under that, the visit plan summary header plus any notification banner occupy another ~10–15% of initial viewport height, with ample spacing above and below. The toolbar sits relatively compactly but with enough vertical breathing room (24–32px) above and below. The visit plan list occupies the bulk of the remaining vertical space in the central column. Each visit-plan item row is a horizontal block: about 35–40% of the row width is reserved for the artwork image on the left and 60–65% for text and controls on the right. The newsletter form at the bottom again uses a centered, single-column layout at about 60% page width.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and editorial. Each major section (header, toolbar, list, newsletter) is separated by 32–48px of vertical whitespace. Within components, internal padding is generally in the 16–24px range. Text line lengths are kept moderate by the constrained central column, and descriptions inside item rows are limited to a few lines before truncation or wrap. Controls in the toolbar and within list items are spaced apart with generous breathing room, avoiding crowded clusters. The empty state emphasizes open space with a single illustration or icon, a headline, one short paragraph, and a primary CTA.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye naturally moves from the global brand and navigation at the top down into the 'My Visit Plan' summary header, which is centered and clearly labeled. If present, a notification banner directly under the header is the next visual stop, followed by the horizontally arranged toolbar. From the toolbar, attention drops into the first visit plan item row. Within each row, the colorful artwork image on the left attracts the eye first, then the gaze shifts right to scan the exhibition title, institution, date, and any visit-specific details and controls. The vertical stack of item rows creates a steady reading rhythm down the page. At the bottom of this flow, the newsletter signup form provides a soft, secondary call to action, aligned with the central column. Overlays like the data loader temporarily sit above everything, dimming the content but not permanently changing the visual hierarchy.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing site logo and navigation, the 'My Visit Plan' page content sits in a centered column that occupies about two-thirds of the viewport width, framed by wide white margins on the left and right, echoing a clean gallery wall. A thin horizontal rule from the global layout visually separates the global chrome from the page content. The visit plan summary header appears first within this central column, with the title ‘My Visit Plan’ and supporting copy centered and surrounded by ample vertical whitespace, creating a calm, editorial opening. Directly beneath, any notification banner spans the same column width as a shallow, subtle strip, followed by a horizontally arranged toolbar that allows users to sort, filter, and print or save their plan. Below the toolbar, a generous gap leads into the main visit plan list: stacked horizontal rows where each exhibition shows a rectangular artwork image on the left and a right-hand column of text and visit controls. Each row is well-padded and separated from the next by clear vertical spacing, giving each exhibition the feeling of an individual gallery label beside a framed work. If the plan is empty, this section is replaced by a centered empty state message and a primary action. At the very bottom of the column, a newsletter signup form reverts to a single-column layout with centered text and fields, maintaining the same width and whitespace treatment as the rest of the page. Functional utilities like data loading and printing appear as subtle overlays or icon controls integrated into this structure, never disturbing the strong top-down flow and minimal, black-and-white aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is an overlay that appears on top of the main content column for the 'My Visit Plan' page, without affecting the placement of other components. When active, it covers the central content area from just below the global navigation / page-level rule down to slightly above the page footer. Horizontally, it matches the width of the main content column (approximately 60–70% of the viewport), centered on the page, with a semi-transparent white or light-gray background that subtly mutes underlying content while keeping the minimalist, gallery-like feel. A small, clean loading indicator (e.g., a simple spinner and short 'Loading your visit plan...' line) is centered both vertically and horizontally within this overlay. The overlay has internal padding of around 24px on all sides so that the indicator never touches the edges. When hidden, this component occupies no visible space and does not shift any other elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a temporary, centered overlay over the main content column; not fixed to the viewport edges, but positioned in front of existing components and scrolls with the page if the user scrolls while loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary header is the first visible component within the 'My Visit Plan' content area. It is centered horizontally within the main content column, which spans about 60–70% of the viewport width. The top edge of the summary header sits approximately 32–48px below the horizontal rule that separates the global navigation from the page content, preserving the generous, gallery-like whitespace. The header includes the main page title ‘My Visit Plan’ as a bold, black, centered line followed by a short, lighter-weight description text that spans no more than 60% of the content column width, also centered. Internal vertical padding of 24–32px ensures space above the title and below the description. The overall block presents as a clean, editorial hero text block with ample white space above and below, visually anchoring the page before any functional controls or list items appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element in the normal document flow; does not stick or float during scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The toolbar sits directly below the summary header, aligned to the same central column. There is a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px between the bottom of the summary header and the top of the toolbar, creating a clear separation between introduction and controls. The toolbar itself is a low-profile horizontal strip spanning nearly the full width of the content column (about 60–70% of viewport), with internal padding of around 16px on all sides. On the left side of the toolbar, filters or sorting controls are arranged in a row, aligned to the left edge of the column. On the right side, the print/save actions (powered by the print-save-handler) are displayed as minimalist text buttons or simple icon buttons aligned to the right edge. The middle area remains lightly populated or empty, reinforcing the sparse aesthetic. The height of the toolbar is modest—roughly 56–72px depending on control size—ensuring it feels like a subtle control bar rather than a heavy panel. The toolbar visually reads just above the list as an interface band separating the descriptive header from the list of exhibitions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily static in the normal document flow. Optionally, on long lists, it can become sticky within the main content column (sticking to the top of the column just below the global header) to keep filters and print/save actions accessible while scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit plan list occupies the central portion of the page beneath the toolbar. It is vertically separated from the toolbar by about 32px of whitespace to emphasize the shift from controls to content. The list is constrained to the same main content column width (60–70% of viewport), centered with large left and right margins. Inside this area, the list is a vertical stack of visit-plan item rows, each row appearing as a horizontal card-like block that stretches the full width of the column. The list itself does not have a visible border; instead, the structure is defined by consistent vertical spacing of 24–32px between each item row and by each row’s internal padding. The list’s top and bottom feel airy, with at least 32–48px of space between the first item and the toolbar above, and between the last item and the newsletter signup form or page bottom below. This creates a calm scrolling rhythm where each exhibition feels like its own unit in a curated sequence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack in the normal document flow; scrolls naturally with the page. It conditionally renders either item rows or is replaced entirely by the empty state when there are no items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each visit plan item row is a horizontal, two-column block that mirrors the exhibition layout from the mockup. The row spans the full width of the main content column and is centered with the rest of the content. The block has internal padding of approximately 16–24px on all sides and is visually separated from neighboring rows by 24–32px of vertical space. Inside the row, the left column occupies about 35–40% of the row width and contains a rectangular artwork image with a fixed aspect ratio (e.g., 3:2 or 4:3), aligned flush to the left. The image fills its column’s width, with either a small 8–16px margin around it or a tight fit with the row padding, depending on the final aesthetic. The right column occupies the remaining 60–65% of the row width and is aligned to the right edge of the main column. Within this right column, content is stacked vertically: the exhibition title appears at the top in bold, followed immediately by the institution name and date range in a smaller, lighter-weight type. Below that, a short snippet of descriptive copy spans a moderate line length, limited to a few lines for readability. Any visit plan-specific interactions (e.g., time slot, priority, remove from plan) are placed either in a small horizontal row at the bottom or aligned to the top-right corner, depending on hierarchy, but spaced so the text never feels cramped. The typography remains black and gray on white, with color coming only from the artwork image, maintaining the minimalist, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static item within the vertical list; no sticky behavior. Hover states or subtle focus outlines can be used for interactivity but do not alter overall layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty visit plan state occupies the same vertical position and width as the visit plan list would. When active, it replaces the list entirely within the main content column beneath the toolbar. There is a 32–48px gap between the bottom of the toolbar and the top of the empty state, reinforcing it as a distinct section. The content is centered horizontally within the column, and the overall block is vertically generous, using at least 64px of top and bottom padding so that it feels spacious rather than cramped. At the center, a simple icon or line illustration aligns with the minimalist black-and-white visual language (with perhaps a very subtle accent color if consistent with the overall brand). Below the icon, a headline like ‘Your visit plan is empty’ appears, centered and bold, followed by one concise line of explanatory text at a lighter weight. A primary CTA button (e.g., ‘Browse exhibitions’) is centered beneath the text with 16–24px of vertical spacing. The empty state reads as a calm, gallery-like message in the middle of the white space, encouraging exploration without visual noise.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that conditionally takes the place of the visit plan list in the normal flow. It does not overlay other content; it simply appears where the list would normally begin.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification banner sits within the main content column, directly below the summary header and above the toolbar. It aligns to the same centered column, spanning its full width. The top of the banner is separated from the bottom of the summary header by about 16–24px, keeping the hierarchy clear without feeling crowded. The banner itself is a relatively shallow horizontal strip with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding, containing a short line of text and an optional close icon aligned to the right. The background remains mostly white, with a subtle border or a very light gray tint to distinguish it from the surrounding content while keeping the overall aesthetic minimal. The text is left-aligned within the column, and any iconography is small and understated. If multiple notifications are needed, they are stacked with 8–12px between them, but typically only one is visible at a time to preserve the clean layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline component in the normal flow. It appears and disappears without shifting the position of elements above it, but will push down content below when present. It does not stick or float during scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter signup form is placed near the bottom of the main content column, following the visit plan list or empty state. There is a generous vertical gap of approximately 48–64px between the end of the list (or empty state) and the top of the newsletter section to signal a transition from task-focused content to a softer, secondary engagement. The form is centered within the column and constrained to about 50–60% of the column width, resulting in a relatively narrow, editorial-looking block in the middle of the page. At the top, a short heading (e.g., ‘Stay updated’) is left-aligned or centered within this narrower block, followed by a brief line of descriptive text. The email input field and submit button are arranged either in a single row (input on the left, button on the right) or in a small vertical stack, depending on responsiveness, but they never stretch edge-to-edge; instead, they align with the inner text width, maintaining generous margins within the column. Internal padding of 24–32px around the form content reinforces the airy aesthetic. Overall, the section feels like a discreet, well-spaced footer module that does not compete with the visit plan content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the bottom of the main content area; scrolls with the page and does not stick or overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The print-save handler has no independent visible container; instead, its controls manifest as part of the visit-plan toolbar. Visually, it appears as one or two minimalist button elements on the right side of the toolbar: for example, a ‘Print Plan’ text button and a simple icon button for saving/sharing. These buttons align vertically within the toolbar’s height and horizontally with the toolbar’s right edge, positioned in a small horizontal row with 8–16px spacing between them. Their styling follows the site’s black-and-white, minimalist aesthetic—simple outlines or underlined text, no heavy fills—so they integrate seamlessly with the toolbar without drawing undue attention. When activated, print or save actions may briefly trigger overlays (such as a system print dialog or a subtle confirmation toast positioned at the top of the content column), but the handler itself does not change the layout of surrounding components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual utility component whose primary spatial behavior is to anchor its controls to the right side of the toolbar. It does not introduce new layout elements outside the toolbar and does not create persistent overlays.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Unified checkout page where visitors review their order (tickets, tours, memberships, or shop items), provide billing details, choose a payment method if required, complete payment, and optionally subscribe to the newsletter.

core_features: Load and display the checkout order using the checkoutOrderId URL parameter via getCheckoutOrder, including order status, item lines, totals, and allowed payment methods.
Show a structured order summary with line items, quantities, prices, and totals so users can verify what they are purchasing or booking.
Provide contextual links back to the originating flow (tickets, tour detail, cart, membership) using the CheckoutOrder.orderType and referenceId for making changes before payment.
Collect billing details (billingFullName and billingEmail) required for confirmation and receipts.
Display the appropriate payment options based on getCheckoutOrder.allowedPaymentMethods and requiresPayment, including pay at gallery / no payment required paths.
Collect card details (cardNumber, cardExpiry, cardCvc) only when an online card payment is required and selected by the user.
Submit the checkout via completeCheckoutPayment, handle success and error responses, and update the page state based on the returned CheckoutOrder.status.
Display a confirmation state after successful completion, including order summary and navigation back to the home page (index.html).
Offer an optional newsletter subscription form that can reuse the billing email and submit preferences via subscribeToNewsletter.
user_workflows: Paid online order (any type: tickets, tours, shop_cart, membership_enrollment): 1) User lands on checkout.html with ?checkoutOrderId=...; 2) Page calls getCheckoutOrder; 3) Order summary and totals are shown; 4) User reviews details or uses contextual 'Change' links to go back to the source page if needed; 5) User enters billingFullName and billingEmail; 6) User selects a paymentMethod from allowedPaymentMethods (credit_debit_card included); 7) User enters cardNumber, cardExpiry, and cardCvc; 8) User clicks 'Complete Purchase'; 9) Page calls completeCheckoutPayment with all fields; 10) On success, page shows confirmation, disables payment form, and offers a 'Back to Home' link to index.html; 11) User can optionally submit newsletter preferences via subscribeToNewsletter.

Order with pay at gallery option: 1) User lands on checkout.html with ?checkoutOrderId=...; 2) Page calls getCheckoutOrder; 3) Order summary is shown and requiresPayment is true but allowedPaymentMethods includes pay_at_gallery_on_arrival; 4) User enters billingFullName and billingEmail; 5) User selects pay_at_gallery_on_arrival; 6) Card fields remain hidden and empty; 7) User clicks 'Complete Reservation'; 8) Page calls completeCheckoutPayment with card fields left empty; 9) On success, page shows a confirmation message indicating payment will be made on arrival, and the updated CheckoutOrder is reflected; 10) Optional newsletter subscription via subscribeToNewsletter.

Order requiring no payment (e.g., free events, fully discounted membership, or zero-total cart): 1) User lands on checkout.html with ?checkoutOrderId=...; 2) getCheckoutOrder returns requiresPayment=false and allowedPaymentMethods including no_payment_required; 3) Order summary and total (zero or fully covered) are shown; 4) BillingFullName and billingEmail are collected for confirmations; 5) Payment method may be auto-selected as no_payment_required and payment UI is minimized; 6) User clicks 'Confirm Booking'; 7) Page calls completeCheckoutPayment with no card data; 8) On success, page shows confirmation and an option to go back to index.html; 9) Optional newsletter subscription via subscribeToNewsletter.

Handling invalid or missing checkoutOrderId: 1) User lands on checkout.html without checkoutOrderId or with an invalid value; 2) Page detects missing/invalid parameter and either skips API call or handles an error from getCheckoutOrder; 3) An error state is shown explaining that the checkout session could not be found or has expired; 4) A link or button navigates the user back to index.html to start a new flow.
interactions: On page init (load or URL change with checkoutOrderId): The page reads the checkoutOrderId query parameter; if present, calls getCheckoutOrder(checkoutOrderId) and enters a loading state until the response arrives or fails.

Order summary interactions: Users can expand/collapse item details (if supported by itemsSummary descriptions), and use 'Change' links that navigate back to the originating page based on orderType and referenceId (e.g., tickets.html?bookingId=<referenceId>, tour_detail.html?tourBookingId=<referenceId>, cart.html?cartId=<referenceId>, membership.html?enrollmentId=<referenceId>).

Billing form interactions: Users type into billingFullName and billingEmail fields; validation runs on blur and/or before submit (e.g., required, email format), and error messages are shown inline without submitting to completeCheckoutPayment.

Payment method selection: When getCheckoutOrder returns allowedPaymentMethods, each method is rendered as a selectable option; on selection change, the chosen paymentMethod is stored in local component state and controls visibility and requiredness of card fields.

Card details interactions: If the selected paymentMethod is credit_debit_card and requiresPayment is true, cardNumber, cardExpiry, and cardCvc fields become visible and required; user input is validated locally (basic format checks) before allowing submission to completeCheckoutPayment.

Submit interaction: Clicking the primary submit button triggers form validation; if valid, the button is disabled, a submitting state is shown, and completeCheckoutPayment is called with checkoutOrderId, billingFullName, billingEmail, paymentMethod, and card fields (card fields are empty or omitted for non-card methods); the response is used to update the page (success confirmation or error).

Error handling: If completeCheckoutPayment returns success=false or an error message, an error banner is displayed, card and billing fields remain editable, and the submit button is re-enabled; users can correct inputs and retry.

Post-success navigation: When completeCheckoutPayment returns success=true and the returned checkoutOrder.status indicates completion (e.g., paid or pending_payment as appropriate), the payment form is locked to read-only state, and a 'Back to Home' or 'Continue browsing' button becomes the primary action, navigating to index.html without extra parameters.

Newsletter subscription interaction: After successful checkout (and optionally also while in progress), users can opt in to a newsletter by confirming or editing their email, choosing topics, interests, and format, and submitting; on submit, subscribeToNewsletter is called and the result determines whether a success or error message is shown; this interaction is independent of the payment flow.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven primarily by the checkoutOrderId URL parameter and the data returned by getCheckoutOrder. On every initial load, the page reads checkout.html?checkoutOrderId=<id>. If checkoutOrderId is missing or empty, the page does not call getCheckoutOrder and instead shows a 'missing checkout' error state and a link to index.html. If checkoutOrderId is present, the page calls getCheckoutOrder(checkoutOrderId) and stores the returned checkoutOrder object, itemsSummary array, requiresPayment flag, and allowedPaymentMethods list in memory for the current view. The orderType and referenceId in checkoutOrder determine which 'Change' link URL is built for editing the underlying booking or cart (using different target pages and query parameters according to orderType). The checkoutOrder.status value determines whether the page is in an editable payment state (in_progress or pending_payment) or in a completed/locked state (paid or cancelled). When the user submits payment via completeCheckoutPayment, the response's checkoutOrder replaces the previous in-memory checkoutOrder, which may change status to paid or cancelled and may also update status-related UI. The selected paymentMethod is initialized from checkoutOrder.paymentMethod if available and valid within allowedPaymentMethods; otherwise, it is defaulted to the first entry of allowedPaymentMethods and stored in local state. Card details are only collected and sent when the selected paymentMethod is credit_debit_card and requiresPayment is true; for other methods, cardNumber, cardExpiry, and cardCvc are sent as empty values. Newsletter subscription state is independent of checkoutOrder state: the newsletter form can prefill the email field from billingEmail once available, but the actual subscription is created only when subscribeToNewsletter is called and returns success. All navigation out of this page uses standard URL-based routing with query parameters, without relying on localStorage; reloading the page with the same checkoutOrderId re-fetches the current order via getCheckoutOrder.

components.functionality: Reads the checkoutOrderId from the URL, validates its presence, invokes getCheckoutOrder to retrieve the checkout order, and manages loading, success, and error states for the rest of the page.

Displays appropriate messaging and navigation options when checkoutOrderId is missing from the URL or when getCheckoutOrder fails (e.g., invalid ID, expired order). Provides navigation back to index.html.

Shows a structured summary of the order: itemsSummary line items, quantities, unit prices, line totals, and order-level totals (totalAmount, currency), plus contextual meta like orderType and status. Also provides 'Change' links that route users back to the appropriate source page for editing the underlying booking or cart using CheckoutOrder.referenceId.

Collects billingFullName and billingEmail from the user for use in completeCheckoutPayment and for email confirmations. Handles validation (required fields, email format) and exposes validity state to the parent checkout form component.

Displays available payment methods returned by getCheckoutOrder.allowedPaymentMethods (such as credit_debit_card, pay_at_gallery_on_arrival, no_payment_required), allows the user to select one, and stores the selection for use in completeCheckoutPayment. Controls the visibility of card detail fields and the submit button label based on the selected method and requiresPayment.

Collects cardNumber, cardExpiry, and cardCvc when the selected payment method is credit_debit_card and requiresPayment is true. Performs basic client-side validation (non-empty, simple format checks) before allowing submission via completeCheckoutPayment.

Coordinates validation of billing and payment details and triggers the final checkout action. When the user clicks the submit button, it validates all sub-forms (billing, payment method, card details as applicable) and, if valid, calls completeCheckoutPayment with checkoutOrderId, billingFullName, billingEmail, paymentMethod, cardNumber, cardExpiry, and cardCvc. It manages submitting state, handles the API response, updates the local CheckoutOrder state, and notifies other components (such as the confirmation panel or error banner).

Displays global status messages and errors during checkout, such as API errors from getCheckoutOrder or completeCheckoutPayment, or validation failures. Updates dynamically based on events from loader and submit controller.

Shows a confirmation view once completeCheckoutPayment succeeds and the returned CheckoutOrder.status indicates the order is finalized (e.g., paid or ready for pay-at-gallery). Renders a read-only summary of items, totals, payment method, and contact email, and provides a primary button that navigates back to index.html and secondary options such as printing the summary if required by the broader site.

Optional section that allows users to subscribe to the gallery newsletter after or during checkout. Prefills the email field from billingEmail when available, lets users choose topics, interests, and preferredFormat, submits via subscribeToNewsletter, and displays subscription success or error feedback.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s structure: a site-wide logo and navigation in a centered single column at the top, followed by a centered page title (“Checkout”) and a separating rule. Below that, the checkout content sits inside a fixed-width, centered container. Within this container, the main body uses a two-column pattern: a narrower left column (about 35–40% of the content width) for the order summary, and a wider right column (about 60–65%) for billing, payment, and submission. Global/ephemeral states (loader, error banner, confirmation) sit in a centered, single-column layer that visually overlays or replaces the two-column area when active, preserving the strong vertical hierarchy from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s structure: a site-wide logo and navigation in a centered single column at the top, followed by a centered page title (“Checkout”) and a separating rule. Below that, the checkout content sits inside a fixed-width, centered container. Within this container, the main body uses a two-column pattern: a narrower left column (about 35–40% of the content width) for the order summary, and a wider right column (about 60–65%) for billing, payment, and submission. Global/ephemeral states (loader, error banner, confirmation) sit in a centered, single-column layer that visually overlays or replaces the two-column area when active, preserving the strong vertical hierarchy from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper 20–25% of the viewport is reserved for logo, navigation, and the Checkout heading with generous top/bottom padding and a rule separating each major band. The main checkout container spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered, with large white margins left and right. Inside this container, the left order-summary column takes about 35–40% of the width, the right form column about 60–65%. Vertical spacing between major groups (summary vs billing vs payment vs confirmation/newsletter) follows the 32–48px rhythm, while internal padding within each card-like block is in the 16–24px range.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains low and editorial: form sections are broken into clear blocks with one main idea per block (order summary, billing, payment method, card details, submit, newsletter). Fields are stacked in a single column or simple two-up pairs at most, with ample vertical spacing (16–24px) between rows. Text lines remain moderate in width thanks to the centered container and two-column structure, and there is purposeful whitespace between each component to maintain a calm, gallery-like checkout rather than a crowded ecommerce feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye travels top-down: logo, nav, horizontal rule, then the centered Checkout heading and another rule. Immediately below, any status or error banner appears as a full-width band within the content container, acting as a visual stop. From there, attention drops into the two-column area: the colorful or visually dense order summary block on the left anchors the content, while the main reading path continues down the right column through billing details, payment selection, card fields, and the primary submit control. After submission, the confirmation panel becomes the main focal point in the same vertical flow, with an optional newsletter opt-in at the end as a softer, secondary call to action.

layout.overall_description: The Checkout page sits within the same minimalist, gallery-like frame as the exhibitions listing. At the very top, the logo is centered with generous vertical padding; directly beneath, a thin row of navigation links runs horizontally, also centered within the same max-width content area. A fine horizontal rule separates this global chrome from the page-specific content. The word “Checkout” then appears as a bold, centered heading with ample whitespace above and below, followed by a second rule that visually introduces the transactional space. The main checkout area is a centered container occupying roughly two-thirds of the viewport width, framed by wide white margins on both sides. At the top of this container, any status or error banner stretches across the full content width as a clean, low-profile bar. Below that, the layout resolves into two columns: on the left, a solid, card-like order summary occupying about 35–40% of the container; on the right, a vertically stacked flow of billing form, payment method selector, card details, and the primary submit control in a 60–65% width column. Each section is separated by generous vertical spacing, with internal padding and simple typographic hierarchy instead of heavy decoration. On successful completion, the right-column form stack gives way to a centered confirmation panel that feels like another editorial block in the same grid, while a softly styled newsletter opt-in sits below as an optional finishing touch. The overall effect is a calm, structured, and visually balanced checkout that feels like part of a curated gallery experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order loader appears as an overlay centered within the main checkout content area, aligned with the same max-width container as the two-column layout. When active, a subtle semi-transparent white veil covers both left and right columns, slightly dimming the order summary and forms underneath while keeping the global header and navigation unchanged. In the middle of this content container, a compact, square or circular loading indicator is centered both horizontally and vertically within a minimalist panel roughly 280–320px wide, with 24px internal padding and a short status line below the spinner. This panel respects the gallery aesthetic with a thin border or soft shadow and plenty of whitespace around the spinner. The loader does not stretch full screen; it remains visually tethered to the central column, reinforcing that it is loading just the checkout content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily fixed within the bounds of the central checkout container, overlaying the two-column content with a translucent mask and centered loader panel when active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The missing-or-error state replaces the main two-column content within the central checkout container. It appears as a single, centered panel spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width, aligned with the same grid as the order summary and forms. The panel is positioned directly below the Checkout heading and its separating rule, with 32–48px of vertical space above and below it. Inside, the error title is centered, followed by a short explanatory message and any action buttons arranged in a vertical stack. Content is kept within a 480–640px width, even on large screens, to maintain a compact, legible block floating in ample white space. No side-by-side columns are visible in this state; instead, the panel stands alone in the center to clearly indicate that the order could not be loaded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll flow, but conditionally replaces the standard two-column checkout content when an unrecoverable error or missing order is detected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order summary sits in the left column of the main checkout container, beginning just below the status/error banner (if any). It occupies roughly 35–40% of the container width and extends vertically as needed to list line items, totals, and key details. The top edge of the summary aligns horizontally with the top of the billing form on the right column, creating a clean, shared baseline. Internally, the summary uses a card-like block with 16–24px padding on all sides, a simple heading near the top, and a vertical list of items with clear line spacing. The right edge of this left column maintains a 24–32px gutter from the right column, reinforcing separation while staying visually connected. On taller pages, the order summary scrolls with the rest of the content and retains generous white space above and below so it reads like a curated information block rather than a crowded receipt.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column of the main checkout area, aligning its top with the billing form and maintaining a fixed proportion (about 35–40%) of the content width regardless of viewport size.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing form anchors the top of the right column in the main checkout container, directly opposite the order summary. It occupies the full width of the right column (about 60–65% of the container width) and is positioned with its top edge aligned to the top of the order summary block, with a consistent 24–32px gutter separating the two columns. The form content is arranged in a single vertical stack, with individual fields running nearly full column width and grouped logically (name, contact, address) with 16–24px of vertical spacing between groups. A clear section heading appears at the top, left-aligned within the column, followed by fields with labels above inputs to keep the layout clean and airy. The bottom of the billing form is separated by 32px of vertical space from the next section (payment method selector), creating distinct bands of content similar to the exhibition rows in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, forming the first large block in the vertical flow of forms and expanding vertically as needed while maintaining fixed lateral alignment with the order summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment method selector appears directly below the billing form in the right column, separated from it by approximately 32px of vertical whitespace to echo the mockup’s section spacing. It spans the full width of the right column and reads as a concise, card-like block with 16–24px internal padding. The section title is left-aligned at the top, with selection options (e.g., radio buttons or pill-style choices) arranged horizontally if there are few options or in a compact vertical list if more, but always confined to the right column’s moderate width. The selector maintains the same left edge alignment as the billing form and card details form below it, creating a clean vertical spine. The height remains modest, preserving the low-density feel and allowing the eye to quickly move down to the card details section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, occupying a thin horizontal band between the billing form and card details, and adjusting its height minimally based on the number of payment options.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The card details form follows immediately under the payment method selector in the right column, with about 24–32px of whitespace separating the two blocks. It occupies the same width as the billing form (full right column) and aligns to the same left and right edges to preserve the strong column structure. Inside, card number, expiration, and CVC fields are arranged in a simple vertical flow, with occasional two-up rows (e.g., month/year side-by-side) only if they comfortably fit within the column while maintaining generous spacing. The card details block uses 16–24px internal padding and a clear section label near the top, but the overall height is kept as compact as clarity allows, so it reads as one continuous step before the final submit control. The bottom of this block is separated from the submit controller by 32px of vertical space, marking the last informational step before action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, sitting directly in the vertical flow between payment method selection and the submit controller and expanding vertically only as needed for required fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit controller sits at the bottom of the active form stack in the right column, directly beneath the card details form and separated from it by approximately 32px of whitespace. It spans the full width of the right column but centers the primary action button within that width or aligns it slightly left, depending on the site’s button convention, while leaving generous empty space around it. Any secondary text (e.g., legal copy or note about security) is placed immediately below the button in a subdued typographic style, with 8–16px of vertical spacing. The block overall is relatively shallow, ensuring the call-to-action stands out as the final element in the right-column vertical journey. The bottom edge of this section leaves an additional 48px or so of whitespace before the end of the main checkout container or before the newsletter opt-in, emphasizing the importance of this action point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the end of the right-column form flow, acting as the final scroll destination of the checkout process prior to any post-purchase components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status-and-errors feedback banner is positioned at the very top of the main checkout content container, just below the Checkout heading and its separating rule and above both the left and right columns. It spans the full width of the central container (matching the combined width of the two columns) but maintains the same generous left and right margins as the rest of the page. The banner appears as a low-profile horizontal strip with 12–16px top and bottom padding, containing concise text aligned left and optionally an icon. There is roughly 24px of vertical space above and below the banner, so it feels like a distinct informational band rather than merging into other components. When visible, it is the first element encountered in the checkout flow, acting as a subtle but clear alert layer without disrupting the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the main checkout container, appearing and disappearing in place without affecting the position of the global header and always pushing the two-column content down when present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation panel replaces the active form-focused content within the main checkout container after a successful submission. It is centered within the same max-width container and generally uses a single-column layout instead of the two-column split, mirroring the simplicity of the page heading area. The panel begins just below the Checkout heading and rule, with 32–48px of whitespace above it. It spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width but keeps its inner content constrained to about 540–640px for comfortable reading. Inside, a clear confirmation title appears near the top, followed by a short success message, order summary highlights, and any next-step actions arranged vertically with 16–24px spacing between elements. The original left-column order summary may be echoed in a simplified form inside this panel, but visually it reads as one unified block in the center, surrounded by open white space. Below the panel, additional 48px or more of vertical space separates it from the optional newsletter opt-in, giving the confirmation room to stand out as the primary end state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll flow but conditionally replaces or hides the two-column checkout content once payment is completed, becoming the central focus of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter opt-in sits near the bottom of the page, directly beneath the main transactional content—either under the submit controller in the right column (pre-purchase) if shown early, or more commonly beneath the confirmation panel (post-purchase). In the post-purchase arrangement, it appears as a centered, single-column block within the same max-width container, with about 48px of whitespace separating it from the confirmation panel above. It spans roughly 50–60% of the viewport width, keeping the form compact and approachable. The heading is centered or softly left-aligned, with a short line of explanatory text followed by an email input and submit button arranged either in a single horizontal row or a vertical stack, depending on available width. Generous whitespace surrounds the component, and bottom padding ensures it doesn’t feel cramped against the page’s footer, maintaining the gallery’s calm, breathing layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static near the end of the main content flow, optionally shown post-purchase, and always centered as a softer, non-blocking call-to-action beneath the primary checkout experience.

name: About
title: About the Gallery
description: Displays dynamically loaded background information about the gallery (mission, vision, history, key programs) and allows visitors to subscribe to the gallery newsletter and navigate to Visit, Exhibitions, and Support pages.

core_features: Fetch and display About content (title, mission, vision, history, key programs) using getAboutContent when the page loads.
Segment the About information into logical sections (mission, vision, history, key programs) that can be deep-linked and auto-focused via URL parameters.
Display key programs as a list of items with summaries and context-aware navigation actions based on each program's linkTarget.
Provide dedicated inline links to Plan your visit (visit.html), Explore current exhibitions (exhibitions.html), and Support the gallery (support.html).
Allow users to subscribe to the gallery newsletter via a form that calls subscribeToNewsletter with email, topics, interests, and preferredFormat.
Handle newsletter subscription states (in-progress, success, error) and expose the resulting state via URL parameters to support sharable or returnable states.
Provide basic validation and error messaging for the newsletter form, driven by the subscribeToNewsletter response and client-side checks.
user_workflows:
View About content:
1. User navigates to about.html (optionally with URL parameters such as ?section=history or ?highlightProgramId=XYZ).
2. The page controller component calls getAboutContent.
3. On successful response, the mission, vision, history, and key programs data are passed to content components for rendering.
4. If the URL contains section, the corresponding section is scrolled into view or visually focused.
5. If the URL contains highlightProgramId, the matching key program item is visually highlighted (state only, no extra API call).

Navigate from About to Visit / Exhibitions / Support:
1. User reads the About content and sees inline navigation actions like 'Plan your visit', 'Explore current exhibitions', and 'Support the gallery'.
2. When a user clicks 'Plan your visit', the browser navigates to visit.html with optional context parameters (e.g., visit.html?from=about&section=overview).
3. When a user clicks 'Explore current exhibitions', the browser navigates to exhibitions.html with optional context parameters (e.g., exhibitions.html?from=about).
4. When a user clicks 'Support the gallery', the browser navigates to support.html with optional context parameters (e.g., support.html?from=about).
5. For key programs whose linkTarget maps to one of these pages, clicking the program’s action navigates to the respective page with its own contextual query parameters (e.g., exhibitions.html?from=about&programId=<keyProgram.id>).

Subscribe to the newsletter from the About page:
1. User scrolls to the newsletter subscription section on about.html.
2. User enters an email address and selects one or more topics and optional interests, and chooses a preferredFormat.
3. On submit, basic client-side validation runs (required email, at least one topic, preferredFormat chosen).
4. If validation passes, the newsletter-subscription-form component calls subscribeToNewsletter with the collected values.
5. While awaiting the response, the form enters a 'submitting' state (e.g., disabling inputs and submit button).
6. If subscribeToNewsletter returns success=true, the form clears or locks inputs, displays a success message, and triggers a URL update to about.html?newsletterStatus=success.
7. If subscribeToNewsletter returns success=false or the call fails, an error message is displayed and the form remains editable; the URL may be updated to about.html?newsletterStatus=error.
8. If the user later revisits about.html with newsletterStatus in the URL, the page reads this parameter and shows the corresponding feedback state without re-calling subscribeToNewsletter.
interactions: Page load: on initial load of about.html, the page controller triggers getAboutContent; during loading, a 'loading' state is managed by the controller and passed to child components.

Section deep-linking: when the URL includes ?section=<sectionName>, after content is loaded, the content component scrolls to and focuses the requested section (e.g., mission, vision, history, key_programs).

Key program click: clicking a key program’s primary action uses the program’s linkTarget value to determine the destination (e.g., visit.html, exhibitions.html, support.html) and constructs an href with context parameters such as from=about and programId=<keyProgram.id>.
Inline navigation links: clicking 'Plan your visit' navigates to visit.html with optional query parameters like from=about (no API calls).
Inline navigation links: clicking 'Explore current exhibitions' navigates to exhibitions.html with optional query parameters like from=about.
Inline navigation links: clicking 'Support the gallery' navigates to support.html with optional query parameters like from=about.

Newsletter input changes: when the user types into the email field or changes topic/interest/format controls, the newsletter-subscription-form updates its internal form state and clears any previous validation errors for that field.
Newsletter form submission: when the user submits the form, the component validates inputs and, if valid, calls subscribeToNewsletter; if not valid, it sets error messages and prevents the API call.

Newsletter response handling: on a successful subscribeToNewsletter call, the component updates its state to 'subscribed', surfaces the message from the response, and updates the URL query parameter newsletterStatus to success; on error, it sets the state to 'error', surfaces the message, and sets newsletterStatus=error.

URL-driven newsletter state: when the page loads or the URL changes, if newsletterStatus is present, the newsletter-subscription-form reads it and sets its initial state (e.g., pre-showing a success or error message) without issuing any additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page uses URL query parameters to control which sections are emphasized and how the newsletter feedback state is displayed. Supported parameters include: section=<mission|vision|history|key_programs>, which, after getAboutContent completes, causes the relevant section to be scrolled into view or given focus; highlightProgramId=<keyProgram.id>, which flags a specific key program as highlighted in the key-programs list; from=<source>, used when generating outbound navigation links (e.g., about or external_campaign) so downstream pages can adjust their own behavior; programId=<keyProgram.id>, appended to outgoing links from key programs (e.g., exhibitions.html?from=about&programId=...) but not used to trigger additional API calls on about.html; and newsletterStatus=<success|error>, which is set by the newsletter-subscription-form after submit via a URL update and is read on load to determine whether to display a persistent success or error message. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; instead, all navigable/returnable state is represented via these URL parameters, while transient UI state (loading flags, validation errors, field values) is kept in memory within page components.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading with getAboutContent, interprets URL parameters (section, highlightProgramId, newsletterStatus), and passes the resulting data and state to child components.

Receives the About content object from the controller (title, missionText, visionText, historyHtml, keyPrograms) and renders the mission, vision, and history segments. It also responds to the section URL parameter to scroll/focus a specific segment.

Displays the list of key programs from getAboutContent and provides navigation actions based on each program’s linkTarget, appending contextual URL parameters (from=about and programId). It also highlights a program if its id matches the highlightProgramId URL parameter.

Provides dedicated links within the About content to Plan your visit, Explore current exhibitions, and Support the gallery. These links construct URLs with from=about and, if relevant, section or programId based on current context.

Collects newsletter subscription details (email, topics, interests, preferredFormat), validates them, and calls subscribeToNewsletter. It manages in-progress, success, and error states and updates/reads the newsletterStatus URL parameter to maintain sharable state.
Shows global feedback messages related to newsletter subscription (success or error) based on NewsletterFormState and the newsletterStatus URL parameter, without performing any API calls itself.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a top-to-bottom, single main column centered in the viewport, matching the mockup’s hierarchy. At the top, the page-controller presents the logo and global navigation in a horizontally centered strip, followed by a thin horizontal rule. A centered page title for the About page sits beneath this, with generous whitespace above and below and another rule separating global chrome from content. The main content area is a constrained central column that adopts a two-column pattern where possible: a left column reserved for visual or structural space and a right column for primary text content. The About content, key programs list, and newsletter form stack vertically as distinct sections within this central content column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a top-to-bottom, single main column centered in the viewport, matching the mockup’s hierarchy. At the top, the page-controller presents the logo and global navigation in a horizontally centered strip, followed by a thin horizontal rule. A centered page title for the About page sits beneath this, with generous whitespace above and below and another rule separating global chrome from content. The main content area is a constrained central column that adopts a two-column pattern where possible: a left column reserved for visual or structural space and a right column for primary text content. The About content, key programs list, and newsletter form stack vertically as distinct sections within this central content column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper ~20–25% of the viewport is dedicated to branding and navigation controlled by the page-controller. Another ~10–15% is reserved for the centered About page heading and its surrounding whitespace. The remaining ~60–70% of the viewport height is occupied by the main content sections (about-content-section, key-programs-list, newsletter-subscription-form), arranged in a vertical stack. Horizontally, all content is constrained to a central column occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width with wide left/right margins. Within the main content column, where a two-column layout is used, the left column occupies about 35–40% of the content width and the right text column about 60–65%, echoing the exhibition image/text relationship.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content remains editorial and spacious. Each major section (About text, Key Programs, Newsletter) is separated by 32–48px of vertical whitespace, following the 8px spacing system. Internal padding within blocks is in the 16–24px range. Line lengths are moderate and headings have breathing room above and below. Lists and forms are kept visually light with ample line spacing, ensuring a low-density, gallery-like feel even when presenting multiple programs or form fields.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from top to bottom: first the bold logo and horizontally arranged navigation links, then the horizontal rule that anchors the top section. The centered About heading becomes the next focal point, sitting alone in space to signal a shift into the About context. Below the second rule, the eye is drawn into the main content: first to the right-hand About text column, with the left column providing a calm margin. The flow then continues down to the Key Programs list (structured in a subtle two-column row pattern) and finally to the newsletter subscription block, which forms the visual endpoint of the page. The feedback banner appears directly above the newsletter form when active, creating a clear immediate connection between user action and system response without disrupting the vertical reading rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The About page reads like a clean, editorial spread within a gallery catalogue. At the top, a generous band of whitespace frames the centered logo, with simple, evenly spaced navigation links aligned horizontally beneath it. A fine horizontal rule runs across the central content width, acting as a visual boundary before the page title. The About heading sits centered in its own field of white space, reinforcing the sense of calm focus. Another horizontal rule marks the start of the main content column, which is centered on the page and relatively narrow compared to the full viewport. Within this column, the About content and Key Programs are presented in a subtle two-column composition, with a quieter left column and a primary right text column, reminiscent of artwork-plus-caption layouts from the Exhibitions page. Each section is separated by generous vertical gaps, so individual blocks feel distinct and easy to scan. At the bottom of the main column, the newsletter subscription form spans the content width in a single-column layout with clear fields and labels, functioning as a calm, inviting call-to-action. When present, the feedback message banner appears immediately above the form, stretching across the same central width, so the entire experience maintains a consistent, museum-like restraint and rhythm from top to bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-controller defines the full-page vertical stack and central alignment. It extends across the entire viewport width but constrains all inner content to a centered container that spans approximately 60–70% of the viewport width (max-width around a typical desktop content width, e.g., 1040–1120px), leaving generous white margins on the left and right. At the very top of this container, the logo (global brand, assumed) is centered horizontally with 32–48px of padding above and below. Directly below the logo, the about-navigation-links run horizontally, also centered, with even spacing (16–24px gaps) between links. A thin horizontal rule (1px line) stretches across the width of the content container below the navigation, separated by about 16px of vertical space. Further down, the About page title sits centered within the same container, occupying a single line or two with 32–48px of whitespace above and below it, emphasizing its importance. Another horizontal rule, aligned with the container edges, is placed roughly 24px beneath the heading to mark the transition into the main About content area. All subsequent components (about-content-section, key-programs-list, newsletter-subscription-form, feedback-message-banner) are positioned within this same central content container and stacked in the normal document flow with generous vertical spacing (32–48px) between each major section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the static page shell in normal document flow, defining the centered content container and vertical stacking. It does not scroll independently or stick; all child components scroll together as a unified page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-section sits directly beneath the lower horizontal rule that follows the About heading, separated from it by roughly 32px of vertical space. It is laid out as a two-column block within the central content container. The entire block spans about 100% of the container width, but internally it is divided into a left and right column. The left column occupies roughly 35–40% of the block’s width and remains visually quiet—functioning as a breathing strip that can hold a subtle image or simply remain white space, echoing the ‘image on the left’ motif from the Exhibitions layout. The right column occupies the remaining 60–65% and holds the primary About text: stacked sections for mission, vision, history, etc. These text sections are arranged vertically in a single column with 24–32px of space between them to create clear breaks. Headings (e.g., ‘Mission’, ‘Vision’, ‘History’) are aligned flush left within the right column and separated from their paragraphs by 8–12px. The top of the text in the right column is aligned horizontally with the top of the left column space, giving the block a tidy rectangular silhouette. Internal padding around the block is about 16–24px on all sides so the content doesn’t touch the container edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying a fixed position in the vertical flow directly under the page title section. On narrower viewports, the two-column layout can stack, with the right text column collapsing beneath the left column area while maintaining vertical spacing and central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The key-programs-list sits below the about-content-section, separated from it by approximately 40px of vertical space to clearly denote a new section. It is contained within the same central content width and follows a structured, low-density list layout. At the top of this section, an optional small section heading like ‘Key Programs’ (if present within this component) would be aligned left within the content container with about 8–16px of space beneath it. Each program item is presented as a horizontally oriented row that echoes the exhibition entries: the row spans nearly the full width of the content container and is internally divided into a narrow left column (~30–35% of the row width) and a wider right column (~65–70%). In each row, the left column can hold a short label, icon placeholder, or program category name, aligned left and vertically centered, while the right column holds the program title in a bold or semi-bold style, with a brief description beneath it. Program rows are stacked vertically with 24–32px of whitespace between them, giving each listing room to stand on its own. The entire list has top and bottom padding of 16–24px so it feels like a cohesive block within the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the main document flow, appearing immediately after the about-content-section. On smaller screens, each program row’s columns can stack vertically with the left column content appearing above the right column content, maintaining generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-navigation-links are placed near the top of the page within the band controlled by the about-page-controller, directly beneath the centered logo. They form a single horizontal row of text links (e.g., Visit, Exhibitions, Support, About), centered within the main content container. Each link is set in a minimalist, uppercase or simple sans-serif style, with equal spacing of about 16–24px between link labels to keep the row airy. Vertical padding above the links is roughly 16–24px from the bottom of the logo, and about 16–24px separates the links from the horizontal rule below them. The links align visually with the central column used by the rest of the page; they do not span the full viewport edge-to-edge, preserving the centered, gallery-like composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the top-of-page chrome. It scrolls away as the user moves down the page; it is not sticky or fixed, maintaining the simple, unencumbered scroll experience seen in the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-subscription-form appears toward the bottom of the main content column, beneath the key-programs-list, separated by approximately 40–48px of vertical space to signal a transition from informational content to an action-oriented block. It is centered within the same content container and spans roughly 100% of that container’s width. Internally, the form adopts a single-column layout to preserve clarity: at the top, a short heading such as ‘Newsletter’ or ‘Stay in Touch’ is flush left, with 8–16px of space above the first form field. The input fields (e.g., name, email) are stacked vertically with 16px spacing between them and full width relative to the form area, but not touching the edges thanks to 16–24px of internal horizontal padding. A submit button is aligned left below the final field with 16–24px of vertical space above it. The entire form is framed with additional top and bottom padding (24–32px) and possibly a subtle border or rule to gently demarcate it from the white background while staying within the black-and-white aesthetic. Overall, the form appears as a calm, rectangular block, well-separated from surrounding content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the page flow, acting as the natural end point of the About content. On smaller screens, the form remains single-column but may reduce horizontal padding slightly to maintain comfortable field widths within the constrained viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback-message-banner appears in close spatial relationship to the newsletter-subscription-form to clearly communicate the result of form submission. When active, it is inserted directly above the newsletter form within the central content container, sharing the same width (100% of the container) and alignment. The banner is a low-height horizontal strip, roughly 48–64px tall, with a subtle background tint within the grayscale palette (e.g., very light gray) to differentiate it from the white page background while preserving the minimalist aesthetic. It has 12–16px of internal vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding. The message text (success or error) is aligned left inside the banner and kept to one or two short lines. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space above the banner (relative to the component above, usually key-programs-list) and 16px between the bottom of the banner and the top of the newsletter form, so it feels connected to the form yet not cramped. When not present, the surrounding components collapse together, closing the gap where the banner would appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears within the normal document flow, pushing the newsletter-subscription-form down when shown. It is not fixed or floating; it scrolls with the content. This behavior ensures that feedback is seen at the location of interaction without introducing visual noise elsewhere on the page.

name: Contact
title: Contact
