description: Provides the gallery’s contact details, a general inquiry form, and an optional inline newsletter subscription, with subject prefill support and a quick link to hours and directions.

core_features: Fetch and display gallery contact information (address, phone number, email addresses, opening hours summary, and a map embed) via getContactInfo.
Provide a general contact form that submits inquiries or feedback using submitContactForm, including name, email, subject, and message fields.
Prefill the contact form subject field from an optional URL parameter `subject` supplied by other pages (e.g., index.html or policies.html).
Link the user from the Contact page to visit.html with the appropriate URL parameter (`section=hours_location`) for detailed hours and directions.
Provide an inline newsletter subscription form that collects email, topics, interests, and preferred email format and submits them via subscribeToNewsletter.
Display success or error feedback messages for form submissions (contact form and newsletter subscription) based on API responses.
user_workflows: General inquiry workflow: 1) User navigates to contact.html (with no URL parameters). 2) Page loads and a data-fetching component calls getContactInfo with no parameters. 3) The contact info component renders the address, phone number, email addresses, opening hours summary, and map embed URL returned by getContactInfo. 4) User fills in the contact form fields for name, email, subject, and message. 5) User submits the form, triggering submitContactForm with the current field values. 6) The page receives the {success, message} response. 7) A status/notification component displays a success or error message based on the response and, if successful, may clear or disable the form fields.

Prefilled subject from another page workflow: 1) User clicks a contact link on index.html or policies.html that navigates to contact.html with a query parameter, for example contact.html?subject=Accessibility%20question. 2) On load, the Contact page reads the URL parameter `subject`. 3) The contact form component initializes its subject field with the decoded value of the `subject` parameter. 4) User reviews or edits the prefilled subject and enters name, email, and message. 5) User submits the form; the page calls submitContactForm with the possibly edited subject and the other fields. 6) The response from submitContactForm is processed and shown to the user via the status component.

Finding hours and directions workflow: 1) User navigates to contact.html and views the basic openingHoursSummary from getContactInfo. 2) User clicks a dedicated link or button labeled to view full hours and directions. 3) This click generates a navigation to visit.html with the query parameter section=hours_location (i.e., visit.html?section=hours_location). 4) The Visit page can then use the section parameter to scroll to or emphasize the hours and location section.

Newsletter subscription from Contact page workflow: 1) User scrolls to the newsletter subscription area of the Contact page. 2) User enters an email address and selects one or more topics and optional interests, and chooses a preferred email format (e.g., HTML or plain text) using the available enum values required by subscribeToNewsletter. 3) User submits the subscription form, which calls subscribeToNewsletter with the collected values. 4) The page receives a response containing {success, subscription, message}. 5) A status component shows the resulting success or error message; on success, it may show a confirmation state (e.g., disable the form or clear fields) using the returned NewsletterSubscription entity if needed for display.

Updating subject via in-page navigation workflow: 1) User is already on contact.html without a subject prefill or decides to change the context of their inquiry. 2) The page may offer internal links or tabs (e.g., for different inquiry types) that update the URL query parameter `subject` using client-side navigation (e.g., to contact.html?subject=Group%20visit%20request) without a full page reload. 3) When the URL changes, the contact form listens to URL changes and updates the subject input value accordingly (unless the user has manually overridden it). 4) User completes the form and submits, sending the updated subject to submitContactForm.
interactions: On initial page load, a data-fetching component automatically calls getContactInfo and passes the response to a contact information display component.

The contact information display component renders clickable email addresses (e.g., using mailto: links) and may render the phone number as a clickable tel: link using the dynamic values from getContactInfo.
A link or button labeled to view hours and directions triggers a navigation event that sets the browser location to visit.html?section=hours_location.
The contact form component initializes its subject field with the value of the URL parameter `subject` if present; if not, the field starts empty.
User interactions with the contact form include onChange events for name, email, subject, and message inputs; these update the form state used when calling submitContactForm.

When the contact form is submitted (onSubmit), the component validates required fields on the client side (e.g., checks that name, email, and message are not empty and email has a valid format) before triggering submitContactForm; if client validation fails, the submission is blocked and inline error messages are shown.

After submitContactForm resolves, a status component receives the {success, message} response; it displays success messages in a positive state and failure messages in an error state, and the contact form may reset or keep values depending on the success flag.

The newsletter subscription form captures user input for email, topics (multi-select), interests (multi-select or optional), and preferredFormat (single select); onChange events update its internal state.

On newsletter subscription form submission, the page calls subscribeToNewsletter with the current values; the response is handled similarly to the contact form, with a status component showing the message and, on success, possibly locking or clearing the form.
Both forms disable their submit buttons while awaiting API responses to prevent duplicate submissions and may show a loading state.

If the URL parameter `subject` changes (for example, via in-page navigation that updates the URL), the contact form listens for that change and updates the subject field initialization logic, optionally prompting the user if overwriting manually entered text.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary URL parameter used by this page is `subject` (string). On page load, the page reads window.location.search to retrieve `subject`. If `subject` is present, the contact form component uses that value as the initial value for its subject input field. If the user navigates to a different `subject` value while staying on the page (e.g., contact.html?subject=Group%20visit%20request), the form detects the URL change and updates its subject field only if the user has not manually modified it, or after user confirmation if they have. The page does not persist state in localStorage; instead, any sharable state is encoded in the URL. The actual field values for the contact form and newsletter subscription form are held in the in-memory state of their respective components until submission. Navigation from the Contact page to Visit (hours and directions) is implemented by setting the browser location to visit.html with the query parameter `section=hours_location`, ensuring that the Visit page can use this URL state to show the correct section.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading of contact details, reading URL parameters, composing the Contact page layout, and wiring together the contact info, forms, and status components.
On page initialization, calls getContactInfo with no parameters and provides the returned contact information object to the contact-info-panel component.
Displays the gallery’s address, phone number, list of email addresses, a brief opening hours summary, and an embedded map using the data retrieved via getContactInfo.
Provides a dedicated clickable element that, when activated, navigates the user from the Contact page to visit.html with section=hours_location in the query string.

Collects user inputs for a general inquiry or feedback (name, email, subject, message), prefills subject from the URL parameter if available, validates the inputs, and submits them via submitContactForm.
data_binding: URL: subject (initial value for subject field)

components.functionality: Displays success or error messages resulting from submitContactForm, updating its content and visual state based on the {success, message} response.

Provides an optional inline newsletter subscription form on the Contact page, allowing users to enter their email, choose topics and interests, select a preferred email format, and submit their subscription via subscribeToNewsletter.
Shows feedback messages for newsletter subscription attempts, indicating whether subscribeToNewsletter succeeded and optionally reflecting details of the created NewsletterSubscription.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same global, centered, single-column header pattern as the exhibitions mockup (logo, nav, divider, then a centered page title like 'Contact'). Directly beneath the title divider, the main contact content appears as a horizontally oriented block that mirrors the exhibition row: the left column holds the contact-info-panel (plus its loader) and the right column holds the contact-form and its status banner. Below this primary two-column row, a clear vertical break introduces a secondary section for the newsletter-inline-form and its status banner, which returns to a more single-column, centered layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same global, centered, single-column header pattern as the exhibitions mockup (logo, nav, divider, then a centered page title like 'Contact'). Directly beneath the title divider, the main contact content appears as a horizontally oriented block that mirrors the exhibition row: the left column holds the contact-info-panel (plus its loader) and the right column holds the contact-form and its status banner. Below this primary two-column row, a clear vertical break introduces a secondary section for the newsletter-inline-form and its status banner, which returns to a more single-column, centered layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 20–25% of the viewport remains dedicated to branding, navigation, and the 'Contact' page title with generous vertical padding. The primary contact row (info on the left, form on the right) occupies the central band of the page within a max-width centered container that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, leaving large left/right white margins. Within this row, the left contact-info column uses about 35–40% of the row width; the right form column uses about 60–65%. The newsletter section beneath spans a similar container width but is visually lighter, with the form constrained to roughly half the container width and centered. Vertical gaps of 32–48px separate major sections and 24–32px separate related components within each section.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains low content density: short line lengths, generous vertical spacing, and minimal visual noise. The contact-info panel surfaces only essential details in a compact stack. The contact form fields are stacked with comfortable spacing, avoiding dense multi-column forms except for very short paired fields if used. The newsletter form is intentionally compact, with one or two fields and a single button, surrounded by whitespace. Notification banners are slim and unobtrusive, spanning their column width but with modest height and padding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves downward: from logo and navigation to the centered 'Contact' title, then into the main contact row. Within this row, the left-hand contact-info panel is seen first as an anchor (similar to how artwork images draw the eye in the mockup), then attention flows to the right to the more interactive contact form and its status banner. After completing or scanning the form, the eye continues down to the newsletter section, which feels like an optional, secondary action. The view-hours-link is nested near the bottom of the contact-info panel, acting as a subtle secondary pathway, while inline status banners briefly interrupt the flow immediately above their respective forms without overpowering the core content.

layout.overall_description: Within a full-width white page framed by generous side margins, the gallery’s standard header (logo and navigation) sits centered near the top, followed by a clean horizontal rule and a single, bold, centered 'Contact' heading. Another fine rule beneath the heading marks the transition into the content area. The main content is housed in a centered container occupying about two-thirds of the viewport width, leaving wide white space on either side. In the first content band, a two-column layout echoes the exhibition listing pattern: on the left, a tall, narrow panel presents the gallery’s address, phone, email, and a subtle 'View hours & directions' link; on the right, a wider column contains a stacked contact form with an unobtrusive status banner immediately above it. Both columns align along a common top baseline and are separated by generous horizontal space, reinforcing the calm, gallery-like feel. Below this band, after a comfortable vertical gap and a light divider, the newsletter section appears as a centered, single-column block: a brief line of copy (if present), a slim status banner, and a compact email subscription form that sits in the middle of the page, still within the main container. The overall impression is a quiet, editorial layout where every interactive element has room to breathe, and the content flows in a clear, top-down sequence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-shell spans the full browser width and height, acting as the main structural container for the Contact page. It adopts the same overall structure as the exhibitions page: a global header region at the top (logo, navigation, and horizontal divider), followed by a centered page title ('Contact') with generous top and bottom padding (approximately 48–64px above and below), and another subtle horizontal rule. Below this rule, the shell constrains the main content into a centered column with a max-width of roughly 1040–1120px, occupying about 60–70% of the viewport width. All primary page components (contact-info-panel/loader, view-hours-link, contact-form, contact-form-status, newsletter-inline-form, newsletter-status-banner) live within this inner column, which is vertically organized with clear section breaks: first the main contact row (two-column layout), then a vertical gap of 40–48px, then the newsletter section. The shell maintains consistent left/right padding (16–24px on small screens, 64px visual margins on large screens) and vertical section spacing based on the 8px grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable page container that centers and constrains content. On narrower viewports, the inner column naturally expands to near full width while preserving padding, and the two-column content within it stacks vertically, but the shell itself remains a standard flow container without fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-loader occupies the exact footprint where the contact-info-panel will appear. It is positioned in the left column of the primary contact row, starting directly beneath the horizontal divider that follows the 'Contact' heading, aligned flush with the left edge of the main content container. It spans roughly 35–40% of the main content width and extends vertically to approximate the eventual height of the contact-info-panel (roughly 240–320px, depending on content), creating a tall, card-like block. The loader aligns at the top with the contact-form column on the right and is separated from it by a horizontal gutter of about 32–40px. When active, it visually indicates loading using a minimal skeleton or shimmer pattern but maintains a clean rectangular silhouette, reinforcing the grid structure. Once data loads, this loader collapses and is fully replaced by the contact-info-panel in the same position.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies space in normal document flow, ensuring the left column height is reserved while data loads. It does not overlap other elements; instead, it is swapped out for the contact-info-panel when loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel sits in the left column of the main contact content row, replacing the loader once data is available. It begins immediately below the content divider under the 'Contact' heading, aligned with the main content container’s left edge. The panel occupies approximately 35–40% of the container width and is vertically aligned with the top of the contact-form column on the right. Internally, the panel presents a vertical stack of items: gallery name or label at the top, followed by address lines, phone, email, and the view-hours-link near the bottom, with 8–16px vertical spacing between items. The panel has comfortable internal padding on all sides (around 16–24px) and sits in open white space, without a heavy border, matching the minimalist aesthetic. A horizontal gutter of roughly 32–40px separates this left panel from the right contact-form column. The height of the panel is moderate, roughly half to two-thirds the height of a typical laptop viewport, leaving ample whitespace below before the newsletter section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in normal flow forming the left half of the two-column row. On tablet and mobile breakpoints, it stacks above the contact-form, expanding to nearly 100% of the container width with maintained internal padding and a vertical gap (24–32px) before the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-hours-link is placed inside the contact-info-panel towards its lower portion, aligned to the left with the other text elements. It appears after the primary contact details (address, phone, email), separated by a slightly larger vertical gap (16–24px) to signal its role as an action. Visually, it may be presented as a single-line text link with an understated underline or arrow, but from a layout perspective it occupies only one text line width, sitting within the panel’s internal padding and not extending beyond the panel’s width. There is at least 16–24px of clear space below this link before the bottom of the panel, so it doesn’t feel cramped against the edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the contact-info-panel’s vertical stack. It participates in normal text flow and does not affect the panel’s column width, only its height depending on the link’s label length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form occupies the right column of the main contact row. It starts at the same vertical position as the top of the contact-info-panel, directly under the content divider, aligned to the right side of the main content container. The form’s column takes up approximately 60–65% of the container width, separated from the left column by a 32–40px gutter. The form content is arranged in a clean vertical stack: labels and inputs aligned left, full-width within this column, with 12–16px vertical spacing between fields and 24–32px between logical groups (e.g., contact details vs. message). The top edge of the form sits just below the contact-form-status banner (when present), with a slim gap of 8–12px between. The form’s overall height is substantial enough to feel like the primary interactive area, potentially extending to half or more of the viewport height, but it is surrounded by sufficient whitespace above, to the sides, and below to avoid visual crowding. The right boundary of the form aligns with the main content container’s right edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that defines the right side of the two-column layout. On smaller screens, it stacks below the contact-info-panel, expanding to nearly the full width of the content container with consistent internal spacing and maintaining the contact-form-status banner immediately above it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form-status banner is positioned directly above the contact-form within the right column of the main contact row. It shares the same column width as the form (about 60–65% of the container) and aligns precisely with the form’s left and right edges, creating a neat vertical column. The banner is relatively slim, with internal vertical padding of about 8–12px and horizontal padding matching the form’s internal alignment. When visible, it occupies a low-profile band that sits approximately 8–12px above the top of the form fields, clearly associated with the form but not dominating the layout. When hidden, the space collapses so the form moves up to sit closer to the content divider, preserving the spacious feel without a persistent empty bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in normal flow, pushing the contact-form downward when present rather than overlaying it. It does not alter column widths or the two-column relationship, only the vertical spacing above the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-inline-form is placed in its own section beneath the main contact row. After the bottom edge of the contact-info-panel and contact-form, there is a generous vertical gap of about 40–48px, optionally marked by a subtle horizontal rule to signal the transition to a secondary action area. Within the same centered main content container, the newsletter form itself is further constrained: it is centered horizontally and typically limited to roughly 40–60% of the container width, creating a compact, focused strip of content in the middle of the page. The form elements (e.g., email field and subscribe button) are arranged in a simple horizontal pair or a short vertical stack, depending on screen size, with sufficient horizontal breathing room. There is comfortable whitespace above (24–32px from any preceding section label or the newsletter-status-banner) and below (32–40px before the page footer or next section), preserving the light editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered block within the main content container. On narrow viewports, it expands to use a higher percentage of the container width (up to near full width, minus padding), with the input and button stacking vertically if necessary while maintaining central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-status-banner sits immediately above the newsletter-inline-form within the same centered section. It shares the same horizontal alignment and width constraints as the newsletter form: centered in the page and matching the form’s width (around 40–60% of the main content container), so the two elements read as a single unit. The banner is shallow, with 8–12px of vertical padding and clean left-aligned or center-aligned messaging, and a small vertical gap of 8–12px separates it from the top of the newsletter form. When not visible, the space collapses so that the form moves up, remaining close to any section label or divider above while still preserving a sense of openness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in normal flow, pushing the newsletter form downward when active. It does not fix to the viewport or overlay other components; it simply occupies a slim band of space within the newsletter section and disappears when inactive.

name: Policies & Accessibility
title: Policies & Accessibility
description: Page that displays the gallery’s privacy policy, terms of use, accessibility statement, and visitor policies, and allows visitors to subscribe to the newsletter with informed consent.

core_features: Fetch and display policy content (privacy policy, terms of use, accessibility statement, visitor policies) using getPoliciesAndAccessibilityContent.
Deep-link navigation to specific sections (privacy, terms, accessibility) via the section URL parameter.
In-page navigation between policy sections with URL state updates (section parameter) and scroll/focus behavior.
Inline newsletter subscription form that uses subscribeToNewsletter and clearly references the privacy policy on the same page.
Basic feedback pattern for newsletter subscription (success/error messaging) without storing any data locally.
user_workflows:
View a specific policy section via footer link:
  1. User clicks a footer link such as “Accessibility”, “Privacy Policy”, or “Terms of Use” on any page.
  2. The user is navigated to policies.html with a section URL parameter (for example, policies.html?section=accessibility, policies.html?section=privacy, or policies.html?section=terms).
  3. On page load, the policies-content-loader component calls getPoliciesAndAccessibilityContent.
  4. When the content is returned, the policies-url-state-manager reads the section parameter and scrolls/focuses the corresponding policy-section (accessibility, privacy, or terms) for the user.
  5. The user reads the section and can use the in-page navigation to move to other policy sections; the URL is updated accordingly.

Browse all policies from a direct URL without parameters:
  1. User opens policies.html directly (no section parameter).
  2. The policies-content-loader component calls getPoliciesAndAccessibilityContent.
  3. All four sections (privacy, terms, accessibility, visitor policies) are rendered in a default order.
  4. Since the section parameter is absent, the policies-url-state-manager applies default behavior (e.g., focus at top of the page) without auto-scrolling to a specific section.
  5. The user uses the policies-section-navigation table of contents to jump between sections; each click updates the section parameter in the URL and scrolls/focuses the relevant section.

Subscribe to the newsletter from the policies page:
  1. User reads the privacy policy and decides to subscribe to the gallery’s newsletter.
  2. User scrolls to the newsletter-inline-subscription-form located in the page (e.g., near the end of the privacy section or at the bottom of the page).
  3. User enters an email address.
  4. User selects one or more topics (e.g., monthly highlights, new exhibitions, special offers) and optionally selects interests (e.g., family activities, education programs). Topic and interest options are rendered based on configuration, not hardcoded content in this page definition.
  5. User chooses a preferred email format (HTML email or plain text).
  6. User submits the form. The component validates the inputs (e.g., required email, at least one topic, a selected preferred format) and then calls subscribeToNewsletter with the provided email, topics, interests, and preferredFormat.
  7. On success, the component displays a success message based on the returned message field and may reflect key details from subscription (e.g., which topics were subscribed to) using the returned NewsletterSubscription.
  8. On failure (success=false), the component shows the returned message as an error state and allows the user to adjust the form and resubmit, without using localStorage.

Switch between policy sections without reloading the page:
  1. User is on policies.html (with or without an initial section parameter) and the policy content has already been loaded via getPoliciesAndAccessibilityContent.
  2. User clicks on an item in the policies-section-navigation (for example, “Terms of Use”).
  3. The navigation component prevents full page reload, updates the URL’s section parameter to terms, and triggers the policies-url-state-manager.
  4. policies-url-state-manager scrolls/focuses the terms policy-section.
  5. If the user navigates back/forward using the browser controls, policies-url-state-manager listens to URL changes and re-applies the correct scroll/focus behavior using the current section parameter.

Use policies as reference during checkout or membership actions:
  1. User is on checkout.html or membership.html and clicks a footer link such as “Terms of Use” or “Privacy Policy”.
  2. User is navigated to policies.html with section set appropriately so they can read the relevant policy.
  3. After reading, the user uses the browser’s back button to return to the checkout or membership page. No additional data is stored client-side on the policies page; it only reads and reacts to the URL.
interactions:
Clicking in-page navigation links:
  - Interaction: User clicks a navigation item in policies-section-navigation (e.g., a link labeled for a specific section).
  - Result: URL is updated to include or change the section parameter; policies-url-state-manager reads this new value and scrolls/focuses the corresponding policy-section. No full page reload is required.

Loading policy content on page initialization:
  - Interaction: Page loads (either directly or via a navigation from another page).
  - Result: policies-content-loader automatically calls getPoliciesAndAccessibilityContent. While waiting, the page can display a loading state (purely functional, not marketing). When data is received, it passes the relevant HTML strings into each policy-section component.

Reading a specific policy via URL parameter:
  - Interaction: User arrives at policies.html with a section query parameter.
  - Result: After getPoliciesAndAccessibilityContent resolves, policies-url-state-manager interprets section (expected values like accessibility, privacy, or terms) and scrolls/focuses the corresponding policy-section, enabling keyboard focus for accessibility where possible.

Submitting the newsletter subscription form:
  - Interaction: User fills out newsletter-inline-subscription-form and clicks the submit button.
  - Result: onSubmit handler validates the data and, if valid, calls subscribeToNewsletter. Based on the response, the form displays either a success state (success=true) or an error state (success=false, displaying message). The component does not store any data locally beyond the current page session state.

Handling form validation feedback:
  - Interaction: User attempts to submit newsletter-inline-subscription-form with missing or invalid inputs (e.g., empty email, no topics selected, no preferredFormat).
  - Result: onSubmit handler prevents the API call, displays inline validation messages, and keeps all user-entered values intact so the user can correct them without losing input.

Using browser navigation with URL state:
  - Interaction: User navigates between sections using in-page navigation (which modifies URL parameters) and then uses the browser back/forward buttons.
  - Result: policies-url-state-manager listens for URL changes (e.g., via popstate) and re-applies the section-specific scroll/focus behavior accordingly, ensuring the UI matches the current URL without relying on localStorage.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary piece of state affecting this page’s behavior is the section URL parameter. This parameter is used exclusively for navigation and scroll/focus behavior; no persistent client-side storage is used.

State rules:
1) Initial load:
   - When the page loads, policies-content-loader immediately calls getPoliciesAndAccessibilityContent. The HTML strings returned (privacyPolicyHtml, termsOfUseHtml, accessibilityStatementHtml, visitorPoliciesHtml) are stored in in-memory page state (e.g., component state) and passed to the corresponding policy-section components.
   - In parallel, policies-url-state-manager reads the current URL. If a section parameter is present, it is normalized and stored in transient state (e.g., currentSection = 'privacy', 'terms', or 'accessibility'), but not persisted outside the page session.
   - Once content is available, policies-url-state-manager uses currentSection to scroll/focus the appropriate policy-section. If section is missing or unrecognized, the default behavior is to focus the top of the page and leave currentSection unset or set to a neutral value.

2) Responding to URL changes:
   - When the user clicks an in-page navigation link, policies-section-navigation updates the URL’s section parameter (for example, via the History API) and emits an event that section has changed.
   - policies-url-state-manager listens for section changes (either via an explicit callback or via the browser’s popstate event) and updates its internal currentSection state to match the newest value from the URL.
   - Upon detecting a new currentSection, policies-url-state-manager scrolls/focuses the associated policy-section. If the new section value does not map to a known section, the manager can choose not to change the scroll position.

3) Mapping URL parameter to sections:
   - Expected values for section are strings like 'accessibility', 'privacy', and 'terms', as defined in the page architecture. Additional internal mappings (e.g., 'visitor-policies') can be supported as needed, but the logic must not rely on any hardcoded policy content, only on identifiers.
   - policies-section-navigation uses these identifiers in its link hrefs (e.g., policies.html?section=privacy) to ensure consistent behavior across internal and external navigation.

4) Newsletter subscription state:
   - newsletter-inline-subscription-form manages only its own transient form state (email, topics, interests, preferredFormat, submission status). On submit, it calls subscribeToNewsletter and stores the returned data (success, subscription, message) in memory to drive success/error UI.
   - No values from newsletter subscriptions are stored in URL parameters or in localStorage; they remain confined to the current component’s runtime state.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches all policy-related HTML content using getPoliciesAndAccessibilityContent and exposes the results (privacyPolicyHtml, termsOfUseHtml, accessibilityStatementHtml, visitorPoliciesHtml) to child components responsible for rendering each policy section.
event_handlers: onInitFetchPolicies: Called when the component is mounted; triggers getPoliciesAndAccessibilityContent.

onPoliciesLoaded: Invoked when getPoliciesAndAccessibilityContent returns successfully; updates internal state and notifies dependent components (policy-section components and policies-url-state-manager) that content is available.
onPoliciesLoadError: Handles any errors from getPoliciesAndAccessibilityContent by setting an error state for functional error messaging (e.g., 'Content could not be loaded').

components.functionality: Provides a structured list of links to each major policy section (privacy, terms, accessibility, visitor policies). Updates the URL’s section query parameter on click and allows keyboard navigation to jump between sections.
event_handlers: onClickSectionLink: When a user clicks a navigation item, prevents full page reload, updates the URL’s section parameter to the associated section identifier, and notifies policies-url-state-manager to apply scroll/focus behavior.
onKeyboardActivateSectionLink: Similar to onClickSectionLink but triggered via keyboard events (e.g., Enter or Space) for accessibility.
onSectionsConfigChange: Handles any changes in the list or order of sections if the configuration is dynamic (e.g., if certain sections are conditionally included).

components.functionality: Renders the privacy policy section using the privacyPolicyHtml field obtained from policies-content-loader. Exposes an anchor or ID that matches the 'privacy' value of the section URL parameter for scroll/focus linking.
event_handlers: onContentUpdate: Triggered when policies-content-loader updates privacyPolicyHtml; re-renders the section content.

onSectionFocused: Called when policies-url-state-manager instructs this section to receive focus based on the URL’s section parameter; ensures the section is scrolled into view and the header or first focusable element is focused.

components.functionality: Renders the terms of use section using the termsOfUseHtml field from policies-content-loader and binds an anchor or ID that corresponds to the 'terms' section value in the URL.
event_handlers: onContentUpdate: Triggered when policies-content-loader updates termsOfUseHtml; re-renders the section content.
onSectionFocused: Called when policies-url-state-manager selects this section based on the section URL parameter; scrolls and focuses as appropriate.

components.functionality: Displays the accessibility statement using the accessibilityStatementHtml field from policies-content-loader. This is the target when section=accessibility is present in the URL.
event_handlers: onContentUpdate: Triggered when policies-content-loader updates accessibilityStatementHtml; re-renders the section content.
onSectionFocused: Invoked by policies-url-state-manager when section=accessibility; scrolls this section into view and sets focus on its header or first focusable element.

components.functionality: Renders general visitor policies (such as ticketing, refunds, membership terms summary, and on-site behavior policies) using visitorPoliciesHtml from policies-content-loader. This section can be linked via an internal identifier (for example, 'visitor-policies') that may also be used in the URL if desired.
event_handlers: onContentUpdate: Triggered when policies-content-loader updates visitorPoliciesHtml; re-renders the section content.
onSectionFocused: Called when policies-url-state-manager maps a section identifier (if configured, such as 'visitor-policies') to this section; scrolls and focuses the section.

components.functionality: Controls interpreting and applying the section URL parameter to the page state. On load and on URL changes, it determines which policy-section should be focused and orchestrates scroll/focus behavior once content is available.
event_handlers: onInitReadUrl: Runs on initial page load to read the URL’s section parameter and set an internal currentSection value.
onUrlChange: Listens for browser navigation events (e.g., popstate) and updates currentSection when the URL’s section parameter changes.

onNavigationLinkSelected: Called by policies-section-navigation when a section link is clicked; updates the URL’s section parameter (for example, via pushState or replaceState) and sets currentSection accordingly.

onPoliciesContentReady: Triggered by policies-content-loader when policy HTML content is loaded; if currentSection corresponds to a known policy-section, instructs that section’s onSectionFocused handler to scroll/focus the content.
onUnknownSectionValue: Handles cases where the section parameter does not match any known section; can clear currentSection or leave it unchanged and avoid any scroll/focus changes.

components.functionality: Allows users to subscribe to the gallery’s newsletter directly from the policies page, especially after reviewing the privacy policy. Collects email, topic preferences, optional interests, and preferred email format, and uses subscribeToNewsletter to create a NewsletterSubscription.
event_handlers: onInitForm: Initializes the form state (empty email, no topics selected, no interests selected, preferredFormat unset or defaulted) without hardcoding the available options in this specification. Options are provided at runtime by configuration or higher-level components.
onChangeEmail: Updates the form’s email field when the user types in the email input.
onToggleTopic: Toggles inclusion of a topic in the topics array when the user checks or unchecks a topic option.
onToggleInterest: Toggles inclusion of an interest in the interests array when the user checks or unchecks an interest option.
onChangePreferredFormat: Updates the preferredFormat field when the user selects an email format option.

onSubmitForm: Validates that required fields (email, at least one topic, preferredFormat) are provided and syntactically valid. If validation passes, calls subscribeToNewsletter with { email, topics, interests, preferredFormat }. Handles the returned object by:
          - Setting a success state when success=true, storing subscription details (NewsletterSubscription) and displaying the message.
          - Setting an error state when success=false, showing message and keeping the form values for correction.
onSubscriptionSuccess: Optionally disables the form or changes it into a read-only confirmation state using the returned subscription data, without persisting it beyond the current page session.
onSubscriptionError: Displays inline error information based on the message field and allows the user to retry submission.

components.functionality: Provides a simple interaction point (e.g., a link or button) near the accessibility section that directs users to an appropriate contact or feedback mechanism (such as a contact page) if they experience accessibility barriers. This component relies only on navigation and does not store or process user data.
event_handlers: onClickFeedbackLink: When clicked, navigates the user to a configured contact or feedback page URL (for example, a contact.html page) without modifying any URL parameters on the policies page itself.
onKeyboardActivateFeedbackLink: Enables users to activate the feedback link via keyboard for accessibility.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column grid, likely a 12-column underlying system with the content constrained to a fixed-width or max-width container and generous left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s centered, linear top-to-bottom structure. Above this JSON’s scope, the logo and global navigation occupy the top, followed by a horizontal rule and a single centered page title ('Policies & Accessibility'). Below another rule, the main page-specific content begins: first the in-page policies navigation centered horizontally, then the policy sections stacked vertically. Each policy section uses a two-column internal layout on larger screens (left: section label/meta, right: body text), echoing the mockup’s image/text exhibition rows. The newsletter form near the bottom adopts a similar horizontal split (left: explanatory copy, right: form fields). Supporting elements like the accessibility feedback link sit in the right column, similar to the mockup’s social icons.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s centered, linear top-to-bottom structure. Above this JSON’s scope, the logo and global navigation occupy the top, followed by a horizontal rule and a single centered page title ('Policies & Accessibility'). Below another rule, the main page-specific content begins: first the in-page policies navigation centered horizontally, then the policy sections stacked vertically. Each policy section uses a two-column internal layout on larger screens (left: section label/meta, right: body text), echoing the mockup’s image/text exhibition rows. The newsletter form near the bottom adopts a similar horizontal split (left: explanatory copy, right: form fields). Supporting elements like the accessibility feedback link sit in the right column, similar to the mockup’s social icons.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content is constrained to a central column spanning roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with wide left and right margins as white framing space. The upper 20–25% of the viewport is reserved for site-wide branding and navigation. The page title and its surrounding whitespace consume another ~10–15% of the initial viewport. The policies-specific content (in-page navigation, policy sections, newsletter form) fills the remaining area in a calm, evenly spaced vertical stack. Vertical spacing between major blocks (page title, in-page nav, each policy section, newsletter form) follows a 32–64px rhythm, large enough to feel gallery-like but consistent. Within each policy section, the left column occupies about 30–35% of the content width and the right column 65–70%, mirroring the artwork/text proportions from the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low and editorial. Policy text is constrained in line length by the central column and the two-column structure, keeping paragraphs comfortably readable. Each section emphasizes a clear hierarchy: short, bold headings in the left column; scannable subheadings and moderate-length paragraphs in the right column. Generous padding (16–24px) inside sections and the newsletter form keeps the layout from feeling text-heavy. Decorative elements are minimal: black-on-white typography, thin horizontal rules, and understated link styles, with color reserved for subtle accents (e.g., the accessibility feedback link icon, newsletter submit button).
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is strong and vertical: logo → global nav → horizontal rule → centered page title → rule → in-page policies navigation → first policy section, then down through subsequent sections. Within each policy section, the eye moves left-to-right: section label and brief meta on the left, then the more substantial body copy on the right. The accessibility feedback link in the Accessibility section mirrors the mockup’s right-aligned social icons, acting as a small secondary attractor. At the bottom, the newsletter form’s contrasting horizontal structure (copy left, form right) creates a clear visual endpoint that still feels consistent with the gallery-like rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a quiet, centered column of black text on a white background, framed by generous side margins. After the bold logo and minimalist global navigation at the top (not defined in this component list), a thin horizontal rule spans the content width, leading the eye down to a single centered heading, ‘Policies & Accessibility’. Another rule subtly marks the transition into the page-specific content. Directly beneath, a horizontal row of in-page navigation links (Privacy, Terms, Accessibility, Visitor Policies) sits centered and airy, hinting at the four main blocks below. Each policy section then appears as an elegant, two-column row within the central container: a narrower left column holding the section heading and brief metadata, and a wider right column holding the main text, with short, readable lines and substantial vertical breathing room between paragraphs. Thin horizontal rules or generous gaps separate each section, reinforcing the gallery-like pacing. The Accessibility section’s right column includes a small, right-aligned accessibility feedback link that visually echoes the mockup’s social icons on the right edge. Near the bottom, before the page ends, the newsletter subscription form appears as a slightly more framed horizontal block: explanatory copy on the left, input fields and submit button on the right, all within the same central width and spacing system. The entire layout feels measured and calm, with a clear top-down reading flow and subtle left-right micro-movements within each section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-fetcher component is positioned at the very top of the policies content area, immediately below the horizontal rule that follows the ‘Policies & Accessibility’ page title and just above the in-page navigation. Visually, when loading, it occupies the same central content column as the policy sections—about 60–70% of the viewport width, centered horizontally with generous left and right margins. Within this band, display skeleton placeholders aligned where the in-page navigation and first section titles will appear: a few thin horizontal bars (representing headings and lines of text) stacked vertically with 16–24px gaps, extending roughly two-thirds of the content width. Once content is loaded, the loader collapses to zero height and leaves no residual visual trace, allowing the in-page navigation to sit directly under the page title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; temporarily occupies vertical space in the main content column during loading, then collapses without affecting the central alignment or spacing rhythm of the other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The in-page navigation is placed directly beneath the page title area and the loader (once resolved), occupying the full central column. It sits about 32–48px below the page title’s lower rule, matching the spacious separation seen between global elements and content in the mockup. The navigation itself is a single horizontal row of links (e.g., ‘Privacy’, ‘Terms’, ‘Accessibility’, ‘Visitor Policies’), centered within the content width and spanning roughly 70% of the central column. Each link is set in a clean, uppercase or small-caps style with even horizontal spacing between them, and there is ample white space (16–24px) above and below the row. On larger screens, when the user scrolls past the page title, this navigation becomes sticky: it pins to the top of the viewport, directly below the global site nav, maintaining the same centered width and acting as a persistent local guide. On smaller screens, it can remain full-width within the content column, optionally wrapping into two lines while staying centered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the content region: remains in normal document flow beneath the page title until scrolled, then fixes to the top of the viewport aligned with the main content column, providing continuous section navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Privacy Policy section is the first major content block after the in-page navigation. It begins approximately 32–48px below the navigation, maintaining the mockup’s generous section separation. The section sits fully within the central column, with a two-column internal layout on larger screens that echoes the mockup’s image-left/text-right pattern: a left column occupying about 30–35% of the content width and a right column occupying 65–70%. The left column is aligned flush left within the content container and contains the section heading (‘Privacy Policy’) and any brief metadata (e.g., ‘Last updated’ date) stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing. The right column begins with a clear left margin from the left column and contains the body text: short to medium-length paragraphs, potentially grouped under simple subheadings. Line length is moderate, avoiding full-width spans, and vertical spacing between paragraphs is 12–16px to support readability. A comfortable bottom margin (40–56px) separates this section from the next, with either a thin, full-width horizontal rule or just whitespace at the bottom of the central column acting as a soft boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the central content column, using a responsive two-column layout that collapses to a single column (heading above body text) on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Terms of Use section is stacked directly beneath the Privacy Policy section, separated by 40–56px of vertical whitespace or a minimal horizontal rule followed by space. It aligns within the same central content column and repeats the two-column structure for strong visual consistency. The left column again takes about 30–35% of the content width and holds the section title (‘Terms of Use’) and any brief framing text, aligned to the left edge and vertically centered at the top of the section. The right column, occupying the remaining 65–70%, contains the main legal text: organized as concise paragraphs and bullet lists where appropriate, each with ample vertical spacing to avoid a dense wall of text. The overall height of this section will likely be greater due to more content, but the top and bottom padding (16–24px inside the section) and the surrounding whitespace keep it in line with the gallery-like, calm aesthetic. The bottom of this section transitions into the Accessibility section with the same generous vertical gap, maintaining a predictable rhythm down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section, mirroring the Privacy section’s two-column layout on larger screens and stacking into a single column on smaller screens while preserving central alignment and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Accessibility section sits below the Terms section with a consistent 40–56px vertical gap, continuing the central column alignment. It also uses the two-column internal layout: the left column (30–35% width) contains the heading (‘Accessibility Statement’) and any concise overview lines; the right column (65–70% width) presents the detailed statement in short paragraphs, potentially with subheadings for different types of accommodations. In the right column, aligned flush to its right edge and positioned in the upper half of the section, the accessibility feedback link is integrated as a small, right-aligned element, mimicking the mockup’s social icons placement. This link appears as a text label with a subtle icon (e.g., an outlined speech bubble or accessibility symbol) and a little vertical breathing room above and below (8–12px), separated from the main text block either by a small gap or positioned just beneath the first subheading. This creates a vertical micro-attractor on the right side without overpowering the main text. The bottom of the section maintains a 40–56px gap to the Visitor Policies section, continuing the calm scrolling experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section with a two-column layout; the accessibility feedback link inside behaves as a static, right-aligned element within the right column, repositioning beneath the heading on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Visitor Policies section follows the Accessibility section, separated by the same 40–56px of vertical whitespace or a subtle rule. It retains the central alignment and two-column structure: a left column at 30–35% width for the heading (‘Visitor Policies’) and a brief summary (e.g., ‘Guidelines for your visit’), and a right column at 65–70% width for the detailed policies (admission, photography, behavior, etc.). Text is presented as short paragraphs or bullet lists with 12–16px vertical spacing and comfortable margins to avoid visual clutter. Because this is the last policy-heavy block, the bottom margin beneath it is slightly more pronounced (48–64px) before the newsletter form appears, creating a clear transition from informational content to the engagement component. The entire section maintains internal padding (16–24px) that keeps text from feeling cramped and aligns with the editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section, maintaining the same responsive two-column behavior as the preceding sections, collapsing to a single, stacked column layout on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is non-visual and occupies no explicit space on the page, but it shapes the behavior of the in-page navigation and the policy sections. Conceptually, it is tied to the central content column, watching for scroll position relative to the top offsets of each policy section. As users click items in the in-page navigation or scroll manually, the manager updates the URL hash to reflect the active section while ensuring the scroll position aligns the section heading just below the sticky in-page navigation. It also coordinates active states in the navigation (e.g., subtle underline or bold style) based on which section’s top is closest to the viewport’s top edge, creating a seamless sense of spatial mapping between the nav and the stacked sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual footprint; logically anchored to the main content column, controlling smooth scrolling offsets and active state synchronization between the in-page navigation and each policy section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter subscription form is positioned near the bottom of the page, directly following the Visitor Policies section with a clear vertical gap of about 48–64px, acting as the final major block in the central column. It appears as a lightly framed or subtly separated card (e.g., with a fine border or a slightly darker rule at the top) spanning the same 60–70% content width and centered horizontally. Internally, it uses a horizontal two-column layout reminiscent of the mockup’s exhibition rows: the left column occupies about 40% of the card width and contains a concise heading and explanatory copy about the newsletter and consent, stacked with 8–16px spacing. The right column occupies about 60% and holds the form fields (email input, optional name, consent checkbox) and submit button, aligned vertically with 12–16px gaps. Generous internal padding (24px on all sides) ensures air around the form controls. The bottom of the form has extra white space (32–48px) before the page footer begins, allowing it to feel like a considered endpoint rather than a cramped add-on.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level card within the central content column, using a two-column layout on larger screens and collapsing into a vertically stacked layout (copy above form fields) on smaller screens while maintaining generous padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The accessibility feedback link is a compact, right-aligned action element visually anchored inside the Accessibility section’s right column. On larger screens, it sits near the top-right area of that column, horizontally aligned to the right edge of the main text block and vertically positioned just below the introductory sentence or first subheading—roughly within the upper third of the Accessibility section. It uses a simple text label like ‘Accessibility Feedback’ accompanied by a small icon, with a slightly bolder or colored treatment than body text to stand out, but smaller and lighter than section headings to remain subordinate. There is a small vertical gap (8–12px) separating it from the paragraphs above and below, so it reads as an intentional, tappable element rather than inline text. On smaller screens where the two columns stack, the link moves to sit directly under the Accessibility heading and introductory copy, still with a right alignment within the content width, preserving its role as a secondary vertical attractor similar to the mockup’s right-side social icons.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the Accessibility section; its position is tied to the right column on large screens and to the top of the stacked content on small screens, maintaining right alignment but not fixed or sticky relative to the viewport.
